Without a conversation entity, the database cannot answer 'what is queued,' 'what is in flight,' or 'what is the predecessor-successor chain of conversations in this workstream.' Adding the conversation entity does not change the session entity or violate the append-only rule for sessions — sessions continue to be after-the-fact records; the conversation entity is the work-unit representation that precedes them and outlives them.
title: Add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle, distinct from the session record

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — No workstream record.** Conversations carry a free-text workstream-name field, or just predecessor and successor links to other conversations; workstreams are inferred from grouping the data rather than modeled directly. Rejected because (i) it conflates the workstream identity with text that may drift across conversations within the same workstream; (ii) workstreams have their own state (planned, in flight, complete) that cannot be inferred from member conversations alone — a workstream is 'complete' only when its scope is satisfied, which is a fact about the workstream not about its conversations.
consequences: A new entity type to design first in the per-entity schema-design sequence (workstream is the most independent — it references nothing else in the new set). Conversations relate to workstreams via a new relationship kind (provisional name `belongs_to_workstream`, naming finalized at the workstream entity's schema-design conversation). The nested-workstream question is deferred to the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation. Retroactive question: backfill workstream records for completed workstreams already in the database (methodology entity schema-design workstream, v0.5 engagement management workstream, v0.6 styling workstream, multi-tenancy routing fix workstream, the catalog ingestion workstream) — covered by PI-022 alongside the conversation-backfill question.
context: The project uses 'workstream' as a real organizing concept — a sequence or group of conversations that share a goal. Examples: the methodology entity schema-design workstream, the v0.5 engagement-management workstream, the v0.6 styling workstream, the multi-tenancy routing fix workstream. Today the database has no idea workstreams exist; they appear only as English words in text and as Markdown filenames at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/{name}-workstream-plan.md`. Queries like 'show me every conversation in this workstream and the state of each' have no answer in the database.
decision: **Add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations.** Statuses: planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled. Fields (subject to the workstream entity's schema-design conversation, which is the first in the per-entity sequence) include identifier (Workstream NNN), name, purpose, optional parent-workstream reference if nested workstreams are admitted (deferred to the workstream schema-design conversation), and lifecycle dates. Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table. The workstream entity's schema-design conversation also resolves whether nested workstreams are supported (a workstream contained inside another).
rationale: The same single-source-of-truth principle. Workstream is a real organizing concept the project uses to scope, sequence, and declare work complete. If that is the unit of organization, it should be queryable. Without a workstream entity, queries about a workstream as a whole — its conversations, its progress, its current state, its outcome — have no answer; the consultant or developer must scan Markdown filenames and read free-text mentions in session records to assemble the picture by hand. Adding the workstream entity makes the organizing concept first-class and queryable.
title: Add a workstream entity representing a coherent line of related conversations, as a first-class record

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Per-artifact case-by-case judgment without an umbrella principle.** Each new artifact type evaluated on its own merits when introduced. Rejected because the case-by-case pattern is what produced the current gap — every artifact type in the filesystem-only category was case-by-case-judged into existence without a forcing principle. **Option (c) — Universal first-class coverage including source code and snapshots.** Rejected as over-broad; source code lives in git with its own audit mechanisms, and snapshots are views of the database, not artifacts of the workflow.
consequences: Future workstreams that introduce new artifact types include an evaluation of whether the type should be first-class in the database. The methodology guides for per-entity schema-design conversations include a question about artifact tracking. The principle is referenced in the workstream-establishing kickoff prompt and in the methodology guide the workstream produces. The principle does not retroactively force backfill of pre-existing workstreams' artifacts (PI-022 covers retroactive migration separately).
context: The first four decisions in this batch (DEC-117 through DEC-120) each introduce one or more new entity types. Stated individually they answer specific questions (how to track files, how to track conversations, how to track workstreams). Stated collectively they serve an umbrella principle: the database is supposed to be the single source of truth for project state, and today it falls short of that role wherever an operational artifact lives only on the filesystem. This decision captures the umbrella principle explicitly so future design choices can refer to it as a constraint.
decision: **The database's coverage extends to every artifact the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes that is governance-meaningful.** An artifact is governance-meaningful if it can fail to exist, exist in stale state, be superseded, or be in flight. The new entity types from DEC-117 through DEC-120 cover the ten artifact types and two work-unit concepts that fit this definition today. New artifact types introduced in future workstreams are evaluated against the same definition; if governance-meaningful, they get a first-class entity type or an addition to an existing one. The principle does not extend to (a) source code (covered by git), (b) database export snapshots which are views of the database itself (circular), (c) ephemeral working files that exist only during a single tool invocation. After this principle is in force, queries like 'what is queued up next,' 'did this payload's apply succeed,' 'what version of the Product Requirements Document was in force at session NNN,' and 'show me every conversation in this workstream' become answerable from the database without filesystem scanning or grep over Markdown.
rationale: The database was introduced explicitly to retire Markdown as the source of truth for governance content. The migration from Markdown to database for charter, status, decisions, and sessions in v0.1 was justified by exactly this argument. As the workflow has grown, new artifact types have accumulated on the filesystem side without parallel additions on the database side, and the original argument applies to each of them. Stating the principle explicitly as a decision lets it function as a constraint at future design choice points — when a new artifact type emerges in a workstream, the question 'should this be first-class in the database' has a default answer (yes, if governance-meaningful) rather than relying on each consultant or developer to re-derive the principle on the spot.
title: Single source of truth coverage extension: the new entity types close the gap between the database's stated role and its actual coverage

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer the governance workstream until after the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation and the multi-tenancy routing fix complete.** Rejected because the gap continues to compound — every conversation that runs without governance entity coverage adds to the retroactive-migration question (PI-022). **Option (c) — Run the governance workstream sequentially after the multi-tenancy routing fix slice B but before Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1.** Rejected because there is no scheduling conflict justifying the sequencing; the work is independent.
consequences: The next Claude.ai conversation opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-entity-schema-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md (the kickoff prompt committed at e1a0be4 on 05-20-26 as part of this scoping conversation's output). That conversation produces the workstream master plan, methodology guide, six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts, its own session record, and any decisions or planning items it surfaces. After it closes, the six per-entity schema-design conversations run in the order specified by the workstream plan. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B continues in parallel as a separate workstream. Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 waits on its own sub-domain amendment as a separate planning conversation, also in parallel. Governance workstream's build planning conversation sets its own target version (v0.7+ candidate, to be confirmed at build-planning time).
context: At the time of this conversation's close on 05-20-26, the project state included: the methodology workstream complete (since 05-12-26); v0.5 (engagement management) shipped (05-18-26); v0.6 (styling rollout) shipped (05-18-26); the methodology-schemas-cbm paper test complete (05-19-26) with a single blocking finding requiring a sub-domain hierarchy amendment to the domain schema, tracked as Planning Item 001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement; the multi-tenancy routing fix planning conversation complete (SES-044 on 05-19-26); slice A of the routing fix shipped (SES-045 on 05-20-26); slice B of the routing fix pending. The governance entity gap identified in this conversation has been a known omission for the entire interval. The question: open the governance workstream now, or sequence it after the in-flight items?
decision: **The governance workstream opens immediately, in parallel to the other in-flight items.** The workstream-establishing planning conversation opens as the next Claude.ai conversation after this close-out lands; it produces the workstream master plan, the methodology guide, and the six per-entity schema-design kickoff prompts. The six per-entity schema-design conversations then run in sequence (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). A build-planning conversation closes the workstream by consuming the six schema specifications. The workstream operates against the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement only; the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement is untouched.
rationale: The governance gap exists independently of the other workstreams; none of them addresses it. The Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation waits on the sub-domain hierarchy amendment regardless of governance work. The multi-tenancy routing fix is unrelated in scope (it touches engagement routing in code; the governance work touches schema design in planning). The work the governance workstream does is design (planning conversations producing schema specifications), not implementation; it does not consume the build capacity that the other workstreams need. Parallel execution does not create scheduling conflict.
title: The governance entity schema-design workstream opens immediately, in parallel to other in-flight work

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — WST, WSTRM, or WORK.** Longer forms; no semantic improvement; would set a soft precedent for three-or-more-letter prefixes that the remaining five conversations might not want to inherit. Rejected because there is no functional reason to prefer length.
consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with WS. The remaining five governance entity conversations have first refusal on their own prefix choices, with no cross-spec length norm locked at this conversation. The workstream entity's spec section 3.1 documents the soft-prefix-length posture explicitly for downstream readers.
context: The workstream entity is the first of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list and contributes to the visible-or-not pattern of cross-spec consistency on prefix length. The existing V2 collision list spans two-letter (PI), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC) prefixes; the governance schema spec guide section 6 admits 2-5 letters without locking a length. The remaining five governance entities have working prefixes spanning two to four letters (CONV, RB, WT, COP, DEP).
decision: **Adopt `WS` as the workstream identifier prefix, format `WS-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /workstreams/next-identifier.** The two-letter form is affirmed as acceptable; the cross-spec norm is NOT locked at two letters — downstream conversations may choose three or four letters where the two-letter form would be ambiguous or read awkwardly. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with WS.
rationale: WS is short, mnemonic, and easy to scan. The two-letter form matches PI's existing posture. The collision list has no conflict with WS. The spec guide's 2-5 letter range admits the choice without justification. Locking the cross-spec norm at two letters would constrain the five downstream conversations needlessly; each conversation has its own context for choosing length.
title: Workstream entity identifier prefix and format: WS, two-letter form acceptable without locking the cross-spec norm

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Direct foreign-key column `conversation_workstream_id` on conversation table.** Would give a single indexed filter for 'list conversations in a workstream' and schema-level NOT NULL enforcement of the parent relationship. Rejected because DEC-120's decision text explicitly chose references-table edge, and the query/enforcement gains do not constitute cause to override a foundation decision. Methodology workstream's mixed-mechanism pattern (entity_scopes_to_domain as references-edge, process_belongs_to_domain as foreign-key) was considered as precedent for case-by-case mechanism choice; rejected for the governance family because architectural consistency across the family outweighs per-relationship optimization.
consequences: All five remaining governance entity schema-design conversations inherit references-edge for analogous parent-child relationships unless they have specific cause to deviate. One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py; matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer rule enforcing exactly-one parent edge per conversation; tests required. The references-table generic supersedes/is_about/references kinds continue to sit alongside the new typed kinds in the universal references table.
context: DEC-120's decision text states explicitly: 'Conversations belong to a workstream via a relationship in the references table.' The kickoff prompt for SES-048 carried a working assumption that the workstream-to-conversation relationship would be a direct foreign-key column on the conversation table (conversation_workstream_id). The discrepancy needed to be resolved at the workstream entity's schema-design conversation — both because the workstream spec must describe how the relationship is modelled and because the choice sets a precedent for analogous parent-child relationships across the remaining five governance entities (close_out_payload to conversation, work_ticket to conversation, deposit_event to close_out_payload, and so on).
decision: **Adopt the references-table edge mechanism per DEC-120's binding decision text.** The workstream-to-conversation relationship is modelled as a references-table edge with new relationship kind `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (source-first per DEC-048: source = conversation, target = workstream). No `workstream_id` column on the conversation table. The access layer enforces 'every conversation has exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge'; POSTs that omit the edge or supply multiple edges return HTTP 422. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_belongs_to_workstream; ENTITY_TYPES += workstream, conversation; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair. Cross-spec precedent locked: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships across the six new entities.
rationale: DEC-120's text is binding — foundation decisions are overridden only with clear cause, and the kickoff's preference for foreign-key offered convenience rather than cause. The references-edge posture matches the governance entity family's coherent architectural shape: parent-child relationships across the six new entities all use the same mechanism, making the family easier to reason about per type and at use time. The query and schema-enforcement costs of references-edge over foreign-key are real but small at expected scale (10-20 conversations per workstream, dozens of workstreams).
title: Workstream-to-conversation relationship via references-table edge per DEC-120, with new relationship kind conversation_belongs_to_workstream

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Four statuses with no superseded.** Supersession via existing supersedes reference edge with terminal status remaining `cancelled`. Rejected because the asymmetry with conversation's superseded status (DEC-119) would invite confusion at the family level. **Option (c) — Reactivatable terminal states (e.g., complete → in_flight on resume).** Rejected because workflow-shape lifecycles work best with terminal-means-terminal discipline; resumption is more accurately modelled as a new workstream record that supersedes the prior one. **Option (d) — Introduce a new typed kind `workstream_supersedes_workstream`.** Rejected as redundant with the existing generic supersedes kind whose semantic is identical.
consequences: New vocabulary in vocab.py: WORKSTREAM_STATUSES = frozenset({planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded}); WORKSTREAM_STATUS_TRANSITIONS dict with all three terminals as empty-successor sets. Access-layer rule enforcing supersession-requires-edge; tests for both the create-with-superseded-status case and the patch-to-superseded case. Cross-spec precedent of truly terminal terminal states for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles; conversation entity inherits it for its seven-state lifecycle. Cross-spec precedent that lifecycles richer than three states are acceptable for governance entities — the spec guide already endorsed this; this decision exercises it.
context: DEC-120's decision text names four statuses: planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled. The kickoff prompt for SES-048 carried a working assumption of three statuses (planned, in_flight, complete) with cancelled and superseded flagged as open questions. DEC-117 references 'three-status lifecycle posture' as the convention inherited from the methodology workstream. Three foundation documents named different status counts; the workstream entity's schema-design conversation had to reconcile and decide the lifecycle. DEC-119's conversation entity carries seven statuses including superseded, raising the question of parity between workstream and conversation.
decision: **Adopt five statuses: `planned`, `in_flight`, `complete`, `cancelled`, `superseded`.** The default starter status is `planned`. Transitions per spec section 3.4.1: planned admits in_flight, cancelled, superseded; in_flight admits complete, cancelled, superseded; the three terminal states (complete, cancelled, superseded) admit no outgoing transitions — terminal means truly terminal. Access-layer rule: setting workstream_status to `superseded` requires an outgoing `supersedes` reference edge to another workstream record; POSTs and PATCHes that violate the rule return HTTP 422 with `supersession_requires_successor_edge`. The supersession edge reuses the existing generic `supersedes` reference kind (already valid for the (workstream, workstream) pair via _kinds_for_pair's source_type == target_type rule once workstream is in ENTITY_TYPES); no new kind is introduced for the supersession relationship. Cross-spec precedent locked: truly terminal terminal states for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles.
rationale: DEC-120's text binds cancelled (already named in the foundation decision); the kickoff's three-status framing drifted and is corrected. Adding superseded as a fifth status provides parity with DEC-119's conversation entity, where superseded is one of seven statuses; the project's operating history demonstrates supersession as a real workflow event (the methodology workstream effectively superseded an originally-planned user-interface version 0.4 kickoff). Truly terminal terminal states preserve timeline semantics — reactivation of a finished workstream blurs the timeline; a workstream that resumes is conceptually a new workstream with an inbound supersedes edge. Reusing the existing generic supersedes kind avoids a redundant typed kind for what is already a well-established cross-entity semantic.
title: Workstream lifecycle: five statuses (planned, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded) with truly terminal terminals and supersession-requires-edge rule

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — No lifecycle timestamps (methodology parity).** Rely on change_log for transition history. Rejected because workstream's workflow-shape lifecycle has queryable per-status durations as a real use case, and methodology parity does not justify ignoring the use case when the methodology lifecycle is different in kind. **Option (c) — Single `state_changed_at` timestamp.** Tracks the last transition only. Rejected as adding nothing over updated_at and change_log together. **Option (d) — Outcome summary field as required-on-complete.** Rejected because the build-planning conversation's session record carries the outcome narrative and a duplicate field invites drift.
consequences: Workstream table has 13 columns (9 substantive + 4 inherited/lifecycle). Server-side enforcement of lifecycle-timestamp population on transitions; tests required. Cross-spec precedent of per-status lifecycle timestamps; conversation entity (with seven states per DEC-119) most likely inherits this. Deposit_event probably just needs applied_at (append-only). Future-release additions (target user-interface version, outcome summary, predecessor-successor reference kinds between workstreams, nested-workstream retrofit) are documented as deferred in spec section 3.8.3.
context: Workstream needed a field inventory under minimum-viable scope: enough to host the organizing-unit concept faithfully without over-engineering. The kickoff prompt named several candidate fields (purpose, description, target user-interface version, master plan link, outcome summary) as working assumptions. The methodology entities (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) carry only base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) and rely on change_log for status-transition history; the kickoff prompt's working assumption for workstream included per-status lifecycle timestamps (planned_at, started_at, completed_at). The divergence between the methodology pattern and the kickoff's per-status pattern needed an explicit decision since it sets a cross-spec precedent for downstream timeline-shaped entities.
decision: **Adopt a nine-field schema plus three inherited base timestamps plus four per-status lifecycle timestamps.** Identity fields: workstream_identifier, workstream_name. Content fields: workstream_purpose (required, one-sentence), workstream_description (required, paragraph), workstream_notes (optional, internal). Classification fields: workstream_status. Relationship fields: NONE (parent-child relationships go through refs per DEC-124; no nesting per DEC-127). Timestamp fields: inherited workstream_created_at, workstream_updated_at, workstream_deleted_at, plus four nullable lifecycle timestamps server-set on the matching transition — workstream_started_at (planned → in_flight), workstream_completed_at (→ complete), workstream_cancelled_at (→ cancelled), workstream_superseded_at (→ superseded). No workstream_planned_at column (redundant with workstream_created_at since planned is the default starter). The three terminal-state timestamps are mutually exclusive on terminal records. No storage-level length caps on text fields. Cross-spec precedent locked: per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped governance lifecycles.
rationale: Workflow-shaped lifecycles benefit from per-status timestamps in a way propose-verify lifecycles don't. Queries like 'average time-to-complete' and 'workstreams that stalled at planned' are first-class under per-status timestamps and indirect joins through change_log under the methodology base-only pattern. Storage cost of four extra nullable columns is negligible. The methodology divergence is appropriate because methodology entities use propose-verify lifecycles (candidate, confirmed, deferred) where per-status timestamps add no value; workstream's lifecycle is different in kind. Deliberately omitted fields (target user-interface version, outcome summary) have weak justifications in the minimum-viable shape: the build-planning conversation's session record carries the outcome story; the build-planning conversation sets the target version; both can grow into the schema additively in a later release.
title: Workstream field inventory with per-status lifecycle timestamps for the workflow-shaped lifecycle

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Self-referential foreign-key column workstream_parent_id (topic.parent_topic pattern).** Rejected because the future retrofit would touch the workstream table; references-edge is a lighter retrofit. **Option (c) — References-edge with new parent-child kind in this release.** Rejected because no real need exists and adding an unused mechanism creates dead vocabulary that the build-planning conversation must aggregate without justification.
consequences: No nesting infrastructure in this release. Soft precedent for downstream governance entities considering hierarchy: prefer flat unless real need surfaces; if hierarchy is added later, prefer references-edge retrofit over self-FK column addition. Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 (sub-domain hierarchy on the methodology domain entity) is NOT pre-decided by this decision — that conversation makes its own call with this reasoning available but not binding. Future workstream-shaped initiatives expressing multi-stage organization use free text or (when needed) future predecessor/successor reference kinds rather than nesting.
context: DEC-120 explicitly deferred the nested-workstream question to 'the workstream entity's own schema-design conversation,' phrasing the deferral as 'optional parent-workstream reference if nested workstreams are admitted.' The workstream entity's spec needed to resolve whether workstream is a flat catalog or a tree, and if a tree, what mechanism (self-referential foreign key like topic.parent_topic, or references-table edge with a new parent-child kind). The choice sets a soft precedent for downstream governance entities considering hierarchy and informs the Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001 sub-domain hierarchy question on the methodology domain entity, though it does not pre-decide PI-001.
decision: **Adopt a flat catalog — no nesting in this release.** Workstream records are siblings. Multi-stage initiatives are expressed via free text in workstream_description or via future predecessor/successor reference kinds (not modelled in this release). The retrofit path, if nesting becomes a real need in a future release, is a references-edge addition with a new relationship kind (provisional name `workstream_parent_of_workstream`) plus an access-layer 'at most one parent edge per workstream' rule plus user-interface tree rendering. The retrofit deliberately uses references-edge rather than self-referential foreign-key so the workstream table is not retroactively modified.
rationale: Zero observed need from the project's operating history — all workstreams to date have been flat siblings. DEC-120's 'if nested workstreams are admitted' phrasing leans toward not-admitting. User-interface cost of hierarchical rendering is real (tree widget, expand-collapse state, breadcrumbs, drag-to-reparent); deferring until signal supports it is the minimum-viable posture. The project's actual multi-stage organizing pattern is sequential (predecessor/successor relationships between workstreams) rather than nested (parent/child trees); sequential edges would model the real pattern more accurately than nesting if added later.
title: Workstream flat catalog, no nesting in this release; retrofit path is references-edge addition not self-FK column

alternatives_considered: **Master plan as a foreign-key column workstream_master_plan_reference_book_id** — rejected per Decision 124's references-edge-over-foreign-key precedent. **Append-only posture for workstream** — rejected because workstream records can be created in error or with the wrong name; correction via edit-or-delete-and-restore is the appropriate posture. **Outcome summary as a required-on-complete field** — already rejected in DEC-126. **Tree-widget sidebar instead of flat panel** — out of scope per DEC-127 (no nesting in this release).
consequences: Vocabulary additions to aggregate at build-planning: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += workstream_planned_in_reference_book; ENTITY_TYPES += reference_book; matching _kinds_for_pair clause for (workstream, reference_book). API endpoint test coverage required across all eight endpoints. Default UI layout with the two natural additions reduces to the existing EntityCrudDialog and ReferencesSection widgets per UI version 0.3 patterns. Sixteen acceptance criteria translate into specific test cases at build-planning time. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide section 7.1) passes: all nine subsections of section 3 substantively filled, at least ten acceptance criteria, explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, and foundation decisions.
context: Routine items batched together at the close of the workstream schema-design conversation: how to model the master-plan-to-workstream linkage, what API endpoints the entity exposes, what the user-interface looks like, soft-delete versus append-only posture, and how many testable acceptance criteria the spec captures. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no deviations were warranted, but the choices set precedents and need to be recorded.
decision: **(a) Master plan linkage** via references-edge with new relationship kind `workstream_planned_in_reference_book` (source = workstream, target = reference_book; cardinality zero-or-one from workstream side; provisional naming, reference_book conversation may refine, build-planning reconciles). ENTITY_TYPES gains reference_book for vocab registration. **(b) Supersession edge** reuses the existing generic `supersedes` kind for the (workstream, workstream) pair; no new typed kind. **(c) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide section 3.5 with no deviations: GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE/restore/next-identifier; all endpoints return the {data, meta, errors} envelope. **(d) User-interface** uses the default ListDetailPanel layout per spec guide section 3.6, with two natural additions documented in the spec — conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane (showing only non-null timestamps) and supersession-requires-edge UX (the user must add the outgoing supersedes edge before committing status to superseded). **(e) Soft-delete** posture is the default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from cancelled status (administrative housekeeping versus lifecycle outcome). **(f) Acceptance criteria** capture sixteen testable statements in spec section 3.7 (gate requires ten).
rationale: Each item follows the spec guide's defaults with reasoning in the spec sections cited. No deviations warranted at this scope. The master-plan kind name workstream_planned_in_reference_book is provisional because the reference_book conversation (third in the workstream order) has not run yet; the kind name may be refined for verb-tense consistency with reference_book's other inbound kinds, and build-planning reconciles. Soft-delete fits workstream because the entity is organizing metadata not transactional fact (append-only fits decisions, sessions, deposit_events; not workstream).
title: Workstream master plan linkage via references-edge, standard API and UI defaults, soft-delete posture, sixteen acceptance criteria

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — CV.** Two letters, matches PI and WS short-prefix posture. Rejected because the abbreviation reads as 'curriculum vitae' or other domain shorthand and reduces scannability. **Option (c) — CONVO.** Five letters, no semantic improvement over CONV. Rejected because the longer form adds no value. **Option (d) — CHAT.** Four letters, changes the term of art from the project's working language. Rejected because the entity is documented throughout the project's writing as 'conversation,' and the prefix should match.
consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with CONV. The remaining four governance entity conversations (reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices, with no cross-spec length norm locked at this conversation. The conversation entity's spec section 3.1 documents the four-letter posture explicitly.
context: The conversation entity is the second of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list. DEC-123 affirmed that the cross-spec norm is not locked at two letters; each downstream conversation makes its own call within the spec guide's 2-to-5-letter range. The kickoff prompt named CONV as a working assumption with CONVO, CHAT, and CV as alternatives. The existing collision list spans two-letter (PI, WS), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC) prefixes.
decision: **Adopt `CONV` as the conversation identifier prefix, format `CONV-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /conversations/next-identifier.** The four-letter form matches the existing PROC and CRMC methodology-entity precedents and avoids the ambiguity of two-letter CV. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with CONV.
rationale: CONV is mnemonic, easy to scan, and unambiguous. The four-letter form aligns with the methodology-entity precedents (PROC for process, CRMC for crm_candidate). Two-letter CV could read as 'curriculum vitae' or other domain shorthand; the longer form removes ambiguity. CONVO and CHAT were considered (the kickoff named both as alternatives); CONVO is one letter longer for no semantic gain; CHAT changes the term of art from 'conversation' (the project's working language) to a different word.
title: Conversation entity identifier prefix and format: CONV, four-letter form matching PROC and CRMC precedents

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Foreign-key column `conversation_identifier` on the sessions table (the kickoff's working assumption).** Would give a single indexed filter for 'session for conversation X' and column-level constraint enforcement of the at-most-one-session-per-conversation rule. Rejected because the SES-048 precedent explicitly chose references-edge for analogous parent-child relationships, the kickoff itself forbade modifying the sessions table shape, and DEC-013 establishes sessions as the most delicate table in the governance system (modifying its shape requires strong justification). **Option (c) — Computed via text matching only (no formal relationship; rely on session.conversation_reference descriptive text per DEC-025).** Rejected because DEC-121's coverage extension principle requires machine-resolvable linkages between governance entities; text matching is exactly the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate.
consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_records_session); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer rule enforcing at-most-one session edge per conversation plus required-when-status-equals-complete rule; tests required for both. The sessions table shape is unmodified; no migration on sessions; no backfill column populate. The references-edge precedent locked by SES-048 is reinforced for the four remaining governance entity schema-design conversations. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs the conversation_records_session edges for historical conversations from the descriptive text in session.conversation_reference.
context: The kickoff prompt for SES-049 carried a working assumption that the conversation-to-session relationship would be a direct foreign-key column on the sessions table (conversation_identifier, back-fillable for historical records, with the kickoff calling the approach 'keep direct relationship simple'). SES-048 locked the cross-spec precedent of references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships (DEC-124). The conversation-to-session relationship is structurally parent-child — a conversation produces zero or one sessions per DEC-013. The kickoff's own 'What this conversation does NOT do' section explicitly forbids modifying the existing sessions table shape, reinforcing the precedent's applicability. The drift between the kickoff's FK working assumption and the locked precedent is exactly analogous to the SES-048 drift on the workstream-to-conversation relationship.
decision: **Adopt the references-table edge mechanism per the SES-048 cross-spec precedent (DEC-124).** The conversation-to-session relationship is modelled as a references-table edge with new relationship kind `conversation_records_session` (source-first per DEC-048: source = conversation, target = session, past-tense 'records' mirrors workstream_planned_in_reference_book's past-participle posture). No `conversation_identifier` column on the sessions table; no modification to the append-only sessions table shape per DEC-013. Cardinality: at most one outgoing edge per conversation. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status = 'complete', enforced at the access layer with the same combined-validation pattern as workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule (HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge}). Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_records_session; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, session).
rationale: The SES-048 precedent is binding — cross-spec precedents are overridden only with clear cause, and the kickoff's preference for foreign-key offered convenience rather than cause. The references-edge posture matches the governance entity family's coherent architectural shape: parent-child relationships across the six new entities all use the same mechanism. The kickoff's own 'do NOT modify sessions table shape' boundary reinforces the choice — the FK alternative would have modified the append-only sessions table per DEC-013, requiring an Alembic migration and a backfill pass to populate historical rows; the references-edge alternative does not touch the sessions table at all. The query and schema-enforcement costs of references-edge over foreign-key are real but small at expected scale (50+ existing conversations, ~10 new per workstream).
title: Conversation-to-session relationship via references-table edge per the SES-048 precedent, with new relationship kind conversation_records_session

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Collapse kickoff_drafted and ready into a single state.** Would reduce the status set from seven to six. Rejected because the operational distinction (kickoff exists but not openable vs. kickoff exists and dependencies met) is real and frequent. **Option (c) — Add a paused/hold status for in-flight conversations awaiting external input.** Rejected because no real operational signal supports it at the time of this conversation; deferred to future-release per spec section 3.8.3 parallel to workstream's deferred pause/resume posture. **Option (d) — Allow regression within the planning lifecycle.** Rejected because timeline semantics are clearer with forward-only; the cost of cancelling and re-planning a conversation when scope changes is real but small.
consequences: Conversations table has 15 columns (6 substantive + 3 inherited base + 6 per-status lifecycle). Server-side enforcement of forward-only transition map plus truly-terminal posture plus complete-requires-session-edge plus supersession-requires-edge; four access-layer rules to test. Future-release additions (estimated duration, priority, conversation kind, outcome summary, paused/hold states, cross-workstream typed kinds) are documented as deferred in spec section 3.8.3. PI-022's backfill pass populates the six lifecycle timestamp columns for historical conversation records; the backfill policy for which historical dates to use is PI-022's call.
context: DEC-119 names seven working statuses for the conversation entity: planned, kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded. The kickoff prompt for SES-049 asked whether all seven were needed in the user-interface version 0 build, specifically whether kickoff_drafted and ready could collapse into one state. The conversation also needed to decide the truly-terminal posture (SES-048 locked terminal-states-are-terminal for workstream; conversation inherits the precedent by default), the transition map within the four non-terminal states, and the access-layer rules tied to specific statuses (the conversation→session edge from Decision 1 implies a required-when rule analogous to workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule).
decision: **Adopt the seven-status set from DEC-119 in full, with the following transition map and access-layer rules.** (a) Status values: planned (default starter), kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete (terminal), cancelled (terminal), superseded (terminal). (b) Forward-only planning lifecycle: the four non-terminal states form a strict line (planned → kickoff_drafted → ready → in_flight), with no regressive transitions. cancelled and superseded reachable from any non-terminal state. (c) Truly-terminal posture inherited from workstream's DEC-125 precedent: terminal states reject all outgoing transitions including between terminal states. (d) Complete-requires-session-edge rule: setting status to complete requires an outgoing conversation_records_session edge per Decision 1 (DEC-130); HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge} on violation. (e) Supersession-requires-edge rule: inherited verbatim from workstream's DEC-125; setting status to superseded requires an outgoing supersedes edge to another conversation record. (f) Six per-status lifecycle timestamps (one per non-starter status; planned_at collapses into created_at per workstream precedent): conversation_kickoff_drafted_at, conversation_ready_at, conversation_started_at, conversation_completed_at, conversation_cancelled_at, conversation_superseded_at. Server-set on transition; three terminal timestamps mutually exclusive at the terminal level.
rationale: The kickoff_drafted-vs-ready distinction is operationally real and was demonstrated by the project's own conversation lineage at the kickoff's authoring time: workstream's kickoff was drafted and ready, conversation's kickoff was drafted but not ready (waiting on workstream to close), reference_book's kickoff was drafted but two conversations away from openable. Collapsing the two states would lose this operationally meaningful distinction or require a separate computed-readiness signal to recover it. The methodology guide explicitly admits 'Specifications with more than three statuses are expected and do not need special justification, provided the states are operationally meaningful and transitions are well-defined' (section 5). The forward-only planning lifecycle preserves timeline semantics — a conversation that 'regresses' from ready back to kickoff_drafted because an upstream dependency surfaced is conceptually a new conversation (cancel and re-plan, or delay the actual opening). The truly-terminal posture matches workstream's. The complete-requires-session-edge rule realizes Decision 1's references-edge mechanism — a complete conversation without a session edge is in an inconsistent state. The six per-status lifecycle timestamps follow DEC-126's per-status timestamps precedent extended from workstream's four columns to conversation's six (more lifecycle states means more timestamps).
title: Conversation lifecycle: seven statuses with truly terminal terminals, forward-only planning lifecycle, complete-requires-session-edge and supersession-requires-edge rules

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Foreign-key column work_ticket_id on conversation.** Rejected per the inherited references-edge precedent from DEC-124. **Option (c) — Foreign-key column conversation_id on work_ticket.** Would model the relationship from work_ticket's side. Rejected for the same precedent reason and because it would commit a column on a not-yet-designed table from this spec's side, which the work_ticket conversation could not change without breaking this spec. **Option (d) — Past-participle kind name (conversation_seeded_by_work_ticket).** Rejected because the active-verb framing matches the project's working language and the source-first convention reads more naturally with the active verb. **Option (e) — Defer the relationship entirely to the work_ticket conversation.** Rejected because the conversation entity's spec must describe its outbound relationships to be considered complete per spec guide section 2; deferring the relationship would leave the conversation entity's spec incomplete.
consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_opens_against_work_ticket); one new ENTITY_TYPES entry (work_ticket); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint and the ENTITY_TYPES enumeration. Access-layer rule enforcing at-most-one work_ticket edge per conversation plus required-when rule binding the edge to statuses past kickoff_drafted. The work_ticket schema-design conversation (third after this one) may refine the kind name; build-planning reconciles. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edges from the kickoff-prompt filenames named in historical session records' seed prompts and topics_covered text.
context: Every conversation has one kickoff prompt — captured as a work_ticket record per the workstream plan's single-use-seed-document framing. The work_ticket entity is the fourth schema-design conversation in the workstream and has not yet been designed at SES-049's writing. The conversation entity's spec must declare the relationship from conversation to work_ticket; the question is the mechanism (references-edge per the precedent, or foreign-key column on conversation as a deviation) and the kind name (which the work_ticket conversation may refine for consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds).
decision: **Adopt references-edge with tentative new kind `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`.** Source = conversation, target = work_ticket. Active verb 'opens against' mirrors how Doug refers to kickoffs in practice ('Opens against PRDs/.../schema-design-kickoff-X.md') and reads as present-tense ongoing relationship between the conversation and its kickoff. Cardinality: at most one outgoing edge per conversation; exactly one inbound edge per work_ticket (the work_ticket's single-use property is enforced from work_ticket's side). Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status is in {kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete}; optional for {planned, cancelled, superseded}. Kind name is tentative — the work_ticket schema-design conversation may refine for verb-tense consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds (for example, if work_ticket ends up with a uniform consumed-by verb pattern, this kind might shift to conversation_consumes_work_ticket); build-planning reconciles. ENTITY_TYPES gains work_ticket. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_opens_against_work_ticket; ENTITY_TYPES += work_ticket; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, work_ticket).
rationale: References-edge inherited from the SES-048 cross-spec precedent (DEC-124) — the conversation-to-work_ticket relationship is structurally parent-child (conversation outlives work_ticket; work_ticket is consumed during one conversation), and parent-child governance relationships use references-edge across the family. Active verb 'opens against' reads naturally in the project's writing context (Doug's commit messages, session records, and kickoff prompts uniformly use this phrasing). Past-participle alternatives (conversation_seeded_by_work_ticket) read more passively and put the work_ticket in the verb role; given conversation is the active organising unit, the source-active framing reads better. Required-when rule mirrors the complete-requires-session-edge rule from DEC-131 and the supersession-requires-edge rule from DEC-125 — the access layer enforces status-relationship consistency uniformly across the conversation entity. Tentative kind-name posture parallels workstream.md's tentative workstream_planned_in_reference_book commitment; build-planning has explicit reconciliation responsibility per the spec guide section 6 cross-spec consistency check.
title: Conversation-to-kickoff (work_ticket) relationship via references-table edge with tentative new kind conversation_opens_against_work_ticket

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer to signal, parallel to workstream.md's posture.** Rejected because conversation sequencing is observed today on every conversation in this workstream; the signal is already present, not pending. **Option (c) — Foreign-key column predecessor_conversation_id on conversation.** Rejected per the inherited references-edge precedent from DEC-124 and because the many-to-many cardinality (a conversation may have multiple predecessors) does not fit a single-column FK. **Option (d) — Reuse supersedes for both sequencing and supersession.** Rejected because the semantics are distinct — supersession terminates the source's lifecycle, succession does not. **Option (e) — Introduce the kind but without a cycle-prevention rule.** Rejected because circular succession (A succeeds B which succeeds A) is semantically meaningless and would create graph-traversal hazards in any query that walks the DAG; cycle prevention is required, with the specific mechanism deferred to build-planning.
consequences: One new relationship_kind to register in vocab.py (conversation_succeeds_conversation); matching Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. Access-layer cycle-prevention enforcement at insert time; tests required. The new cross-spec precedent on typed-sequencing-frequency-justification is documented in the spec's section 1 and 3.3.1 and informs the four remaining schema-design conversations: reference_book may have version-sequencing semantics that justify a typed kind, work_ticket has single-use semantics that argue against any sequencing, close_out_payload is produced once per conversation (no sequencing), deposit_event has retry semantics that may justify a typed retry-sequencing kind. Each downstream conversation applies the frequency test. PI-022's backfill pass reconstructs conversation_succeeds_conversation edges from in_flight_at_end fields of historical session records.
context: Conversations within a workstream are often sequenced. This conversation's kickoff names a predecessor (the workstream schema-design conversation, SES-048) and a successor (the reference book schema-design conversation, planned). In a strictly sequenced workstream like the governance schema-design workstream, every conversation has explicit predecessor and successor relationships. In other workstreams (like the user-interface version 0.5 engagement-management workstream), conversations may be parallel — the workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors. The chaining is captured today only in the in_flight_at_end fields of session records (free text describing what comes next); DEC-121's coverage extension principle says machine-resolvable linkages should replace free-text correspondence. The question for this conversation: does the schema introduce a typed sequencing kind in this release, or defer to signal as workstream.md did for its parallel workstream_succeeds_workstream question (section 3.8.3 of workstream.md)?
decision: **Introduce new relationship kind `conversation_succeeds_conversation` in this release.** Source = successor conversation, target = predecessor conversation, source-first per DEC-048. Cardinality many-to-many (a build-planning conversation has six predecessor schema-design conversations; a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors). No required-when rule — chaining is optional, captured when meaningfully sequenced. Cycle prevention deferred to build-planning (the working assumption is access-layer DFS at insert time; the error response is HTTP 422 with {error: circular_conversation_succession}). Diverges from workstream's defer-sequencing posture on the strength of frequency. **Establishes new cross-spec precedent: typed sequencing edges are introduced when the entity family's sequencing is structurally frequent.** The remaining four governance entity schemas (reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) inherit the precedent and may introduce parallel typed sequencing edges of their own, or defer, based on the same frequency test. Vocabulary additions: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS += conversation_succeeds_conversation; matching clause in _kinds_for_pair for (conversation, conversation) — note that the existing _kinds_for_pair rule 'if source_type == target_type: kinds.add(supersedes)' already admits supersedes for this pair; the new clause adds conversation_succeeds_conversation alongside without conflict.
rationale: Conversation sequencing is structurally part of every conversation's existence within a workstream — every conversation in a sequenced workstream has predecessors and successors. The existing fallback (filter conversations by workstream_id and status=planned, sort by planned_at) works for strict sequences but fails when a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple parallel successors. The text-matching alternative (parse in_flight_at_end fields to find named-conversation references) is exactly the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate. Workstream's defer-sequencing posture is appropriate at workstream-entity granularity because workstream sequencing is rare (most workstreams are independent of each other); the frequency argument inverts at conversation granularity. The supersedes kind and the conversation_succeeds_conversation kind capture distinct semantics — supersedes is a directed replacement (one conversation abandoned, another carries forward, source's lifecycle terminates at superseded), while conversation_succeeds_conversation is a directed continuation (both conversations exist as healthy lifecycle objects, source positioned after target in planned sequence). The two kinds never co-occur on the same source-target pair.
title: conversation_succeeds_conversation introduced for predecessor-successor chaining; new cross-spec precedent that typed sequencing edges are introduced when entity-family frequency justifies

alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with conversation_kind enum** — rejected for minimum-viable scope; kind taxonomy emerges from real-use signal. **Workstream membership optional** — rejected because DEC-120's text binds. **API surface without list-endpoint filters** — would force client-side filtering of unfiltered lists; rejected because the filters are small additions with material query-time benefit. **Master pane without Workstream column** — would require detail-pane open to see workstream membership; rejected because workstream membership is the most operationally relevant property of a conversation. **Append-only posture for conversation** — rejected because conversation records can be created with the wrong title or pointed at the wrong workstream; correction via edit is appropriate. **Sixteen acceptance criteria (matching workstream)** — would have skipped the two additional rules-specific criteria; rejected because the workstream-membership-required rule and the list-endpoint filters are conversation-specific and deserve test coverage.
consequences: Conversations table has 15 columns (6 substantive + 3 base + 6 lifecycle per DEC-131). Two new query-string filter parameters on the list endpoint; test coverage required for both independently and composed. Master-pane Workstream column requires a small extension to ListDetailPanel's column-rendering (the column derives its value from a references-edge target rather than a direct column read); the build-planning conversation specifies the access-layer helper. Soft-delete is the default UI version 0.3 pattern with no code changes required. Eighteen acceptance criteria translate into specific test cases at build-planning time. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide section 7.1) passes: all nine subsections of section 3 substantively filled, eighteen acceptance criteria (gate requires ten), explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md (the just-completed predecessor specification), foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and SES-048's six decisions DEC-123..128 as inherited precedents.
context: Routine items batched together at the close of the conversation schema-design conversation: the field inventory beyond identifier/status/timestamps, the deliberately-excluded fields, the workstream-membership cardinality rule, the API surface, the master pane and detail pane layouts, soft-delete versus append-only posture, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no significant deviations were warranted, but two natural additions warrant explicit decision recording (the list-endpoint filters for operational queries and the master-pane Workstream column for at-a-glance workstream membership).
decision: **(a) Field inventory** is six content/classification fields (conversation_identifier, conversation_title, conversation_purpose, conversation_description, conversation_notes, conversation_status) plus nine timestamps (three inherited base — created_at, updated_at, deleted_at; six per-status lifecycle per DEC-131 — kickoff_drafted_at, ready_at, started_at, completed_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at). Intended-deliverable description is folded into conversation_description rather than carried as a separate column. Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope: estimated_duration, priority, conversation_kind, outcome_summary — each is documented as future-release in spec section 3.8.3. **(b) Workstream membership** is mandatory at the access layer — every conversation has exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge; POSTs that omit the edge return HTTP 422 with {error: missing_workstream_membership_edge}; PATCH/PUT that removes the edge without replacing it returns the parallel 422. **(c) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide section 3.5 plus two new query-string filters on the list endpoint: workstream_identifier filters by workstream membership; status filters by status value. The two filters compose. **(d) Master pane** has five columns (vs workstream's four), adding Workstream as the third column showing the workstream_identifier of the membership edge's target. Workstream-selector and status-selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive the two new query-string filters. Default sort by Identifier ascending. **(e) Detail pane** uses the default ListDetailPanel layout per spec guide section 3.6 with conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering on the detail pane (showing only non-null timestamps) and complete-requires-session-edge / supersession-requires-edge UX (the user must add the matching outgoing edge before committing the status change). **(f) Soft-delete** posture is the default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from cancelled status (administrative housekeeping versus lifecycle outcome). **(g) Acceptance criteria** capture eighteen testable statements in spec section 3.7 (gate requires ten; workstream produced sixteen; two additional criteria added for the workstream-membership-required rule and the new list-endpoint filters).
rationale: Field inventory follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; deferred fields are listed in spec section 3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. Workstream-membership-mandatory realizes DEC-120's binding text. List-endpoint filters are operational additions: 'what conversations are in workstream X' and 'what conversations are currently in_flight' are routine consultant questions, materially friction-reducing if the API answers directly rather than the client filtering an unfiltered list of 50+ conversations. Master-pane Workstream column is the at-a-glance complement — without it, the operator must open each conversation's detail to see its workstream. Soft-delete fits conversation because the entity is organizing metadata not transactional fact (append-only fits decisions, sessions, deposit_events; not conversation). Eighteen acceptance criteria fully satisfy the spec guide's per-spec completeness gate (section 7.1) and the cross-spec consistency check (section 7.2).
title: Conversation field inventory, list-endpoint filters, master-pane Workstream column, soft-delete posture, eighteen acceptance criteria

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — REFB.** Four letters. Rejected because it adds two characters with no value; the collision concern that motivated the longer form turned out not to exist. **Option (c) — REFBOOK.** Seven letters. Rejected for the same reason at twice the cost. **Option (d) — RBK.** Three letters. Rejected because the K is silent and reads as a typo rather than a meaningful character; RB is the natural abbreviation. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference RB-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with RB. The kickoff's open methodology question 2 (reference-book identifier collision risk) is resolved in favour of the two-letter form. The remaining three governance entity conversations (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes WT, COP, DEP do not collide with RB but each conversation makes its own call. The reference_book table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^RB-\d{3}$`. The GET /reference-books/next-identifier helper returns the next available RB-NNN value.
context: The reference_book entity is the third of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list. The schema-spec methodology guide's section 8 named the working assumption RB and flagged 'Reference-book identifier collision risk: RB is short and may collide visually with future prefixes' as an open methodology question for this conversation to resolve. The existing collision list at this spec's writing spans two-letter (PI, WS), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC, CONV) prefixes.
decision: **Adopt `RB` as the reference book identifier prefix, format `RB-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /reference-books/next-identifier.** The two-letter form matches the existing WS and PI short-prefix posture established by DEC-123 (which affirmed two-letter form is acceptable). The collision-risk concern from the spec guide's section 8 is resolved by checking the remaining-three governance entities' working prefixes — WT (work_ticket), COP (close_out_payload), DEP (deposit_event) — none of which begins with R or shares visual shape with RB. The existing REF prefix (references entity) is three letters and structurally distinct; the two-letter RB versus three-letter REF does not introduce visual ambiguity. The longer-form alternatives (REFB, REFBOOK, RBK) add no value beyond length. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with RB.
rationale: RB is mnemonic, short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with the precedent set by DEC-123 (workstream chose two-letter WS) and DEC-129 (conversation chose four-letter CONV per its own judgment), confirming the spec guide's section 8 working assumption that each downstream conversation makes its own length call within the 2-to-5-letter range. The collision-risk concern named in the spec guide's section 8 turns out to be a false alarm: the remaining three governance entities have working prefixes that do not collide with RB, and the REF / RB distinction reads cleanly because of the visual-shape difference between two and three uppercase letters. The longer alternatives offered no semantic improvement and would have made every reference book identifier two or three characters longer for no payoff.
title: reference_book entity identifier prefix and format: RB-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with REF or remaining-three working prefixes

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Versioned-replace modeled on charter and status.** Rejected because the singleton-with-payload pattern bundles content storage into the version row; for reference books the content lives in git/files, and bundling would create a second source of truth that could drift. **Option (c) — Embedded version array on the parent record.** Rejected because array manipulation in SQL is inefficient and the version-at query would require array scanning rather than indexed lookup. **Option (d) — Content-addressed snapshots.** Rejected as premature complexity; the project has no fork semantics need today, and the schema admits a future retrofit additively. **Option (e) — Link to git commits with no in-database versioning.** Rejected because every version query would require git subprocess calls and the V2 database would not be the source of truth for governance state, contradicting DEC-121.
consequences: Two database tables: `reference_books` for the parent records plus `reference_book_versions` for the child rows. ON DELETE CASCADE foreign key from child to parent so a hard-delete of the parent cascade-removes versions; soft-delete on the parent does not affect children (their rows persist, supporting restore-recovery). Two denormalized pointer columns on the parent — reference_book_current_version_label and reference_book_current_version_date — maintained by access-layer recompute. Three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /versions, POST /versions, GET /version-at?as_of=...) realizing the operational queries. The (reference_book_id, version_label) unique constraint admits two reference books each having their own v1.0 row. No DELETE on individual version rows in this release; versions are append-only in practice. The model establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the remaining three schemas to consider; work_ticket is single-use (no versioning), close_out_payload is single-shot (no versioning expected), deposit_event is append-only (no versioning).
context: The kickoff prompt named versioning as the principal design question for the reference_book entity. Reference book files accumulate versions over their lifetimes — the methodology workstream master plan has v1.0 (05-11-26) and v1.1 (05-12-26); the methodology guide has v1.0 and v1.1; the multi-engagement architecture document has multiple versions. The schema needs to represent that version history in a way that supports the operational query 'what version was in force at the time of session NNN' (in-force-at-time-T semantics). The kickoff named four candidate models: (a) versioned-replace modeled on charter and status, where the reference_book record IS the current version and previous versions live in a parallel reference_book_version table; (b) embedded version array, where a reference_book record holds its current state plus a JSON array of version history; (c) content-addressed snapshots, where each version is a separate record with a content hash; (d) link to git commits, where no in-database versioning is done and git provides the history.
decision: **Adopt the parent record plus child versions table versioning model.** A reference_book record carries the parent metadata (identifier, title, description, kind, status, file path) plus two denormalized current-version pointer fields (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date). A sibling table reference_book_versions carries one row per known version of a reference book, addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label) unique. Each version row carries version_label, version_date, optional version_summary, plus base timestamps. The parent's denormalized pointer fields stay in sync with the child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write — the row with the highest version_date wins as 'current'. The in-force-at-time-T query is realized by GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... returning the row with the highest version_date ≤ as_of. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern, distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern.
rationale: The parent-plus-child model is the lightest shape that captures version history faithfully for a documentary entity. The charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern (Decision 29) embeds the full payload in each version row, which is appropriate when the database is the source of truth for content. For reference books, the content lives in git/files — the file path string and the project's existing git history together carry the content; the database carries only version metadata (label, date, summary). The embedded JSON array alternative would require array manipulation for every version operation and would not support the version-at query efficiently. Content-addressed snapshots add complexity (content hashing, fork semantics) for benefits that real-use signal does not yet justify. Link-to-git-commits would push every query through git subprocess calls — heavier than the database, and disagrees with DEC-121's single-source-of-truth principle for governance state. The parent-plus-child model supports the version-at query directly with a single SQL clause and stays additively retrofittable (a future release can add a version_payload JSON or BLOB column on the child table if FTS or full-content queries become a real need).
title: reference_book versioning model: parent record plus child versions table, distinct from charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Follow DEC-126 verbatim with three per-status timestamp columns (archived_at, superseded_at).** Rejected because per-state duration is not an operationally meaningful query for documentary entities; the columns would consume storage with no value. **Option (c) — Use append-only posture instead of soft-delete-with-restore.** Rejected because reference book records can legitimately be created with errors (wrong title, wrong file path, wrong kind) and edit-and-correct is the appropriate flow; append-only fits decisions, sessions, and deposit_events where the rows are transactional fact, not metadata. **Option (d) — Add only one per-state timestamp (terminal_at).** Rejected as inconsistent — either follow DEC-126 fully or deviate to base-timestamps-only with a clean principle. The mixed posture would be harder to reason about.
consequences: Reference book records have eleven columns total (seven content/classification fields + two denormalized version pointers + three inherited base timestamps). Server-side enforcement of truly-terminal posture plus supersession-requires-edge rule; access-layer rules to test. New cross-spec precedent established for the remaining three governance specs — work_ticket inherits per-status timestamps (workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle), close_out_payload's choice depends on whether the conversation lands a workflow or append-only model, deposit_event almost certainly uses base timestamps plus one applied_at (append-only fact). PI-022's backfill pass does not need to reconstruct historical per-status timestamps for reference book records (none exist); the backfill records the bare metadata and the existing file's Revision Control table provides the version_date values for the child versions table.
context: The schema-spec methodology guide's DEC-126 precedent established that workflow-shaped governance lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps (e.g., workstream's workstream_started_at, workstream_completed_at; conversation's six per-status columns per DEC-131). Reference book's lifecycle is structurally different from workflow: its status states (active, archived, superseded) capture documentary outcomes rather than workflow progress. The conversation surfaced the question: does reference_book carry per-status lifecycle timestamps following the DEC-126 precedent, or does it deviate, and if so on what principle?
decision: **Adopt a three-status documentary lifecycle (active → archived / superseded) with base timestamps only (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at — no per-status lifecycle timestamps).** The lifecycle inherits two access-layer rules from workstream and conversation: (a) truly-terminal posture — both terminal statuses (archived, superseded) reject all outgoing transitions including between terminal states; (b) supersession-requires-edge rule — setting status to superseded requires an outgoing references-table edge of kind `supersedes` to another reference_book record, returning HTTP 422 {error: supersession_requires_successor_edge} on violation. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: **documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only**, deviating explicitly from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts: work_ticket has a workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle and inherits per-status timestamps; close_out_payload is likely workflow-shaped (drafted → applied) or append-only after apply; deposit_event is append-only with one timestamp. None of the three is documentary-shaped.
rationale: Reference book's status states do not carry per-state duration semantics worth querying. 'How long was the methodology guide in active status before being archived' is not an operationally meaningful question — what matters is when the document existed (already captured by created_at) and when its role ended (already captured by the archive/supersede transition's updated_at, with full before-and-after detail in the change_log table). Status transitions are also infrequent — a typical reference book moves from active to archived or superseded at most a few times in its lifetime, vs. a workstream or conversation which moves through multiple non-terminal states routinely. Adding six per-status timestamp columns would consume storage with no query-time benefit. The deviation from DEC-126 needs to be principled, not ad-hoc; the principle is documentary-vs-workflow, which is a clean structural distinction and a precedent the remaining three specs can apply explicitly. The truly-terminal posture and supersession-requires-edge rule are inherited because they apply regardless of workflow-vs-documentary — both express integrity constraints on status-relationship consistency.
title: reference_book documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only; new cross-spec precedent for documentary-vs-workflow distinction

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Open-string kind field.** Rejected because it admits typos and label drift, degrading filterability and master-pane scannability. **Option (c) — Closed enum with only DEC-117's seven values.** Rejected because three legitimate kinds beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) are observed in the project's history; using `other` for them would degrade filtering. **Option (d) — Closed enum with twenty-plus values speculating future kinds.** Rejected as YAGNI; admit kinds as real-use signal surfaces them. **Option (e) — Per-kind tables (one table per kind, polymorphic association).** Rejected as premature schema specialization; the kinds share enough metadata (title, description, file_path, status) that one table with a discriminator is correct.
consequences: Reference_book table's kind column has a CHECK constraint enumerating the eleven values, plus a matching domain-validated set in the access layer. The master-pane Kind column renders the enum values as friendly labels (e.g., 'Product Requirements Document' for `product_requirements_document`) per the UI rendering note in spec §3.6.2. List endpoint supports `?kind=<value>` filter for at-a-glance subset queries. Adding a twelfth kind in a future release is a one-line vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on the CHECK constraint; the `other` sentinel covers the gap until then. PI-022's backfill pass assigns one of the eleven values to each historical reference book record; ambiguous cases (a document that legitimately fits multiple kinds) pick the dominant kind and use `other` only as a last resort per the spec §3.8.2 backfill guidance.
context: DEC-117 named seven reference book artifact types as the family-1 scope: workstream master plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, and apply script. The conversation surfaced the question of whether reference_book_kind should be a closed enum (with future additions requiring migration) or an open string, and whether the enum should cover only DEC-117's seven or extend to additional types the project's operating history surfaces.
