The project's history at this conversation's writing includes documents that legitimately fit reference-book semantics but were not named in DEC-117's seven: architecture documents (multi-engagement-architecture.md), conduct framework documents (PRDs/process/conduct/*.md), and investigation reports (multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md, methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-findings.md).
decision: **Adopt `reference_book_kind` as a closed eleven-value enum**: product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, architecture_document, schema_specification, conduct_framework, investigation_report, apply_script, session_startup_document, other. The first seven values listed above plus apply_script and session_startup_document map directly to DEC-117's seven named artifact types. The three additions beyond DEC-117 — `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report` — capture documentary types observed in the project's operating history that legitimately fit family-1 semantics. The `other` value is the sentinel for documents that do not fit the named categories; the intent is that real use will surface additional kinds, added by extending the enum in a future migration. The enum is closed because the spec wants validation to catch misclassification at insert time; the open-string alternative would admit typos and inconsistent labels.
rationale: Closing the enum lets the access layer reject typos and inconsistent labels at insert time, which protects the master-pane Kind column's scan-ability (no 'product requirements document' vs 'product-requirements-document' vs 'PRD' confusion). Eleven values is small enough to enumerate in a combo box without scrolling. The three additions beyond DEC-117 are real categories observed today; deferring them would force consultants to use the `other` sentinel for documents that have a clear category, which would degrade master-pane filtering. The `other` sentinel exists as an escape hatch for unforeseen kinds and as a signal-collection mechanism — if `other` rows accumulate, the project knows a new kind is needed. Open-string alternatives were rejected because they admit drift; the open-pattern-and-validate alternative (regex-validated kind strings) was rejected because the validation rules would be no easier to maintain than an enum migration.
title: reference_book_kind closed eleven-value enum covering DEC-117's seven artifact types plus three observed additions plus an other sentinel

alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with reference_book_author column** — rejected for minimum-viable scope; sole-author working pattern; one migration away. **Field inventory with reference_book_engagement_scope column** — rejected because per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit. **File-path validation including filesystem-existence check** — rejected because it would brittle-couple records to the current commit; a future build prompt may add a separate 'find broken pointers' maintenance query. **Title uniqueness globally (across engagements)** — rejected because logical documents legitimately exist in multiple engagements (the methodology guide as a reference book record in the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement and in the CBM client engagement); per-engagement uniqueness matches the isolation principle. **Append-only posture instead of soft-delete** — rejected because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; correction via edit is appropriate.
consequences: Reference_books table has eleven columns (seven content/classification + two denormalized version pointers + three base timestamps). File-path validation runs at the access layer on POST/PUT/PATCH. Title-uniqueness check runs at the access layer with case-insensitive comparison scoped to the engagement (the per-engagement database boundary makes this trivially correct). Soft-delete on the parent leaves child version rows in place; restore reactivates the parent and the children are still there. PI-022's backfill pass populates the seven content/classification fields for historical reference book records; the backfill policy on which historical documents become reference_book records is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.
context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the field inventory beyond identifier, status, and timestamps; the deliberately-excluded fields; the file-path semantics; the engagement-scoping question; and soft-delete versus append-only posture. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no significant deviations were warranted, but the per-engagement-isolation question and the file-path validation rules warrant explicit decision recording because they shape access-layer behavior.
decision: **(a) Field inventory** is seven content/classification fields (reference_book_identifier, reference_book_title, reference_book_description, reference_book_notes, reference_book_kind, reference_book_status, reference_book_file_path) plus two denormalized version-pointer columns (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date) plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). No per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-137. Notes is a consultant scratchpad — internal, not part of the user-facing summary. **(b) Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope**: reference_book_author, reference_book_owner (Doug is sole author/owner today; one migration away if multi-author engagements emerge), reference_book_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit per DEC-115/DEC-116). **(c) File-path semantics**: repo-relative path string with validation rules — non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix (https://, file://). Path is not validated for existence at write time (file may be authored separately and the record updated to point at it); a future build prompt may add a 'check path resolves' verification. The path is resolved at use time against the consuming repository's root — typically the crmbuilder repo for dogfood records and the client repo for engagement-specific records. **(d) Title uniqueness**: case-insensitive unique within the engagement. **(e) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `archived` status (a lifecycle outcome) and `superseded` status (a successor-replaced outcome). Soft-deleting the parent does not affect child version rows; their rows persist for restore-recovery. **(f) Storage-level length caps**: none on text fields, matching workstream and conversation precedents.
rationale: Field inventory follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. Author/owner omitted because Doug is the project's sole author and adding the columns prematurely would require a backfill policy without operational value. Engagement-scoping flag omitted because V2's per-engagement isolation (DEC-115/DEC-116) makes the scoping implicit — the same logical document (e.g., the methodology guide) may exist as a reference book record in each engagement that cites it. File-path validation rules prevent common errors (absolute paths breaking on a different operator's machine, `..` segments allowing path traversal, scheme prefixes that aren't filesystem paths) without committing to filesystem-existence validation that would brittle-couple the database to the current commit. Title uniqueness scoped to the engagement matches the per-engagement isolation principle. Soft-delete fits because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; create-with-error and correct-via-edit is the appropriate flow.
title: reference_book field inventory, repo-relative file path semantics, no engagement-scoping flag, soft-delete posture

alternatives_considered: **API surface without version-management sub-endpoints (everything via PATCH on the parent)** — rejected because the version-management operations have distinct semantics (append a child row, recompute denormalized pointer) and forcing them through the parent's PATCH would conflate the parent's lifecycle with the child's. **Master pane without Kind column** — rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful filter for the reference book catalog and forcing detail-pane open to see kind is high-friction. **Detail pane with version-history as a separate dialog** — rejected because the version list is the entity's distinctive shape; making it a subordinate panel keeps it visible alongside the rest of the metadata. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum)** — would have skipped the version-management-specific criteria; rejected because the version round-trip and in-force-at-time-T query are the entity's distinctive behaviors and deserve dedicated criteria.
consequences: Eleven REST endpoints (eight parent + three child) to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The version-management sub-endpoints route through the access layer's recompute logic for the denormalized current-version pointer on the parent. Master-pane Kind column requires a small extension to ListDetailPanel's column-rendering (the column has a friendly-label mapping for the eleven enum values). The inline version-history section in the detail pane is a new UI pattern relative to workstream and conversation but follows naturally from this entity's versioning model — work_ticket and the remaining entities will not need it. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria (gate requires ten), explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md (the predecessor specifications), foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the twelve prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..134 from SES-048 and SES-049 as inherited precedents.
context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the version-management sub-endpoints realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the detail-pane version-history section, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. The version-management endpoints and the version-history UI section are natural additions following from DEC-136's parent-plus-child versioning model; the Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). **(b) Three version-management sub-endpoints**: GET /reference-books/{id}/versions (list versions in version_date descending), POST /reference-books/{id}/versions (record a new version), GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... (return version in force at instant). No DELETE on individual version rows in this release (versions are append-only in practice; the build-planning conversation may add a DELETE if real need surfaces). **(c) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission for the parent record; version rows do not have prefixed identifiers (addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label)). **(d) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Version (denormalized from current-version pointer), Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. Kind selector and status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters. **(e) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section), kind combo, file_path, status combo, version-history section (subordinate panel with Add Version affordance, current version visually distinguished), ReferencesSection. **(f) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. The version-history section is hidden in create mode (one or more versions may be supplied via a `versions` array in the request body) and fully interactive in edit mode. **(g) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; workstream produced sixteen; this spec follows workstream's count, focused on schema migration, validation rules, lifecycle enforcement, version round-trip, in-force-at-time-T query, access-layer signatures, REST endpoint behavior, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore, sidebar position, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against the governance-schema-workstream-plan reference book).
rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. The three version-management sub-endpoints are the smallest set that realizes the operational queries named in the kickoff (list versions, record a new version, query in-force-at). The Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog — consultants ask 'what's the PRD for v0.6' or 'what's the methodology guide for governance entity schema-design.' The inline version-history section is the entity's distinctive UI shape; making it a subordinate panel in the detail pane (rather than a separate dialog) keeps the version list visible alongside the rest of the record's metadata. Sixteen acceptance criteria match workstream's count and fully satisfy the spec guide's per-spec completeness gate (§7.1 requires ten); each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
title: reference_book API surface plus three version-management sub-endpoints plus default UI with Kind column and inline version-history section plus sixteen acceptance criteria

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — WKT.** Three letters. Rejected because it adds one character with no value; the collision check did not surface a need for additional disambiguation. **Option (c) — TIX.** Three letters with ticket-tracking-system connotations. Rejected because the connotations mischaracterize the entity (work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues). **Option (d) — TKT.** Three letters, similar to TIX. Rejected for the same connotation reason and additionally because TKT reads as a phonetic abbreviation rather than a meaningful initialism. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference WT-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with WT. The kickoff's working-assumption prefix is confirmed. The remaining two governance entity conversations (close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes COP and DEP do not collide with WT but each conversation makes its own call. The work_ticket table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^WT-\d{3}$`. The GET /work-tickets/next-identifier helper returns the next available WT-NNN value.
context: The work_ticket entity is the fourth of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix is set against the prefix-collision list extended by DEC-123 (WS), DEC-129 (CONV), and DEC-135 (RB). The kickoff's working assumption was WT; alternatives named were WKT, TIX, TKT. The schema-spec methodology guide section 6 admits 2 to 5 letters; DEC-123 affirmed the two-letter form is acceptable; DEC-135 made the same call for RB. The collision-check requires examining (a) all existing prefixes in the V2 prefix set, (b) the two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP for close_out_payload, DEP for deposit_event) named in the workstream master plan, and (c) the visual-shape distinguishability of any two-letter candidate against neighboring two-letter prefixes.
decision: **Adopt `WT` as the work_ticket identifier prefix, format `WT-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier.** The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123 (WS) and DEC-135 (RB). The collision check confirms: no existing prefix begins with W other than WS (workstream), and the two-letter WT versus two-letter WS differs in the second character with visually-distinct shapes — W-T versus W-S read unambiguously side by side. The two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP, DEP) do not begin with W. The longer-form alternatives WKT, TIX, TKT add no value beyond length and TIX/TKT carry connotations from ticket-tracking systems (Jira, Trello, GitHub Issues) that the project deliberately does not invoke — 'work ticket' in this project is a single-use seed document, not an issue-tracking artifact.
rationale: WT is mnemonic ('work ticket' → WT), short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123's affirmation that two-letter prefixes are acceptable and matches DEC-135's RB call. The W-T versus W-S second-character difference produces visually distinct shapes (T has a horizontal top stroke; S is curved) and reads cleanly in mixed contexts. The longer-form alternatives offer no semantic improvement and would have made every work_ticket identifier two characters longer for no payoff. TIX and TKT additionally carry external connotations that mischaracterize the entity — the project's work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues with assignees and triage states.
title: work_ticket entity identifier prefix and format: WT-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with WS or remaining-two working prefixes

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — bright line by lifecycle (work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions).** Rejected because it conflates the versioning model with the family definition; a work_ticket's lifecycle doesn't end at consumption (the record persists with status `consumed`), and not all reference_books accumulate versions in practice. **Option (c) — re-categorization (move records between tables if the use pattern changes).** Rejected because moving records between tables forces a classification-history concern (which table did this live in last week? what queries return it?) that adds operational complexity without payoff; parallel coexistence achieves the same effect more cleanly. **Option (d) — single-table polymorphism with a discriminator column.** Rejected as premature schema unification; the two entities have meaningfully different field inventories (work_ticket has consumed-requires-edge and single-use rules; reference_book has version-pointer columns and a child versions table) and lifecycle semantics; collapsing them would obscure both. **Option (e) — mutual exclusivity at the file_path level (a file_path may exist in at most one of the two tables).** Rejected because parallel coexistence is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views; forcing mutual exclusivity would make the kickoff's natural archival-plus-authoritative pattern unrepresentable.
consequences: Schema enforces per-table uniqueness on file_path (work_ticket_file_path unique within work_tickets; reference_book_file_path unique within reference_books) but does not enforce cross-table uniqueness. PI-022's backfill pass classifies each historical workflow file by intent at creation; ambiguous cases default to work_ticket (the more conservative classification — a work_ticket can be supplemented by a parallel reference_book record later, but a reference_book record carries versioning machinery that may not fit single-use seeds). The methodology workstream master plan example (ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md) is backfilled as a work_ticket; if successor citation patterns warrant a parallel reference_book record, it is authored as a separate operation. Section 2 of the spec carries the boundary-discipline narrative in full; section 3.8.2 captures the backfill-policy details for PI-022 to resolve. The build-planning conversation may add a cross-table sanity-check query (e.g., 'show all file_paths that exist in both tables') as a maintenance tool, but no schema-level constraint is needed.
context: The kickoff named the boundary with reference_book as the principal design question for the work_ticket entity, given that both entity types are workflow files with similar surface fields (title, description, file_path, status). The distinction sits in lifecycle and reference pattern: work_ticket is single-use (produced for one conversation, consumed by it, not subsequently referenced as a source of truth); reference_book is long-lived and multi-consumer (cited by many conversations as authoritative reference). The kickoff named three candidate boundary policies: (a) bright line by intent — authorial intent at creation time settles the classification; (b) bright line by lifecycle — work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions; (c) re-categorization — allow a work_ticket to be re-categorized to a reference_book after the fact if it turns out to be referenced repeatedly. The kickoff also named a boundary case: the methodology workstream master plan was produced by SES-011, whose kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) was retroactively cited by successor planning documents — is that retroactive forward-referencing enough to reclassify the kickoff as a reference_book?
decision: **Adopt bright line by intent at creation, with no in-place re-categorization, and explicit support for parallel coexistence of work_ticket and reference_book records pointing at the same `file_path`.** A file is a work_ticket if it was authored as a single-use seed for one specific conversation; it is a reference_book if it was authored as a long-lived multi-consumer reference. The classification is settled at creation and does not change. If a file authored as a work_ticket turns out to be referenced repeatedly enough to warrant authoritative-reference treatment, a separate reference_book record is authored against the same `file_path`; the two records coexist as independent governance views with different identifiers, different lifecycles, and different fields. The schema does NOT enforce mutual exclusivity at the `work_ticket_file_path` / `reference_book_file_path` level — each column is unique within its own table, not unique across the union. The methodology workstream master-plan boundary case raised in the kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) resolves as a work_ticket; the retroactive forward-referencing by successor planning documents is archival mention, not authoritative reading.
rationale: Intent-at-creation is the simplest rule that maps cleanly to the project's actual authoring pattern. Every kickoff and every CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md file is authored with single-use intent for one specific conversation or execution; that intent is captured at creation and does not change even if the file is later mentioned by successor documents. Re-categorization (option c) would require the schema to admit moving records between tables, which forces a version-history-of-the-classification-itself concern that adds complexity without operational payoff — the catalog of work_tickets and the catalog of reference_books should each be stable. Lifecycle-based classification (option b) would conflate the versioning model (reference_book has child versions; work_ticket does not) with the family definition (single-use vs multi-consumer), which are independently meaningful and should not be collapsed. Parallel coexistence (the same file_path string in both tables) is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views: the kickoff's archival role as 'the file that opened SES-011' lives on a work_ticket record; if the file's role evolves to 'the document successor planning conversations cite authoritatively,' a parallel reference_book record is authored and both records coexist. Uniqueness scoped to each table preserves the schema's simplicity.
title: work_ticket boundary discipline with reference_book: intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, file_path may coexist across both tables

alternatives_considered: **Four-status lifecycle collapsing drafted and ready.** Rejected because the 'ready to open' versus 'still authoring' distinction is operationally meaningful and asked about by consultants. **Four-status lifecycle collapsing cancelled and superseded.** Rejected because the 'abandoned outright' versus 'abandoned with successor' distinction is a meaningful authorial signal and informs the supersedes edge. **Three-status lifecycle (drafted, consumed, abandoned).** Rejected as too coarse — loses ready-to-open visibility and supersession distinction. **Workflow lifecycle without terminal-edge requirements (rely on the references-create dialog's filtering to prevent invalid states).** Rejected because the references-create dialog cannot enforce status-level invariants from outside the access layer; the consumed-requires-edge and supersession-requires-edge rules must live at the access layer to be reliable. **Reactivation-from-terminal admitted (e.g., a consumed work_ticket can be reset to ready if the conversation's first opening was a false start).** Rejected because the project's discipline that role-resumption is modelled as a new record is well-established and reactivation would complicate the lifecycle without operational payoff; a false-start consumption is modelled as soft-deleting the work_ticket administratively and creating a fresh record.
consequences: Five-value CHECK constraint on work_ticket_status. Four per-status timestamp columns added to the schema. Access-layer enforcement of (a) valid-transition map per §3.4.1, (b) truly-terminal posture (no transitions out of terminal states), (c) consumed-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-consumed violation, (d) supersession-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-superseded violation. The new consumed-requires-edge precedent is captured for the remaining two schema-design conversations to inherit; close_out_payload's `applied` terminal will follow the same pattern with the inbound edge from deposit_event. Spec §3.4 carries the full lifecycle specification including transition map, transition semantics, both edge-requirement rules, the single-use enforcement rule (DEC-144 separately), kind-enum semantics, and soft-delete semantics. Acceptance criteria 5-7 and 9-10 (spec §3.7) translate the lifecycle and edge rules into testable statements.
context: The work_ticket entity's lifecycle expresses the single-use seed document's progression from authoring through consumption (or abandonment). DEC-137 from SES-050 settled the documentary-vs-workflow distinction: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only (reference_book's posture); workflow-shaped lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126. Work_ticket is workflow-shaped — consultants ask 'when did this kickoff become ready?' and 'when did the conversation consume it?'; the transitions are real-time-meaningful events. The kickoff named a working set of five status values (drafted, ready, consumed, superseded, cancelled) and asked whether all five are needed and what transitions are valid. Additionally, the kickoff's family-definitional question — what distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level — points at the `consumed` status as the family-defining terminal: the act of consumption is what binds a work_ticket to its one conversation, and the schema needs to enforce that binding.
decision: **(a) Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: `drafted` → `ready` → `consumed`, plus two abandonment terminals reachable from drafted or ready: `cancelled` and `superseded`.** Default starter status is `drafted`. (b) **Terminal-states-are-terminal discipline inherited from DEC-125 verbatim.** All three terminals (consumed, cancelled, superseded) reject every outgoing transition, including transitions between terminal states. A consumed work_ticket cannot become superseded even if the kickoff is later rewritten — the rewrite is a new work_ticket. (c) **Per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126.** Four per-status timestamp columns (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at), server-set on transition, mutually exclusive among the three terminal-state columns (exactly one is populated on any terminal record). (d) **Supersession-requires-edge rule inherited verbatim from DEC-125.** Setting status to `superseded` requires an outgoing `supersedes` edge to another work_ticket record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "supersession_requires_successor_edge"}`. (e) **NEW — Consumed-requires-edge rule.** Setting status to `consumed` requires an inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge from a conversation record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge"}`. This is the inverse-direction analogue of the supersession-requires-edge rule: supersession-requires-edge requires an outgoing successor edge; consumed-requires-edge requires an inbound consumer edge. **Establishes new cross-spec precedent**: for any governance entity with a terminal state defined by an external act of consumption or application, the transition to that terminal requires the edge naming the consumer or applier to be present, in whichever direction (inbound or outbound) the relationship is canonically modelled. Close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal is the next case (inbound edge from deposit_event required); deposit_event's append-only-with-one-timestamp shape sidesteps the question (the record's creation IS the apply event).
rationale: Five statuses capture the operational vocabulary cleanly: drafted (authoring in progress, file may be incomplete), ready (file committed, conversation can be opened), consumed (conversation opened against this work_ticket — terminal), cancelled (abandoned without consumption — terminal), superseded (abandoned with a successor work_ticket — terminal). Collapsing drafted and ready would lose the visibility into 'what kickoffs are ready to open' versus 'what kickoffs are still being authored' — a real consultant query. Collapsing cancelled and superseded would lose the distinction between 'abandoned outright' and 'abandoned with a successor carrying the work forward' — a meaningful authorial signal. Truly-terminal terminals match the precedent from DEC-125 and DEC-131; the project's discipline that a terminal entity's role-resumption is modelled as a new record (not an edit) is preserved. Per-status timestamps fit the workflow-shaped lifecycle per DEC-126; the consultant queries the timestamps answer ('when did this become ready?' 'when was it consumed?') are real-time-meaningful. The consumed-requires-edge rule is the schema-level realization of the family-definitional binding between a work_ticket and its one consuming conversation: a work_ticket cannot be consumed without the consumer being known. The cross-spec precedent generalizes naturally: any terminal defined by an external act requires the edge naming the actor; the direction of the edge follows from the verb-orientation of the relationship.
title: work_ticket workflow-shaped lifecycle with five statuses, truly terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), and supersession-requires-edge rule

alternatives_considered: **Status-conditional rule (single-use enforced only on consumed status).** Rejected because it permits a drafted or ready work_ticket to accumulate two or more inbound consumption edges before consumption, which is operationally ill-defined (which conversation 'really' consumed it?) and forces ad-hoc resolution at status-transition time. **Documenting the property without access-layer enforcement.** Rejected because family-definitional invariants must be reliable; documentation alone permits backfill or accidental writes to bypass the family classification silently. **Enforcing the rule via a database-level UNIQUE constraint on (target_id, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket').** Rejected because the references table is generic (no per-kind unique constraints); the access layer is the right place for kind-specific invariants. **Allowing multiple inbound consumption edges but flagging in a maintenance view.** Rejected for the same reason as 'documenting only' — the invariant must be enforced, not surfaced after the fact.
consequences: Access-layer write path for the `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` relationship kind checks the target work_ticket's existing inbound edge count before admitting the write. The check is a single COUNT query against the references table filtered by target_type='work_ticket', target_id=<the_work_ticket>, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket'; the write is admitted only if the count is zero. Acceptance criterion 8 (spec §3.7) translates the rule into a testable statement. The rule applies regardless of the work_ticket's status (acceptance criterion explicitly tests this). PI-022's backfill pass must respect the rule; ambiguous historical cases (a file used by two conversations before this entity type existed) are resolved per spec §3.8.2 — either by authoring one work_ticket with the first consumption typed and the second as a generic `references` edge, or by authoring two work_ticket records with different identifiers pointing at the same file_path. The schema admits the latter per DEC-142's parallel-coexistence rule (uniqueness on file_path is per-table, not cross-table).
context: DEC-117 defines work_ticket as family 2 of the three workflow-file families: single-use seed documents with at most one consumer. The family-definitional property is the single-use semantics — a work_ticket is produced for one specific conversation, consumed by that conversation, and not subsequently referenced as a source of truth. The schema must express this single-use property as a hard constraint at the access layer; otherwise the family-definitional property is documented but not enforced, and historical or accidental multi-consumer attachments would silently corrupt the family classification. The consumed-requires-edge rule (DEC-143) requires the inbound edge to exist at consumption, but does not bound the count of inbound edges. The single-use property requires an upper bound (≤ 1) on inbound edges of the consumption kind.
decision: **Enforce at the access layer: a work_ticket record may have at most one inbound edge of kind `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`, regardless of the work_ticket's current status.** POST attempting to add a second inbound consumption edge to a work_ticket that already has one returns HTTP 422 `{"error": "work_ticket_single_use_violation"}`. The rule applies at all statuses: a drafted or ready work_ticket may have zero inbound edges; a consumed work_ticket has exactly one; a cancelled or superseded work_ticket may have zero (the typical case — abandoned before consumption) but may also have one if a partial consumption occurred before abandonment. The rule never admits two or more.
rationale: The single-use semantics defined by DEC-117 are family-definitional and must be enforced at the schema level to be reliable. Documenting the property without enforcing it would allow accidental or historical multi-consumer attachments — for example, a backfill script attempting to record two conversations consuming the same kickoff — to silently bypass the family classification. The 422 error surfaces the violation immediately, forcing the consumer to choose between (a) using the existing single-use work_ticket for the right conversation and creating a new work_ticket for the second consumer, or (b) reclassifying the file as a reference_book if it genuinely had multiple authoritative consumers. The status-independence of the rule prevents corner cases where a cancelled work_ticket might be reused or where a consumed work_ticket might accidentally accumulate a second consumption edge. The rule is also what schema-level-distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book: reference_book admits arbitrarily many inbound edges (each `workstream_planned_in_reference_book` from a workstream that cites it as master plan, each generic citation from a conversation, etc.); work_ticket admits exactly one of the typed consumption kind.
title: work_ticket single-use enforcement rule: at most one inbound conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edge per work_ticket, regardless of status

alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with separate work_ticket_target_deliverable column.** Rejected because the target deliverable is almost always named in the first paragraph of work_ticket_description; a separate column duplicates the narrative. **Field inventory with work_ticket_author column.** Rejected for minimum-viable scope; Doug is sole author today. **Adopt the tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind in addition to the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` kind.** Rejected because it breaks the one-edge-per-relationship pattern and would force consumers to handle two equivalent forms. **Adopt the typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind in this release.** Rejected because the query frequency does not justify schema-level investment per DEC-133's precedent; the generic `references` kind is adequate. **Adopt a sub-kind taxonomy below the four named work_ticket_kind values (e.g., kickoff_prompt with sub-kinds session_prompt, update_prompt; claude_code_prompt with sub-kinds apply_prompt, build_prompt).** Rejected as premature taxonomy specialization; the four values cover the project's observed work-ticket families and a sub-kind axis can be added in a future release if querying by it becomes routine.
consequences: Work_tickets table has eleven columns. CHECK constraints on identifier format (`^WT-\d{3}$`), kind enum (four values), status enum (five values), file_path validation (non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix). Per-status timestamp columns are nullable and server-set on transition; the consumed/cancelled/superseded triple is mutually exclusive on any terminal record. No new relationship-kind vocabulary added by this spec; the entity type `work_ticket` is already listed in conversation.md's additions table and the existing generic kinds plus the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` cover every relationship work_ticket needs. PI-022's backfill pass populates the eleven content/classification/file-pointer/timestamp fields for historical work_ticket records; the backfill policy on historical multi-consumer cases and unknown timestamps is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.
context: Three field-inventory and vocabulary items decided at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: (a) the complete field inventory beyond identifier, status, and base timestamps; (b) whether to introduce the tentative inbound relationship-kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` named by conversation.md §3.3.2 as a candidate; (c) whether to introduce a typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind for the kickoff-prompt read-list citation pattern (every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books). Each item is structurally a vocabulary or schema-shape decision that the spec must lock before the per-spec completeness gate is met.
decision: **(a) Field inventory (eleven columns total):** identity fields (work_ticket_identifier, work_ticket_title); content fields (work_ticket_description, work_ticket_notes); classification fields (work_ticket_kind, work_ticket_status); no foreign-key columns (every relationship lives in `refs` per DEC-124's references-edge precedent); file pointer (work_ticket_file_path); three inherited base timestamps (work_ticket_created_at, work_ticket_updated_at, work_ticket_deleted_at); four per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126 for workflow-shaped lifecycle (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope: work_ticket_author and work_ticket_owner (Doug sole today, one migration away), work_ticket_target_deliverable (folded into description), work_ticket_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation implicit per DEC-115/116), content-blob storage (file lives in git), sub-kind enum (the work_ticket_kind enum is closed at four values for this release). **(b) Decline tentative kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation`.** Conversation.md §3.3.2 named it as a candidate inbound-from-work_ticket-perspective name for the same relationship that conversation.md §3.3.1 already declared as outbound-from-conversation-perspective via `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`. The existing outbound kind is the sole canonical edge for the relationship, viewed from work_ticket's side as inbound. Introducing a parallel inbound name would create two relationship-kind values for the same edge and break the one-edge-per-relationship pattern enforced by the source-first naming convention (DEC-048). **(c) Decline typed kind `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` for read-list citations.** Every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books; the citation pattern is structural and recurs across all kickoff prompts. However, applying DEC-133's frequency-justified test: the question 'what work_tickets read reference book RB-042' is operational metadata about authoring, not a queried-by structure (consultants don't run that query at scale). The generic `references` kind covers the citation adequately. If queries by read-list become routine in operation, a future release adds the typed kind with the matching vocab and access-layer support (one-line vocab.py + backfill pass converting existing generic edges).
rationale: **(a) Field inventory** follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; the four per-status lifecycle timestamps fit DEC-126's workflow-shaped lifecycle precedent (work_ticket is workflow-shaped per DEC-143). Deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. The target-deliverable description is folded into work_ticket_description because most kickoffs name the target deliverable in the first paragraph of their description; a separate column would duplicate the narrative. **(b) Declining the tentative kind** preserves the one-edge-per-relationship pattern. The source-first naming convention (DEC-048) settles direction: the conversation entity is the actor that 'opens against' the work_ticket; the kind name expresses the conversation's verb, with work_ticket on the receiving side. Introducing a parallel inbound name would force every consumer of the references table to handle two equivalent forms of the same edge. The decline mirrors DEC-132's tentative-kind-name resolution from SES-049 (where conversation.md declined the tentative `conversation_succeeds_conversation` inverse name). **(c) Declining the typed read-list kind** is the application of DEC-133's frequency-justified test: typed sequencing or relationship kinds are introduced when the entity-family frequency of the query justifies the schema-level investment. Read-list citations are authored once at kickoff drafting time and rarely queried-by; the generic kind suffices. The retrofit cost is low (one line of vocab + a backfill pass converting existing generic edges to the typed kind), so deferring is non-prohibitive if the query pattern emerges.
title: work_ticket field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation kind; declines typed work_ticket_reads_reference_book kind

alternatives_considered: **API surface with version-management sub-endpoints.** Rejected because work_ticket is single-use, not versioned; the sub-endpoints would have no semantics. **Master pane without Kind column.** Rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the work_ticket catalog. **Master pane with status-first default sort (per the kickoff's hint).** Rejected in favour of the partial adoption — status is the fourth column (visible without scrolling) and the Status filter combo serves the browse-by-status pattern, but the default sort remains identifier-ascending for cross-spec consistency with workstream/conversation/reference_book. **Detail pane without conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering (always show all four columns even when null).** Rejected because nullable timestamps cluttered the pane unnecessarily — the conditional rendering matches the underlying data and stays clean. **Open-string kind field instead of closed enum.** Rejected per DEC-138's precedent that closed enums with `other` sentinels are the structural pattern for kind classifications. **Six-value kind enum naming session_prompt and update_prompt as first-class values.** Rejected as premature speciation; the CBM filename conventions are captured as kickoff_prompt with title/description distinction for this release. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum).** Rejected because the consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, and end-to-end backfill exemplar deserve dedicated criteria; sixteen matches the project's working baseline.
consequences: Eight REST endpoints to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The list endpoint's `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters combine additively (both filters narrow the result set). Master-pane Kind column requires the friendly-label mapping for the four enum values (e.g., 'Kickoff prompt' for `kickoff_prompt`). The detail-pane's conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering follows the underlying nullable columns; the references-section displays the inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge prominently as the family-defining relationship per spec §3.6.3. Edit dialog hint logic for missing consumption/supersedes edges adds a small UX layer atop the access-layer 422 enforcement. Soft-delete is administrative and orthogonal to the lifecycle statuses; the operational vocabulary distinguishes 'archive an erroneous record' (soft-delete) from 'this work_ticket was abandoned' (cancelled status) from 'this work_ticket was consumed' (consumed status). The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria, explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md and reference_book.md as predecessor specifications, foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the eighteen prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..140 from SES-048/049/050 as inherited precedents.
context: Routine items batched together at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the toolbar Status filter combo, the soft-delete posture relative to the cancelled and consumed status outcomes, the kind enum membership and the `other` sentinel semantics, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults or the precedents established by DEC-128/134/140, but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). No version-management sub-endpoints (work_ticket is single-use, not versioned). All endpoints return the V2 `{data, meta, errors}` envelope. **(b) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission; helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier. **(c) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Status, Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. The Kind column is the third column (between Title and Status) — operationally meaningful classification, parallel to reference_book's pattern. Kind selector and Status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters; both default to 'all'. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter per the kickoff's note that browse-by-status is the work_ticket's natural pattern. **(d) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section, collapsed by default), kind combo, file_path, status combo, lifecycle timestamps section (read-only labels rendered conditionally for non-null timestamps), ReferencesSection. **(e) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. Edit dialog detects missing consumption edge when transitioning to `consumed` and surfaces an inline hint; same for missing supersedes edge when transitioning to `superseded`. **(f) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `cancelled` status (a lifecycle outcome — work_ticket was abandoned without being consumed) and `consumed` status (a successful-use outcome — the conversation opened against it). **(g) Kind enum**: closed four-value enum (`kickoff_prompt`, `claude_code_prompt`, `ad_hoc_prompt`, `other`). The `other` sentinel is the documented escape hatch for kinds that emerge in operation. The CBM `SESSION-PROMPT-*` and `UPDATE-PROMPT-*` filename conventions are captured as `kickoff_prompt` for this release with the distinction recorded in title or description; a future release adds first-class enum values if those families warrant. **(h) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; this spec matches workstream's sixteen, conversation's eighteen, reference_book's sixteen), covering schema migration, identifier and kind and status enum validation, terminal-states-truly-terminal, consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, supersession-requires-edge rule, lifecycle timestamp server-set behavior, file_path repo-relative validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint behavior with kind and status filter parameters, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore round-trip, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against this conversation's own work_ticket.
rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. No version-management sub-endpoints because work_ticket is single-use (the file is consumed once and not subsequently versioned). The Kind column is a natural addition parallel to reference_book's; consultants ask 'what apply-close-out prompts are queued' or 'what kickoffs are ready,' and kind plus status are the two operational axes. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter — a consultant browsing the work_ticket catalog typically wants to see what is `ready` to be opened (the kickoff's note explicitly named browse-by-status as the natural pattern); the toolbar combo serves this without committing to a status-first default sort that would diverge from reference_book's identifier-first cross-spec consistency. Conditional rendering of lifecycle timestamps in the detail pane (only non-null timestamps shown as read-only labels) keeps the pane clean across the lifecycle. Soft-delete posture matches workstream/conversation/reference_book precedents and is explicitly distinct from the two lifecycle outcomes (cancelled, consumed). The closed four-value kind enum covers every work_ticket observed in the project's history; the `other` sentinel preserves additive extensibility. Sixteen acceptance criteria match reference_book's and workstream's counts and fully satisfy the gate; each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
title: work_ticket API surface, default UI with Kind column and Status filter combo, soft-delete posture, closed four-value kind enum, sixteen acceptance criteria

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — two-letter CO or CP.** Rejected for ambiguity (CO reads as Company; CP is too generic). **Option (c) — four-letter PYLD, CLOSE, CLOUT.** Rejected as length-without-disambiguation; the three-letter form is unambiguous against the existing prefix set. **Option (d) — five-letter CLOSEOUT.** Rejected as visually cluttering when paired with the NNN suffix.
consequences: All close_out_payload records use the COP-NNN identifier pattern. The access layer's identifier-validation regex is ^COP-\d{3}$. The next-identifier helper endpoint returns {"next": "COP-NNN"} for the next available number. The cross-spec consistency check before build-planning verifies no collision against any other governance-entity prefix introduced by the six schemas (workstream uses WS, conversation uses CONV, reference_book uses RB, work_ticket uses WT, close_out_payload uses COP, deposit_event chooses its own at its conversation).
context: Per the spec guide section 6, identifier prefixes must be 2 to 5 uppercase letters and must not collide with existing prefixes. The kickoff prompt named COP as the working assumption; alternatives examined were two-letter forms (CO, CP) and four-to-five-letter forms (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE). The existing prefix set: DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT. DEC-123 affirmed each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5-letter range without locking the cross-spec norm at any particular length.
decision: **Adopt COP as the close_out_payload entity identifier prefix.** Three letters, matching the most common existing prefix length (DEC, SES, RSK, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT). Identifier format: COP-NNN, zero-padded to three digits (COP-001, COP-046, etc.). The two-letter form CO was rejected for ambiguity (reads as Company or Carbon monoxide); CP was rejected as too generic. Four- and five-letter alternatives (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE) add length without disambiguation gain. COP survives the collision check against the full existing prefix list.
rationale: Three-letter is the dominant precedent length and reads cleanly with the standard NNN suffix. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation chooses its own prefix length, three letters here is appropriate without binding the deposit_event conversation to any particular choice on its own facts.
title: close_out_payload entity identifier prefix and format: COP-NNN, three-letter form, no collision with existing prefixes

alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — JSON column.** Rejected as denormalization of content that lives authoritatively on the filesystem. Adds a write-time consistency burden (database column may drift from file) and a column-vs-file integrity question on every read. **Option (b) — text column with parse.** Rejected as worst-of-both-worlds: same storage cost as (a) without the in-database query benefit. **Option (d) — hybrid file plus snapshot column.** Rejected as redundancy under git's existing audit guarantees; the snapshot adds maintenance burden (when does the snapshot get refreshed? how does it stay in sync with the file?) for marginal benefit. The derived-view endpoint pattern named in the chosen option supplies content-query convenience without the synchronization burden.
consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries one file-pointer column (close_out_payload_file_path) with the same repo-relative validation rules as work_ticket_file_path and reference_book_file_path. The same physical file may legally coexist as a file_path value across all three tables (work_ticket, reference_book, close_out_payload) under the section-2 boundary discipline; uniqueness is within each table only. The apply script's evolution to consume payload content from the file (current behaviour) versus from a database lookup of file_path (alternative behaviour) is a build-planning concern — both are consistent with this schema. The future derived-view endpoint, if shipped, is non-breaking; no table change.
context: The kickoff prompt named this as the principal design question — how the structured JSON payload is represented in the database. Four options surfaced: (a) JSON column storing the payload directly for in-database queryability; (b) text column with on-demand parse; (c) file-path-with-metadata fields where the file remains the source of truth; (d) hybrid file-plus-snapshot. The choice has consequences for how the build-planning conversation's apply path interacts with the payload entity. All 50+ historical payloads under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ live as JSON files; the apply script (apply_close_out.py) reads the file directly.
decision: **Adopt option (c): repo-relative close_out_payload_file_path column as the only payload-content representation; no payload_content JSON column on the entity table.** The payload's authoritative content lives in the file under git, exactly as reference_book records' content does. The database carries the governance view (identifier, title, description, status, lifecycle timestamps, references to producing conversation and applying deposit_event). The apply script continues to read the file at apply time; future content-query needs are satisfied by a derived-view endpoint (provisional path GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/content) that reads file_path on demand and parses the JSON, not by a stored column. The schema admits the future endpoint additively without requiring a table change.
rationale: The cross-spec precedent is decisive. DEC-139 (reference_book) and DEC-145 (work_ticket) both adopted repo-relative file_path with no content column — file-tracking governance entities store the path, not the content. Close_out_payload is structurally identical in shape: a file at a deterministic location under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/, produced once, applied once, audited via git history. The fact that the content is JSON rather than markdown is a content-format detail, not a governance-shape difference. Denormalizing a 25-to-60 KB JSON blob into a database column per record adds storage cost, mutation-vs-file-integrity questions, and write-time validation complexity for no first-class query need that the file itself does not already satisfy.
title: close_out_payload content representation: repo-relative file_path pointer only, no payload_content column

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only with no soft-delete.** Rejected because legitimate record-cleanup operations exist (e.g., removing a stale draft authored by mistake). Append-only fits the deposit_event side of the family-3 pair — the apply-outcome record — not the payload side which is mutable in pre-apply states. **Option (c) — three-status simplification (drafted → applied; cancelled and superseded collapsed into a single 'abandoned' terminal).** Rejected because cancelled and superseded carry different governance semantics — cancelled has no successor; superseded names one. Collapsing them loses the distinction between 'discarded' and 'replaced by'. **Option (d) — admit pause/resume by allowing transitions out of cancelled.** Rejected per the truly-terminal precedent established by workstream and conversation. A payload that needs to resume is a new payload that supersedes the prior; the audit trail prefers two records with a supersession edge over one record with a non-monotonic history.
consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries four per-status lifecycle timestamp columns (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at) in addition to the three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). The access layer enforces: status-transition validation per the lifecycle map; applied-requires-inbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the deposit_event edge); supersession-requires-outbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the supersedes edge); production-edge-required-at-all-statuses (HTTP 422 if attempted without exactly one outbound close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge); single-producer rule (HTTP 422 if more than one production edge attempts to coexist). Soft-delete is orthogonal to lifecycle state; a record can be in any of the five lifecycle statuses and additionally soft-deleted. This conversation does NOT introduce a new cross-spec precedent — every rule follows an inherited precedent applied to this entity's facts. The append-only-vs-soft-delete posture for deposit_event is deferred to that conversation.
context: The kickoff prompt named the lifecycle questions as four sub-questions: status set, append-only-vs-soft-delete, schema version field, and inbound-edge requirements for terminal states. The cross-spec precedents in force at conversation open: DEC-125 (truly-terminal terminals, supersession-requires-outbound-edge), DEC-131 (multi-state workflow-shaped lifecycle for governance entities that need it), DEC-143 (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — work_ticket's consumed-requires-edge, explicitly named close_out_payload's applied-terminal as the precedent's first downstream application).
decision: **Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: drafted → ready → applied, plus terminals cancelled and superseded.** Default starter status is drafted. The applied terminal requires an inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge (realizing DEC-143's inverse-pattern precedent on this entity's facts). The superseded terminal requires an outgoing supersedes edge to another close_out_payload (mirroring DEC-125's pattern verbatim). The production-linkage edge close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is required at all statuses regardless of lifecycle state — including drafted — because the family-3 definition (per DEC-117) is that a payload is produced at a conversation's close; the producing conversation is part of the payload's identity. All three terminal states (applied, cancelled, superseded) are truly terminal — no transitions out, including transitions between terminal states. Re-application of a payload is modelled as a new payload that supersedes the prior; reactivation as a status transition is not supported.
rationale: The shape parallels work_ticket exactly because the governance roles are structural mirrors: work_ticket has drafted → ready → consumed; close_out_payload has drafted → ready → applied. Both have terminals cancelled and superseded. Both have a single non-starter terminal (the successful-use outcome) requiring a specific external-act edge to be present. The truly-terminal posture inherited from workstream and conversation prevents audit-blurring (an applied payload becoming un-applied would break the deposit_event's referent). The production-edge-required-at-all-statuses rule realizes the family-3 definition cleanly: a payload without a producing conversation is malformed by DEC-117's definition, regardless of how complete the payload file itself is.
title: close_out_payload workflow-shaped lifecycle: five statuses with truly terminal terminals, applied-requires-edge rule realizing the SES-051 precedent, supersession-requires-edge rule, production-edge required at all statuses

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — foreign-key column conversation_id on the close_out_payloads table.** Rejected per the references-edge-over-FK cross-spec precedent. Modifying the conversations table or adding columnar coupling between governance entities is the pattern the precedent specifically declines. **Option (c) — typed close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload sequencing kind.** Rejected per DEC-133's frequency-justified test; sequencing in the close_out_payload family is rare enough that the generic supersedes kind suffices and the typed kind would add vocabulary noise without query value. If a real frequency emerges, the typed kind is a one-line vocab.py addition in a future workstream.
consequences: One new relationship-kind vocabulary entry close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is added by the build-planning conversation alongside the other governance schemas' additions, with the matching _kinds_for_pair clause and the Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. The access layer enforces exactly-one-outbound-edge cardinality at all statuses for this kind. The inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload kind from the next conversation's entity is listed informationally in this spec's section 3.3.2 only; its vocab.py registration and access-layer at-most-one-inbound cardinality enforcement belong to deposit_event.md. Same-type supersession is admitted automatically once close_out_payload is in ENTITY_TYPES because the existing _kinds_for_pair rule for same-type pairs admits supersedes.
context: Every close_out_payload is produced at exactly one conversation's close (per DEC-117's family-3 definition). The kickoff prompt named this relationship's mechanism as an open question: foreign-key column on the close_out_payloads table versus references-table edge. The cross-spec precedent locked by DEC-124 and DEC-130 is references-edge over foreign-key for all parent-child governance relationships. Separately, the question of whether close_out_payload sequencing (one payload replaced by another for the same conversation) should be typed (e.g., close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload) or use the generic supersedes kind was governed by DEC-133's frequency-justified test.
decision: **Production-linkage lives in the references table with a new relationship kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; cardinality is exactly-one outbound edge per close_out_payload record, enforced at the access layer at every status including drafted.** A conversation may be the target of zero or more such edges (zero if not yet closed; one in the typical single-payload-per-conversation case; more than one if drafts were superseded and replaced). The new relationship-kind vocabulary entry is registered in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, with the _kinds_for_pair clause binding it to the (close_out_payload, conversation) pair only. The ENTITY_TYPES set adds close_out_payload (conversation is already in ENTITY_TYPES from SES-049's registration). Same-type supersession reuses the existing generic supersedes kind for the (close_out_payload, close_out_payload) pair — no new typed kind close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload is introduced.
rationale: References-edge for parent-child governance relationships is the locked cross-spec precedent. No reason on this entity's facts to deviate. The production relationship is naturally one-edge-one-kind, consistent with conversation's belongs_to_workstream pattern and work_ticket's inbound-from-conversation pattern. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, typed sequencing kinds are introduced when the entity-family's sequencing is structurally frequent. Close_out_payload sequencing (one draft replaced by another) is rare in practice — most conversations produce exactly one payload that is then applied. The frequency-justified test points at generic supersedes for the same-type pair, consistent with workstream's posture and unlike conversation's (where the every-conversation-belongs-to-a-predecessor-or-successor frequency justified the typed kind).
title: close_out_payload production-linkage via references-edge with new kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; declines typed sequencing kind in favour of generic supersedes

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — add close_out_payload_kind enum with values like 'session_close_out' and 'bulk_import_payload'.** Rejected because no other variants exist or are currently anticipated. Adding the enum now would require populating every record with the same value indefinitely; adding it later (when a second variant actually emerges) is a one-line additive migration. **Option (c) — add close_out_payload_schema_version column with default 1.0 for all historical records.** Rejected as premature; the JSON structure has been stable across 50+ files. **Option (d) — denormalize record-counts-by-kind (decisions_count, planning_items_count, references_count) into columnar fields.** Rejected because the values are easily computed from the file on demand and would require a write-time consistency burden between file content and column values.
consequences: The close_out_payloads table has exactly eleven columns. No kind enum. No schema_version column. No counts columns. Future additive migrations remain available for any of these if real-use signal supports them; the schema accommodates them without breaking existing records. The detail pane in the desktop UI does not render a Kind row (since the field does not exist); the master pane's column list does not include a Kind column for the same reason. If a derived-view endpoint exposing payload content is shipped in a future build, it can compute and return record-counts-by-kind from the file at request time.
context: The kickoff prompt named the field-inventory question and three specific sub-questions: whether to include a close_out_payload_kind classification enum (since other state-write package variants might emerge), whether to include a close_out_payload_schema_version field tracking payload structural version, and whether to denormalize record-counts-by-kind metadata into columnar fields. The existing 50+ payload files all share the four-section shape (label, session, decisions, planning_items, references) with no version field on the payload content itself.
decision: **Adopt the eleven-column shape: close_out_payload_identifier, close_out_payload_title, close_out_payload_description, close_out_payload_notes, close_out_payload_status, close_out_payload_file_path, plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) and four per-status lifecycle timestamps (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at).** Decline a close_out_payload_kind enum because every record in this table is the same kind (the single artifact type close-out-payload-at-conversation-close); the kind enum has no taxonomic axis to express. Decline a close_out_payload_schema_version column because the payload's JSON structure has been stable across all 50+ historical files; tracking schema version is premature optimization. Decline denormalized record-counts-by-kind columns; the file_path read at use time supplies the counts when needed.
rationale: The eleven-column shape parallels work_ticket's field inventory verbatim (work_ticket has eleven columns too: identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status, file_path, three base timestamps, four per-status lifecycle timestamps). Two structural differences worth noting: work_ticket has a kind enum (four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) while close_out_payload has no kind enum because no taxonomic axis exists; close_out_payload uses a different status set (drafted/ready/applied vs. work_ticket's drafted/ready/consumed) reflecting the different terminal-state external act (apply vs. consume). The decline of payload_schema_version is principled: the schema admits the column as a one-line additive migration if structure ever evolves, and pre-adding the column on the speculation that structure will evolve violates minimum-viable scope. The decline of record-counts-by-kind columns reflects the same scoping discipline as the file_path-only content-representation decision (DEC-148): the file under git carries the authoritative content; derived views compute counts from it on demand.
title: close_out_payload field inventory including four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines close_out_payload_kind enum (single-kind entity) and close_out_payload_schema_version column (structure stable)

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only after POST, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE.** Rejected because legitimate pre-apply mutation cases exist (correcting a typo in description before the payload is finalized as ready; updating notes after the payload is authored but before it is applied). **Option (c) — collapse Status filter combo into the inherited 'show deleted' toggle.** Rejected because the two filters do different work (lifecycle status vs. soft-delete state); combining them would conflate orthogonal concepts. **Option (d) — defer the Status filter combo to a future UI release.** Rejected because the operational pattern is structurally part of the entity's day-one use (the very first reason to browse the close_out_payloads catalog is to see what is queued for apply), and adding the combo at build-time is a small ListDetailPanel filter machinery extension matching work_ticket's pattern.
consequences: The build-planning conversation produces a build slice for close_out_payload that mirrors work_ticket's build slice in shape: one Alembic migration creating the close_out_payloads table; one access-layer module with the eight standard methods; one FastAPI router with the eight endpoints plus the next-identifier helper; one desktop panel using ListDetailPanel with the four columns and the Status filter combo toolbar addition; one detail pane rendering the eleven fields plus the ReferencesSection widget with the family-defining production-edge prominently displayed; create/edit/delete/restore dialogs matching the v0.3 pattern; sixteen test cases mapped to the acceptance criteria. Vocabulary additions consolidated with the other governance schemas' additions into one vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. No deviations from V2's standard API or UI architecture; the close_out_payload panel reads, looks, and behaves like the other governance-entity panels.
context: The remaining design questions after lifecycle, relationships, fields, and content representation were settled: which API endpoints to expose, which UI deviations from default the entity needs, what soft-delete semantics apply, and what acceptance criteria define correct implementation. The cross-spec precedents for these questions were locked by DEC-128 (workstream — standard defaults), DEC-134 (conversation — standard defaults with one natural addition), DEC-140 (reference_book — standard API with three version-management sub-endpoints, Kind column and version-history detail pane addition), and DEC-146 (work_ticket — standard API with Kind column and Status filter combo additions).
decision: **Adopt the standard V2 endpoint set with no deviations: GET /close-out-payloads (with ?include_deleted and ?status query filters), GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads, PUT /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, PATCH /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, DELETE /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/restore, GET /close-out-payloads/next-identifier.** All endpoints use the {data, meta, errors} envelope. Identifier auto-assignment server-side on POST omission. The desktop UI uses the default layout with one natural addition: a Status filter combo in the master-pane toolbar (parallel to work_ticket's addition), because browse-by-status is the operational pattern for payloads — ready to see what is queued for apply, applied to see what has been deposited. No Kind column in the master pane (no kind field exists). Soft-delete is the default behaviour and is explicitly orthogonal to lifecycle state: cancelled is a lifecycle outcome (the payload was abandoned without being applied; the record remains visible in list endpoints), applied is a successful-use outcome (the deposit_event records the apply; the record remains visible), soft-deleted is a record-management operation (the consultant removed the record from default views). Sixteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, identifier validation, status-transition validation, the three terminal-states-truly-terminal cases, the three edge-requirement rules (applied/superseded/production), per-status timestamp server-set behaviour, file-path validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint shape, identifier-auto-assignment race-freedom, soft-delete round-trip, vocabulary registration, sidebar position, master pane columns, detail pane rendering, and end-to-end backfill of a representative historical payload.
rationale: Standard defaults plus one natural addition is the established posture for governance entities with operational browse patterns (work_ticket and reference_book both did this on their own facts). The Status filter combo addition is naturally justified by the close_out_payload's operational pattern: a consultant scanning the entity catalog wants to see 'what is queued for apply' (status=ready) or 'what has been applied' (status=applied) at a glance. Soft-delete as the posture for the payload side of the family-3 pair (with append-only naturally fitting deposit_event on the other side, deferred to that conversation) is consistent with DEC-013's framing of when append-only is the right pattern — append-only fits transactional records (a decision, a session, a deposit event), not state-write packages with legitimate pre-apply mutability.
title: close_out_payload API surface, default UI with Status filter combo addition, soft-delete posture distinct from cancelled lifecycle status, sixteen acceptance criteria

alternatives_considered: **Option B — definitions + assignments in YAML.** The deployment artifact fully describes the running security posture; new users require a YAML edit and a Configure run. Defensible for small, stable user bases where 'redeploy on new user' might even be a feature (auditable user lifecycle in git). Rejected because the operational cost compounds quickly (every CBM mentor recruitment adds churn), the YAML loses its 'structure declaration' purity by mixing in identity data, and the industry pattern is clear that policy-as-code stops at the role boundary rather than extending to membership.
consequences: Recorded as Q7 in the amended gap-analysis Section 6 (commit eb1d7e7). The role and team declarations in programs/security.yaml describe structure only — no members:, users:, or assignments: blocks on roles or teams.
