When the v1.2 implementation lands (Parts A–E per DEC-154), the role_manager.py and team_manager.py deploy managers create and update Role and Team records via /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team but do not touch the User entity or any user-to-role / user-to-team linkage tables. Operators manage user lifecycle (creation, role assignment, team membership, deactivation) through the target CRM's standard administrative UI. The definitions-only posture is final for v1.2 and v1.3; revisiting requires a new decision.
context: Opening question of how to express deployment-time security in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The fundamental fork: does the YAML own role and team *definitions* (the structures — what each role can access, which teams exist) AND user-to-role and user-to-team *assignments* (the membership state — which users belong to which roles and teams), or only definitions, with assignments treated as runtime data managed in the target CRM UI? Both options are defensible: the definitions-plus-assignments model fully describes the running security posture from the deployment artifact, but requires a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The definitions-only model leaves runtime user management in the CRM UI but matches the industry pattern of treating IAM policy as code and identity membership as runtime state.
decision: **Adopt definitions-only.** YAML declares role and team structures and their permissions (scope-level access, system permissions, field-level permissions when v1.3 ships). User-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed exclusively in the target CRM UI; they are not represented in any YAML file and not touched by the deploy pipeline. Adding a new user (e.g., onboarding a new mentor at CBM) does not require a YAML edit or a Configure run — the user is created in the CRM UI and assigned to roles and teams in the CRM UI.
rationale: Matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state). For CBM's operational reality (mentors onboarded continuously as recruitment progresses), the alternative would require a YAML commit, push, and Configure run for every new mentor — operationally heavier than the structural benefit it provides. The structure of the security posture is fully described in the YAML (which roles exist, what each can do, which teams exist); the membership state lives in the CRM where it changes most frequently and where the CRM UI already provides the management surface. Decouples the deployment artifact's churn rate from the user population's churn rate.
title: YAML security scope: definitions only, not user assignments

alternatives_considered: **Option A — narrow v1.2.** Ship the existing plan (Parts A–D as originally drafted) without scope-level or teams. Scope-level access and teams remain Manual Configuration line items. Rejected because the deployment artifact cannot describe a meaningful security posture without scope-level access — the Mentor role cannot be expressed at all (every role would either see all entities or none). The cost of waiting on field-level to ship anything at all is too high. **Option B — expanded v1.2.** Ship the existing plan plus scope-level access plus teams in a single v1.2 release. Defensible but doubles the v1.2 surface area, delays delivery of either piece, and forces field-level into a release before the additional pilot input the original plan flagged as needed has accrued. Rejected on delivery speed and on the principle that field-level deserves more pilot data before commitment.
consequences: Gap-analysis Section 6 amended end-to-end (commit eb1d7e7) — Q1, Q2, Q3 amended; Q7 and Q8 added; five v1.2 parts (A–E) and a deferred-to-v1.3 section. UPDATE-PROMPT for the next conversation authored at PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (commit f152181; SHA fix at 4ff2e43) directing the drafting of app-yaml-schema.md v1.2 sections covering Parts A–E. The v1.2 implementation prompt series for Claude Code (analogous to v1.1's archived A–H series at PRDs/_archive/yaml-schema-prompts/) is the downstream artifact for the conversation after that — covering loader extensions, validator extensions, role_manager.py and team_manager.py, and API client extensions for /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The v1.3 field-level + presets work is queued behind the v1.2 implementation series. MR-pilot items MR-MC-AC-001/002 remain on the MANUAL CONFIGURATION list through v1.2 release.
context: The existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md targeted YAML schema v1.2 for the entire Role-Based Access Control category — Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions, the original target driven by MR-MC-AC-001/002), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel and layout role-aware visibility, added 05-03-26). Verification against the actual codebase in this conversation confirmed: (1) the loader at espo_impl/core/config_loader.py recognizes only version, description, content_version, entities, and relationships — no security-related top-level keys; (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist, so an unknown security: block parses as a silent no-op; (3) no role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers exist; (4) the EspoCRM REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities at /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The strawman drafted earlier in the conversation covered scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no), teams, and system permissions — none of which appeared in the existing v1.2 plan. The existing v1.2 plan covered field-level access; the strawman did not. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.
decision: **Adopt Option C — scope-level + teams as v1.2; field-level shifts to v1.3.** Category 6 splits across two schema versions. v1.2 ships five parts: Part A — Roles declaration (kept from original plan); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access with whitelist semantics (NEW); Part D — System permissions including assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (renumbered from original Part D, content preserved). v1.3 ships the deferred field-level read/edit permissions (was original Part B) and named permission presets (was original Part C). Dedicated security file is renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect the roles + teams scope.
rationale: Scope-level access and teams are universal to every role in every domain (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; every team-scope access level requires a team to exist) and translate directly to target-CRM role and team primitives without needing condition-expression infrastructure. Field-level access is the harder problem (state-dependent permissions, asymmetric per-role per-field overrides, cross-CRM mapping ambiguity, condition-expression dependency) and the original plan's own commentary acknowledged that field-level 'deserves careful design across at least one more pilot domain before commitment.' Reordering ships meaningful security capability now (the Mentor role can be expressed in YAML for the first time, even if imperfectly) instead of waiting on the full field-level design. The MR-pilot's two field-level items (MR-MC-AC-001/002 — ten admin-only fields and ten state-dependent mentor-editable fields) remain Manual Configuration through v1.2 and ship at v1.3.
title: Category 6 reordering: v1.2 ships scope-level access + teams (Parts A–E); v1.3 ships field-level + presets

alternatives_considered: DEPT (four letters) — rejected for visual collision with 'Department' in unrelated contexts. DEPOS (five letters) — rejected for length-without-disambiguation; no parallel five-letter precedent exists in governance or methodology. EVT (three letters) — rejected for reading as generic 'event'; loses the deposit-bucket family connotation. APPLY (five letters) — rejected for reading as a verb rather than entity name; collides with the apply script and apply-close-out prompt series naming. DP (two letters) — not seriously considered (too short; reads as initials).
consequences: DEP becomes the canonical identifier prefix for the new entity type. Format DEP-NNN matches every other governance prefixed identifier in V2. No vocabulary or naming conflicts surface in the cross-spec consistency check. The COP/DEP pairing makes the family-3 deposit-bucket reading visually obvious in audit queries and database snapshots.
context: The deposit_event entity is the sixth and final governance entity type designed in the governance-entity schema-design workstream. Per the spec guide section 6 cross-spec consistency conventions, the identifier prefix is 2-to-5 uppercase letters, zero-padded to three digits, and must not collide with the existing prefix list (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT, COP). The kickoff prompt named DEP as the working assumption with DEPOS, DEPT, EVT, APPLY as alternatives raised.
decision: Adopt DEP as the identifier prefix with format DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits (e.g., DEP-001, DEP-054).
rationale: Three-letter form matches the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP, plus the two-letter exceptions WS, WT, RB and the four-letter PROC). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading: a COP-NNN payload is applied by DEP-NNN-or-other events. Survives the collision check against the current prefix list. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5 letter range, three letters is appropriate here.
title: deposit_event identifier prefix and format: DEP-NNN

alternatives_considered: Option B — workflow-staged multi-status (running → success/failure/aborted, with per-status timestamps, PUT/PATCH admitted during running, edit dialogs for the status transition). Rejected on the grounds that (a) the 'running' state lasts ~3 seconds and is essentially never observed in operation, (b) PUT/PATCH endpoints and editable dialogs contradict the fact-about-an-event intuition the entity is meant to capture, (c) the unclean-crash visibility benefit it would provide is small relative to the cost (apply runs interactively in ~3 seconds; crashes are rare and the terminal output is right there), (d) it would reopen the precedent the predecessor specs anticipated. Option A — born-terminal append-only without log capture. Rejected after the user's pushback as too thin on diagnostic detail: a crashed-mid-flight apply would leave no governance record and no log file to consult; the operator would have only the (potentially-closed) terminal output. The Option C synthesis (born-terminal append-only WITH log capture) addresses both concerns.
consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment. A third lifecycle category for governance entities. Cascading effects: deposit_event has _outcome (not _status), single _created_at timestamp (no per-status timestamps), POST + GET only API surface, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE, no soft-delete, read-only UI with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs. The apply script (apply_close_out.py) requires modification per Slice D of the build-planning conversation: write stdout to a log file during apply, POST the deposit_event record at end with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated. A new directory (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/) is established as the canonical log file home; whether files there are git-tracked or gitignored is a build-planning policy decision.
context: The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The kickoff prompt named a four-state workflow lifecycle (running, success, failure, aborted) as a working assumption while explicitly noting that 'the deposit_event entity is expected to introduce the append-only-with-one-timestamp precedent' per the close_out_payload spec section 3.4.6 and work_ticket spec section 3.4.3 anticipations. The choice cascades into status/outcome field semantics, timestamp count, API surface (PUT/PATCH admitted or not), and UI shape (read-only audit log versus editable workflow panel). The user pushed back on the initial recommendation by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis, prompting a synthesis option to be put on the table.
decision: Adopt born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture. The deposit_event record is POSTed by the apply script at apply completion with outcome and all diagnostic fields populated; no intermediate 'running' state; no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE; single timestamp (deposit_event_created_at) carrying the event moment. A new deposit_event_log_file_path field (repo-relative path per the file-pointer precedent from reference_book/work_ticket/close_out_payload) points at a stdout transcript captured during apply, providing durable diagnostic detail without status-transition machinery.
rationale: Born-terminal append-only realizes the precedent anticipated by predecessor specs (close_out_payload section 3.4.6, work_ticket section 3.4.3), matches the operational shape of apply (creation IS the event), and yields the smallest possible API and UI surface for an entity whose purpose is durable auditing rather than workflow tracking. The 'running' intermediate state has no operational meaning for a 3-second apply and would introduce edit machinery incompatible with the fact-recording intent. The log file synthesis addresses the user's continuous-status concern by capturing diagnostic detail in a separate durable artifact (the log file) rather than forcing the entity itself into a workflow lifecycle. The synthesis preserves the structural simplicity of born-terminal append-only while addressing the legitimate diagnostic visibility need that motivated the user's pushback. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped (workstream, conversation, close_out_payload, work_ticket) and documentary (reference_book).
title: deposit_event lifecycle shape: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log capture (new cross-spec precedent)

alternatives_considered: Per-target-type kinds within references-table (deposit_event_wrote_session, deposit_event_wrote_decision, etc., four kinds, four pair clauses) — rejected for unnecessary vocabulary growth when a single conceptual relationship is being modeled. JSON column on deposit_events table (deposit_event_records_written array of target_type/target_id pairs) — rejected for bypassing V2's relationship-vocabulary discipline, making the reverse query JSON-search-bound rather than a direct refs-table query. Dedicated back-reference table (new deposit_event_records table with deposit_event_identifier, target_type, target_id columns) — rejected for introducing a parallel relationship-storage mechanism outside the references-table discipline without compensating benefit. Target-side back-reference columns (written_by_deposit_event_identifier on each of the five target tables) — rejected for inverting the references-edge precedent, requiring migrations on established methodology/governance entity tables, and making the forward query a fan-out across all target tables.
consequences: One new vocabulary entry in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS: deposit_event_wrote_record. One new _kinds_for_pair clause: source_type='deposit_event' and target_type in ('session', 'decision', 'planning_item', 'reference') admits the kind. Plus the parent-linkage kind deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload from DEC-159's field inventory. Apply script flow at end-of-apply: step 5 creates the deposit_event record, step 6 emits one back-reference edge per HTTP-200/201-created record (409-SKIPped records are not back-referenced). Cardinality: one DEP → many records (zero or more); one record → zero or one DEP (zero if created outside the apply path, one if created by apply). The back-reference edges themselves are NOT recursively back-referenced (the recursion stops at the substantive content layer). Future expansion to additional target entity types is one-line additions to the _kinds_for_pair clause.
context: Each apply attempt creates zero or more records in the database (a session, decisions, planning_items, references, possibly other governance entity types in the future). The deposit_event record needs to point at those records so 'what did this apply create?' and 'which apply created this decision?' are first-class governance queries. The kickoff prompt named four alternative mechanisms: references-table edges (the working assumption per the established DEC-124 references-edge cross-spec precedent), JSON column on the deposit_events table, dedicated back-reference table, or target-side columns. The references-table option further admits a sub-choice between a single generic kind and per-target-type kinds.
decision: Adopt references-table edges with one new generic-verb cross-cutting kind, deposit_event_wrote_record, spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in a single vocabulary entry and a single _kinds_for_pair clause covering all four pairs.
rationale: Continues the DEC-124 references-edge-over-foreign-key cross-spec precedent locked by SES-048. Adds the minimum viable schema growth (one vocab entry, one pair rule). Supports both directional queries via the existing references-table machinery: 'which records did DEP-001 write?' (forward) and 'which DEP wrote DEC-117?' (reverse) are both single refs-table queries. Single generic kind chosen over per-target-type variants on the strength of (a) cross-cutting nature — the relationship intentionally spans multiple target types and a single conceptual kind captures that intent; (b) established generic-verb naming pattern in the existing vocabulary (references, is_about, supersedes, decided_in all use generic-verb naming without target-type binding); (c) minimum-viable schema growth. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, the generic single-kind variant is the right choice for this cross-cutting cross-target-type relationship.
title: deposit_event back-reference mechanism: references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record

alternatives_considered: Option B — at-most-one (one DEP per COP, single-success rule). The apply script checks for an existing successful DEP and refuses to create a second one. Rejected because it forces re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit purpose of the entity and the close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3's invitation to relax. Option C — at-most-one-success-but-multiple-failures. First successful apply creates DEP-1; subsequent successful re-applies refused; failed applies admitted. Rejected because the distinction between 'successful re-apply' and 'failed re-apply' has no operational meaning at the access layer (a successful re-confirmation IS valuable governance information; refusing to record it is artificial).
consequences: Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Surfaces a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec section 3.3.2 and section 3.4.3 carry at-most-one language that needs revision to zero-or-more. Per the kickoff's no-inline-amendment rule, this conversation does not edit close_out_payload.md; the revision is handled at the build-planning conversation's consistency gate. The deposit_events table grows linearly with apply attempts (not exponentially); most COPs will have exactly one DEP, some debugged ones will have a small number. The access layer's atomic transaction on POST /deposit-events with outcome=success against a ready COP transitions the COP atomically; POSTs against an already-applied COP succeed without COP transition; POSTs with outcome=failure leave the COP unchanged regardless of pre-state.
context: The close_out_payload spec section 3.8.3 adopted at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge as the safe default and explicitly deferred the call to this conversation. DEC-156's born-terminal append-only shape forces the question into the open: each apply attempt either creates a new immutable DEP record (multi-event), or the apply script refuses to create a second one (at-most-one). The apply script is already idempotent on re-run (HTTP 409 SKIPs for already-present records), and re-apply is an operationally meaningful pattern (verification, retry-after-partial-failure, debugging). The choice affects edge cardinality, the close_out_payload's status transition rule, and what audit history the deposit_events table captures.
decision: Adopt multi-event-per-payload. Each apply attempt creates a new deposit_event record. A close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures or re-confirmations) do not move the close_out_payload's status (it's already at applied and terminal per the truly-terminal precedent). Failed DEPs against a ready close_out_payload create a governance record of the failed attempt but do not transition the close_out_payload.
rationale: Naturally pairs with born-terminal append-only (DEC-156): each attempt is a fact about an event; multiple attempts are multiple facts. Aligns with the operational reality of re-apply (verification, retry, redeploy). Captures the full apply history of a payload as a governance-queryable artifact. The at-most-one alternative would force re-apply attempts into ghost runs with no governance record, contradicting the audit-log purpose of the deposit_events table. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent — born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record — useful for any future workstream that revisits this producer/consumer pair shape.
title: deposit_event re-apply semantics: multi-event-per-payload (new cross-spec precedent)

alternatives_considered: _status field name (vocabulary consistency with workstream/conversation/close_out_payload/work_ticket) — rejected because the field's semantic shape is permanent fact, not workflow state; naming should follow semantics. Include _notes field — rejected because consultant scratchpad pattern is incompatible with immutable record. SQL-level JSON shape enforcement via SQLite's JSON1 extension — rejected for portability and engine-specific complexity. Retain base-schema _updated_at and _deleted_at columns locked to null/created — admissible at implementation but the spec declares no semantics (build-planning chooses implementation path). Denormalize total-records-written as a separate column — rejected as premature optimization; derivable from records_summary or edge count.
consequences: The deposit_events table has nine columns total. The records_summary JSON shape is canonically {sessions: int, decisions: int, planning_items: int, references: int} with additive extension admitted. The error_info JSON shape carries kind, message, step, optional http_status, optional traceback when outcome=failure; null when outcome=success (access-layer cross-check). The apply_context JSON shape carries apply_script_version, invocation, runner. The log_file_path stores repo-relative paths under the canonical pattern PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log. Field-naming convention break (_outcome rather than _status) is documented in the spec as a deliberate deviation; build-planning's section 7.2 consistency check confirms acceptance as the new precedent rather than violation.
context: Field inventory is mostly cascading from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only fixes timestamp shape; DEC-157 fixes relationship modeling — no foreign-key columns). The conversation surfaced four sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: outcome field name (semantic precision vs. vocabulary consistency); inclusion of title/description fields for parity with predecessor entities; JSON shape lock-in for the three diagnostic JSON fields; base-schema timestamp deviation under full append-only.
decision: Adopt the nine-column shape: deposit_event_identifier, deposit_event_title (auto-generated by apply script), deposit_event_description (auto-generated), deposit_event_outcome (enum success|failure), deposit_event_records_summary (JSON), deposit_event_error_info (JSON, conditional null/non-null on outcome), deposit_event_apply_context (JSON), deposit_event_log_file_path (repo-relative), deposit_event_created_at. Adopt _outcome over _status for semantic precision. Document the three JSON shapes (records_summary, error_info, apply_context) in spec language but not at the SQL constraint layer; Python helper module shares the shape between apply script and access layer. Deviate from base-schema timestamps: no _updated_at (record is immutable), no _deleted_at (no soft-delete; full append-only). Decline _notes (incompatible with immutable record), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable from records_summary sum or wrote_record edge count).
rationale: Identity and content fields (identifier, title, description) provide parity with predecessor entities and avoid runtime computation in master-pane list rendering; auto-generation by the apply script removes consultant authoring overhead. _outcome field name reflects the semantic shape — born-terminal fact about an event rather than workflow state implying transitions; the naming distinction is the precedent being established. Three JSON diagnostic fields capture the rich detail of an apply attempt without committing to a fixed column schema that would need migration for shape evolution; SQL-level shape enforcement adds engine-specific complexity without compensating benefit (Python validator pattern is the canonical enforcement elsewhere in V2). Base-schema deviation on _updated_at and _deleted_at is the precedent being established — born-terminal append-only entities omit update and delete semantics. Field declines (_notes, denormalized count) follow from the entity nature (immutable record, derivable summary).
title: deposit_event field inventory: nine columns including three diagnostic JSON fields; _outcome over _status; declines _notes, _updated_at, _deleted_at, denormalized total count

alternatives_considered: Master-pane sort ascending (cross-panel consistency) — rejected for working against the audit-log read pattern. 'View log' button in detail pane opening the file in the platform's default viewer — rejected as introducing filesystem-access machinery that doesn't earn its complexity for a 30-record-lifetime entity; future-add if real friction emerges. UI Create dialog used exclusively for backfill — rejected as muddying the 'apply script is the only authoring path' intuition; the backfill script approach follows established precedent (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py).
consequences: deposit_events route table registers POST and GET endpoints only. PUT, PATCH, DELETE, /restore return HTTP 405 (or HTTP 404 if framework auto-resolves; implementation choice for build-planning). Detail pane wires no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs and disables the references-create dialog's 'Add reference' affordance. Right-click context menu offers two read affordances. Master pane default sort is identifier descending. Outcome filter combo and badge styling for outcome are wired. Fifteen acceptance criteria establish the build-planning conversation's per-slice acceptance list aggregation baseline. PI-022 backfill is Slice E of the build-planning conversation's implementation plan, executed as a one-off script after the build slices land.
context: API surface and UI shape cascade from prior decisions (DEC-156 born-terminal append-only forces POST + GET only; the read-only nature forces no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs). The conversation surfaced three sub-choices warranting explicit settlement: master-pane sort order (ascending consistency vs. descending audit-log nature); log-file viewer affordance (plain-text path vs. in-app button); backfill authoring path (strict no-Create-dialog vs. admit dialog for PI-022 backfill).
decision: Adopt reduced API surface (POST + GET only, no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE, no /restore). Adopt read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete/Restore dialogs. Master pane: ListDetailPanel with columns identifier, title, outcome (badge-styled), created_at; Outcome filter combo (All/Success/Failure) in the master-pane toolbar; default sort identifier descending (audit-log deviation from V2's default ascending). Detail pane: vertical read-only layout rendering all fields plus the references section with parent-linkage edge prominent at top and wrote_record edges grouped by target_type with count badges; 'Add reference' affordance disabled. Right-click context menu reduced to two lightweight read affordances: Copy identifier, Copy log path. Plain-text log_file_path display with no in-app viewer in this release. Strict no-Create-dialog posture: PI-022 backfill execution via one-off script (Slice E), not via UI. Fifteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, vocabulary additions, POST validation, conditional error_info rule, parent-linkage and back-reference edge handling, atomic close_out_payload transition on success, multi-event-per-payload semantics, records-summary cross-check, method-not-allowed responses, GET envelope and filter, UI panel placement and behavior, apply script modification.
rationale: Born-terminal append-only (DEC-156) forces POST + GET only — no update or delete operations have meaning. Read-only UI follows from the same precedent — the apply script is the canonical authoring path; there is no consultant authoring flow for new records. Master-pane sort descending matches the audit-log read pattern (newest first) and is itself a recognizable signal that this is a log, not a workspace; documented deviation tied to entity nature. Outcome filter combo parallels work_ticket and close_out_payload's Status filter combo on the operational browse-by-classification pattern. Plain-text log_file_path display defers in-app viewer machinery as a future-add pending real friction signal; copying the path to a terminal is two seconds of friction. Strict no-Create-dialog keeps the 'deposit_event is a fact captured by the apply script' intuition clean; PI-022's backfill scope is a build-planning concern handled via one-off script per the predecessor pattern (apply_ses_011_planning_records.py and similar).
title: deposit_event API surface, UI shape, soft-delete posture, fifteen acceptance criteria: minimum-viable read-only audit log

alternatives_considered: Bundle with the next multi-tenancy-routing-fix slice — rejected because the routing fix is parallel methodology work with no entity-type changes; bundling adds risk to both. Bundle with a hypothetical v0.7 'governance + routing' combined release — rejected for the same reason. Defer to v0.8 to bundle with as-yet-unnamed parallel work — rejected because the governance entities are ready to ship now and delay incurs governance-coverage debt.
consequences: __version__ = '0.7.0' set in Slice F. README.md and crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md updated in Slice F. About dialog reflects v0.7 automatically via the version constant. The release tag v0.7.0 is pushed at Slice F close. v0.6 styling remains the baseline state until Slice A's migration applies; v0.7 lands additively without back-port concerns.
context: The governance entity schema-design workstream produced six entity-type schema specifications across SES-048 through SES-054. The build-planning conversation must set the target user-interface version for the release that ships these entities. The active version sequence has v0.6 (styling) most recently shipped (__version__ = 0.6.0 in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py at this conversation's authoring time). The kickoff prompt named the target version as open at this writing.
decision: Target user-interface version 0.7 for the governance entity release. The release is dedicated to the six new entity types (workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) and their supporting infrastructure (vocabulary additions, apply-path integration, retroactive backfill of the governance workstream itself). __version__ bumps to 0.7.0 in Slice F.
rationale: Dedicated release reads cleanly in the version history: v0.4 methodology entities, v0.5 engagement management, v0.6 styling, v0.7 governance entities. Bundling with later work (e.g., multi-tenancy routing fix slices in flight, Cleveland Business Mentors Planning Item 001) would muddy the entity-type release narrative and make per-slice testing harder. The six new entity types plus the apply-path integration plus the PI-022 Phase 1 backfill is already a substantial release; no need to dilute it.
title: Target user-interface version 0.7 for the governance entity release

alternatives_considered: Five-slice topology bundling Slice D with Slice E — rejected because the apply-script modification is independently valuable. Seven-slice topology splitting Slice A into two (schema migrations alone, then access layer) — rejected as over-decomposition; the migration plus the access layer that exercises it benefits from being authored together. Eight-slice topology with one entity type per slice — rejected because the entity types share infrastructure (vocab, migrations, panel base classes); per-entity slicing duplicates effort across slices.
consequences: Six Claude Code execution prompts authored at this conversation's close (committed in this push). Doug executes them sequentially via Claude Code; each slice's acceptance criteria reviewed before the next slice opens. The total v0.7 effort is bounded by Slice A's complexity (largest by line count) and Slice E's data-volume risk (idempotency must be solid). Build closeout session lands at the end of Slice F.
context: Six new entity types plus apply-path integration plus retroactive backfill plus documentation updates is too much for one slice; needs decomposition. The methodology-entity v0.4 release used a slice-per-entity topology (A foundation, B-E one entity each, F closeout); the v0.5 engagement-management used an A-E topology with the schema-and-API in Slice B; the v0.6 styling used an A-F topology with retrofit slices. The governance-entity release shares more structurally with v0.4 (multiple new entity types) but adds two complications: the apply-path modification (Slice D) and the retroactive backfill (Slice E).
decision: Six-slice topology in strict sequential dependency order: Slice A (schema migrations, vocab.py update, ORM models, all six repository modules); Slice B (REST API routers and envelope handling); Slice C (desktop UI panels, sidebar integration, CRUD dialogs); Slice D (apply_close_out.py modifications, log file capture, atomic deposit_event POST); Slice E (PI-022 Phase 1 backfill via one-off script); Slice F (documentation updates, About-dialog version bump to 0.7.0, build closeout session, follow-on planning items). Each slice has its own Claude Code prompt under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.7-{A..F}-*.md. Each slice gates on the prior slice's acceptance criteria passing.
rationale: Six slices is the smallest decomposition where each slice has a coherent scope and an independent acceptance gate. Bundling A with B is feasible but inflates the slice; the API routers benefit from being authored with the access layer already shipped (clearer test boundary). Bundling D with E is feasible but the apply-script modification is a generally-useful change independent of the backfill, and bundling delays the routine close-out improvements until Phase 1 backfill is also done. Six independently revertable slices is the safest topology: a failure in any slice can be rolled back without affecting prior slices, and the application remains in a coherent state (e.g., if Slice C ships and Slice D fails, the API and UI work even though the apply path is still on the v0.6 behavior).
title: Six-slice execution topology for v0.7 in sequential dependency order

alternatives_considered: Sub-grouping into 'Governance — content' (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning items, topics, references) and 'Governance — workflow' (workstreams, conversations, reference_books, work_tickets, close_out_payloads, deposit_events) — rejected as over-decomposition for this release; deferred to signal. Reordering to alphabetize the combined group — rejected because alphabetical order obscures the workstream-order semantic. Introducing a divider between the existing eight and the new six — rejected as a low-value UI affordance.
consequences: Slice C appends the six new sidebar entries to the existing Governance group in the named order. The sidebar configuration file (likely a Python dictionary or JSON config) gains six entries; no new sub-group code paths needed. If future user signal supports sub-grouping, the change is mechanical: introduce a sub-group rendering pattern, move six entries underneath. Until then, the fourteen-entry Governance group is the production layout.
context: The existing Governance sidebar group has eight entries (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning items, topics, references). Adding the six new governance entity panels (Workstreams, Conversations, Reference Books, Work Tickets, Close-Out Payloads, Deposit Events) makes the group fourteen entries deep. The workstream-establishing conversation flagged this as an open question deferred to build-planning. Two reasonable approaches: append the new six at the end of the existing group with no sub-grouping (Option A); introduce a 'Governance — workflow' sub-group for the six new entries (Option B); reorder the existing group to bury or promote certain entries (Option C).
decision: Append the six new entries to the existing Governance group in workstream order (Workstreams, Conversations, Reference Books, Work Tickets, Close-Out Payloads, Deposit Events). No sub-grouping in v0.7. The resulting fourteen-entry group accepted as scannable.
rationale: Sub-grouping (Option B) introduces a new UI pattern that doesn't exist elsewhere in the application; the methodology sidebar group has four entries and the v0.5 engagement-management work didn't introduce sub-groups. Adopting sub-groups here sets a precedent that bleeds across other sidebar groups for inconsistency. The fourteen-entry Governance group is at the threshold of scannable; if signal emerges that users have difficulty finding entries, sub-grouping can be introduced in a future release as a sidebar refactoring. Workstream-order placement is the natural reading order: workstream → conversation → reference_book → work_ticket → close_out_payload → deposit_event mirrors the schema-design conversation sequence and the dependency order of the entities. Reordering the existing group (Option C) breaks muscle memory for existing users.
title: Governance sidebar grouping for v0.7: append the six new entries in workstream order with no sub-grouping

alternatives_considered: Gitignore the log files; store the path in deposit_event_log_file_path but never commit the file — rejected because the file value is wasted (the deposit_event record points at a file that may or may not exist on any given workstation). Track only failure-case logs; gitignore success-case logs — rejected as introducing per-file decision logic at commit time; failure cases are rare so the storage cost is dominated by success cases, and consistent tracking is simpler. Compress logs at commit time — rejected as premature optimization; raw text is searchable in git history without decompression.
consequences: Slice D creates the deposit-event-logs/ directory with .gitkeep and README.md. Slice D's apply script modification writes the log file at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log; the apply's caller (typically Claude Code or Doug running locally) commits the log file alongside the close-out payload commit. Slice E's backfill creates placeholder log files (dep_NNN-historical.log) for the eight historical applies that pre-date the entity type; each is a one-line file noting the log was not captured at apply time. The directory grows by one file per apply attempt going forward.
context: The deposit_event schema (DEC-159) introduced a deposit_event_log_file_path column carrying the repo-relative path to a captured stdout log written by the apply script during each apply run. The deposit_event spec section 3.8.1 deferred to build-planning whether these log files are git-tracked (committed alongside the corresponding close-out payload commit) or gitignored (treated as ephemeral local artifacts with the path recorded but the file uncommitted).
decision: Deposit-event log files are git-tracked under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN.log. Files are committed alongside the close-out payload commit (typically by the apply-script's caller). The directory carries a README.md and a .gitkeep to ensure the directory is present in fresh clones. No .gitignore patterns added for the log files themselves.
rationale: The log file is the diagnostic record of an apply attempt. Failure-case logs especially are valuable: they capture the apply script's stdout up to the point of failure, including the HTTP request bodies and the API response payloads. Without git tracking, these logs are only available on the workstation that ran the apply — useless for code review or for retrospective debugging by anyone else. The cost is modest: ~5KB per apply attempt (the apply script's stdout for a typical close-out payload is a few dozen lines), and apply attempts are infrequent (one per close-out conversation, plus the occasional re-apply for verification). The total directory size after a year of v0.7 operation is on the order of a few hundred KB. Git tracking parallels the close-out payload files themselves (close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json), which are also committed; the deposit-event log is the matching diagnostic artifact for the apply.
title: Deposit-event log file tracking policy: git-tracked under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/

alternatives_considered: Defer the reconciliation to a separate conversation — rejected because the inconsistency would block Slice A's repository implementation; the access-layer logic at close_out_payloads.py needs the settled cardinality. Issue a v1.0.1 patch rather than v1.1 — rejected because the spec guide doesn't recognize a three-segment versioning convention; v1.1 is the natural label for the post-audit revision (paralleling reference_book.md's v1.1 audit-correction pattern). Re-author the close_out_payload spec from scratch — rejected as wasteful; the substantive content is correct, only two paragraphs need revision.
consequences: close_out_payload.md committed at v1.1. The Slice A repository implementation at close_out_payloads.py implements the first-success-transitions logic per the revised §3.4.3. The deposit_event-side repository at deposit_events.py drives the close_out_payload transition atomically via the access layer per the deposit_event spec §3.4.3. The reconciliation is the build-planning equivalent of a v1.1 patch and serves as the precedent for any future cross-spec consistency findings that surface late in a workstream's lifecycle.
context: The deposit_event spec section 3.8.1 surfaced a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec sections 3.3.2 and 3.4.3 carried at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge language that DEC-158 (SES-054 deposit_event Decision 2 — multi-event-per-payload precedent) supersedes. Section 3.8.3 of close_out_payload.md v1.0 explicitly invited the relaxation. The deposit_event-side spec settled the cardinality at zero-or-more; the close_out_payload-side spec needed in-place text revision to reflect the settlement.
decision: Execute the cross-spec consistency reconciliation by revising close_out_payload.md in place to v1.1. Substantive changes: §3.3.2 cardinality column language updated from at-most-one to zero-or-more with first-success-transitions clarification; §3.4.3 applied-requires-edge rule augmented with the full first-success-transitions semantics — the ready→applied transition fires on the first inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge from a deposit_event record with outcome=success; subsequent inbound edges (failures, re-confirmations from re-applies) admitted without further close_out_payload state change; failure edges never drive the transition. The applied-requires-edge rule itself stands — setting status to applied still requires at least one inbound success edge. Revision Control table gains a v1.1 row; Change Log gains a v1.1 entry; Last Updated and Status fields updated.
rationale: The reconciliation is required for cross-spec coherence — the deposit_event spec describes multi-event-per-payload semantics that the close_out_payload spec must admit at the cardinality and state-transition levels. In-place revision is the natural mechanism per the spec guide section 8's revision-in-place posture for cross-spec consistency findings (as opposed to a new conversation). The first-success-transitions semantics make the state machine deterministic: only success drives the transition, only the first success drives it, subsequent edges (success or failure) admit without re-triggering. This matches the apply script's behavior (per the Slice D plan) and the access layer's intended atomic POST behavior.
title: Cross-spec consistency reconciliation executed: close_out_payload.md v1.1 admits zero-or-more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges

alternatives_considered: Single-phase full backfill in Slice E covering everything — rejected because the scope inflates Slice E (~200 records vs ~50 for Phase 1), increases risk of partial-backfill states, and delays v0.7 ship without delivering proportional value. Go-forward only (no backfill) — rejected because the governance workstream's own records are the proof of concept; not backfilling them leaves the application without a single workstream record to display in the new panels at v0.7 ship. Selective backfill (only workstream and conversation records, skipping work_ticket and reference_book) — rejected because the supporting records (kickoffs as work_tickets, schema specs as reference_books) are needed for the cross-entity navigation patterns to demonstrate.
consequences: Slice E executes Phase 1 via the one-off script crmbuilder-v2/scripts/backfill_governance_phase_1.py per implementation plan §2.5. At Slice F closeout, three follow-on planning items authored: PI-023 (Phase 2 — prior workstreams), PI-024 (Phase 3 — prior conversations), PI-025 (Phase 4 — historical applies as deposit_events). Each follow-on PI references PI-022 via is_about edge. PI-022's status remains Open at v0.7 ship; the planning items panel shows it alongside the three new children. Future operational decisions about when to run Phases 2 through 4 are made on a per-phase basis; each phase is its own scoped effort.
context: PI-022 (authored by SES-046, status Open) tracks retroactive migration of governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database. The planning item's description outlines six categories of backfill candidates and three resolution paths (go-forward only, selective backfill, full backfill with reconstructed outcomes). Refinement is the build-planning conversation's responsibility per the workstream plan section 5.4.
decision: Refine PI-022 into a phased execution plan. Phase 1 executes in Slice E of v0.7: backfill the governance entity schema-design workstream itself (1 workstream, 8 conversations, 8 close_out_payloads, 8 deposit_events, 8 work_tickets, 10 reference_books with ~14 version rows, ~70 reference edges). Phases 2 through 4 deferred to follow-on planning items authored at the v0.7 build closeout session: Phase 2 (PI-023) — prior workstreams (methodology entity schema-design, v0.5 engagement management, v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, Cleveland Business Mentors paper test, catalog ingestion); Phase 3 (PI-024) — prior conversations SES-001 through SES-045 and their kickoffs as work_tickets where reconstructable; Phase 4 (PI-025) — historical applies as deposit_events for the ~38 prior close-out payload files. PI-022 itself remains Open at v0.7 ship; discharge waits on completion of all phases.
rationale: Phase 1 is the proof-of-end-to-end test that the new entity types work against real content — and the content closest to hand is the workstream that designed them. The eight conversations (SES-047 through SES-052, SES-054, plus this conversation SES-055) plus their supporting records exercises every new entity type, every new relationship kind, and every access-layer rule. Failures in Phase 1 surface immediately and block Slice F's closeout — exactly the right gating. Phases 2 through 4 are operationally valuable but lower-stakes: they backfill records that are already operating without governance entities (the conversations happened, the payloads applied, the project moved forward); discovering issues in Phase 1 first means Phases 2 through 4 can run against a hardened implementation. Each subsequent phase is scoped at its own time; phases need not run sequentially and can be parallelized across workstreams. PI-022 stays Open as the parent tracker; the three follow-on planning items (PI-023, PI-024, PI-025) are the discharge children.
title: PI-022 refinement: phased retroactive backfill plan; Phase 1 in Slice E covers the governance entity schema-design workstream itself

alternatives_considered: (a) Restructure every governance repository to validate edges before the autoassign insert (impossible because edges reference the not-yet-assigned identifier). (b) Replace the SAVEPOINT pattern with a single explicit BEGIN-then-INSERT-with-retry-on-IntegrityError without nesting (works but rewrites the well-precedented autoassign across every repo). (c) Add a 'remove orphan on rollback' compensating action in every repository (fragile, can't fully reverse side effects). (d) Accept the orphan rows and document the limitation (violates the access layer's transaction contract and would surface as data corruption under any real edge-rule failure in production). Option (e), the SQLAlchemy SQLite recipe, addresses the root cause at the engine layer and is what the bug actually warrants.
consequences: Every transaction begins with BEGIN IMMEDIATE; the connection is in non-autocommit mode (controlled by SQLAlchemy). Concurrent writers wait up to 5s on the lock before erroring. Existing tests stay green (no regressions across the 628-test suite). Future engine work in the per-engagement DB inherits the same correctness. If a real performance problem surfaces under heavy concurrent read load (extremely unlikely for the project's workload), the busy_timeout can be tuned or BEGIN switched to DEFERRED with the deadlock risk re-evaluated.
context: Slice B testing uncovered that a 422 failure on a governance create left an orphaned partial row (verified: a conversation POST missing the workstream-membership edge persisted CONV-001 despite the API returning 422 and the session_scope context manager calling session.rollback()). Root cause: the governance repositories use the v0.4 SAVEPOINT-based autoassign pattern (begin_nested + savepoint.commit for collision-safe identifier assignment) followed by post-insert edge-rule validation. Under pysqlite's default autocommit emulation, RELEASE SAVEPOINT durably commits to disk because no outer BEGIN was ever issued; a later session.rollback() then has nothing to undo. The methodology repos never exercised the pattern because they validate before insert, so the bug was latent until the governance entities' insert-then-validate-edges shape exposed it.
decision: Apply the standard SQLAlchemy SQLite transaction-control recipe in access/db.py: in the connect handler, set dbapi_conn.isolation_level=None to disable pysqlite's autocommit emulation and add PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000 so contended writers queue rather than fail fast; in a new 'begin' event handler, emit 'BEGIN IMMEDIATE' explicitly so concurrent writers acquire the RESERVED lock at transaction start (avoiding the deferred-then-upgrade deadlock the eight-thread concurrent-POST tests would otherwise hit).
rationale: The fix is the SQLAlchemy-documented recipe for SQLite plus explicit transactions, applies one foundational change (the engine in access/db.py) that makes the entire access layer's transaction semantics correct, and lets every governance repository's validate-after-insert pattern work as written. The methodology repos get the same correctness for free even though they never exposed the bug. The full access+api suite runs faster under explicit transactions (~107s vs ~7min for access alone previously) because the explicit transactions are more consistent for SQLAlchemy's flush ordering. The trade-off is BEGIN IMMEDIATE acquires the RESERVED lock at transaction start even for read-only sessions, mildly serializing reads against writes; for the project's desktop/single-user/test loads this is operationally invisible and is what the busy_timeout exists to absorb.
title: SQLite transaction-control fix — explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE + busy_timeout for proper rollback

alternatives_considered: Option I — resolves relationship_kind only, populate resolution_reference column, leave Gap A and Gap C unaddressed. Cheapest but does not satisfy the audit-trail goal that Doug named as the actual requirement. Option II — Option I plus work_ticket authoring methodology for every Claude Code prompt, no commit entity. Middle scope, covers most of the chain but defers the planning_item-to-commit-SHA traceability piece. Defer Option III to a later release after Option II ships — rejected because the back-fill burden grows monotonically with each conversation that ships without a work_ticket record, and the bundled scope is coherent enough to ship in one workstream.
consequences: Seven planning items authored (PI-027 through PI-033). Seven design decisions named for resolution in the PI-027 methodology conversation (commit identifier strategy, granularity of resolves, ingestion trigger, commit record fields, multi-repo scoping, typed addresses kind, work_ticket authoring rule scope) plus the blocks direction question. The workstream lands as a v0.8 user-interface release per the existing release sequence (v0.7 governance entities, v0.8 code change lifecycle). After landing, every Claude Code prompt committed must be authored as a work_ticket record in the same close-out that produces the prompt file; every close-out that resolves a prior planning_item must declare the resolution in the payload; every conversation that produces commits must enumerate them in the payload for ingestion. PI-033 back-fill walks the existing 25 Open planning_items, the existing Claude Code prompt files that pre-date the methodology, and the historical commits in both repos.
context: The diagnostic script run against CRMBUILDER.db revealed that only one of 26 planning_items shows status Resolved, with the resolution achieved via a manual status flip with no companion governance edge. Three gaps surface: work_tickets are not being authored when Claude Code prompts get committed (Gap A), no resolves relationship_kind exists for planning_items and the resolution_reference column is NULL on every row (Gap B), and git commits are not first-class entities — they live only as text in session.artifacts_produced (Gap C). Three options were framed at increasing scope: Option I closes Gap B only (resolves edge plus resolution_reference population); Option II closes Gaps A and B (resolves plus work_ticket authoring methodology, using the existing work_ticket entity); Option III closes all three (Option II plus first-class commits with git-log ingestion).
decision: Adopt Option III. The Code Change Lifecycle workstream will add the commit entity to V2 as a first-class governance record, add resolves relationship_kinds, build git-log ingestion, and update methodology to author work_ticket records for every committed Claude Code prompt. The planning_item-to-commit-SHA audit chain becomes machine-readable end-to-end via a single SQL join across planning_items, refs, sessions, work_tickets, conversations, close_out_payloads, deposit_events, and commits.
rationale: Doug's explicit rationale: integration burden grows with delay. Every new Claude Code prompt committed without a work_ticket record extends the back-fill burden; every conversation closing without a resolves mechanism leaves more planning_items in indeterminate state. The audit-trail use case (defect investigation traceback from a code change to the planning item that motivated it, used in 2027 to investigate a regression in a feature that shipped in 2026) is not satisfied by free-text artifacts_produced — the prose is read-once. Option I closes the narrow gap (status flips work) but does not give the trace from intent to delivery. Option II covers most of the chain but stops at the conversation entity, requiring out-of-band tooling to walk from a conversation record to its commits. Option III completes the chain so that future defect investigation in the V2 system is a single SQL query against governance data rather than a tool chain across the database, git log, and prose fields.
title: Adopt Option III for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream — first-class commit entities, full planning_item-to-commit-SHA audit chain as data

alternatives_considered: Schema-first — define the commit entity schema and the new relationship_kinds, then write methodology to describe how they get used. Rejected on the dark-design risk. Parallel — methodology and schema spec drafted concurrently. Rejected because the schema spec is structurally derived from the methodology and parallel drafting risks divergence. Iterative — sketch a minimum schema, run a few real conversations against it, refine. Rejected because the access-layer is constrained by migration sequencing and iterative refinement on the database structure is costly relative to a one-pass methodology document.
consequences: PI-027 (methodology) is the first planning item to resolve. PI-028 (commit schema spec) cannot begin until PI-027 ships. The methodology document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md is the source of truth for the schema decisions; if the methodology document changes after PI-028 ships, that is a v0.8.x patch rather than a v0.9 release.
context: The Code Change Lifecycle workstream involves both schema additions (new commit entity type, new relationship_kinds, refs CHECK constraint migration) and methodology changes (work_ticket authoring rule, close-out payload sections, resolution discipline). Two reasonable sequencings: schema-first (define the entity and edges, derive the methodology around the schema's affordances) or methodology-first (write the methodology document defining the seven-stage chain and the access-layer rules, then derive the minimum schema additions from the methodology).
decision: Methodology-first. PI-027 authors the methodology document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md before any schema work begins. The methodology drives the schema spec (PI-028 commit entity schema) and the access-layer changes (PI-029). The seven deferred design decisions plus the blocks direction question are settled in the methodology conversation.
rationale: Schema-first risks designing in the dark. The seven deferred decisions are methodology questions — when does a commit count as resolving a planning item versus merely touching it? Does a conversation's commits all live in one engagement DB or split by repo? Should every kickoff document be a work_ticket or only Claude Code prompts? — that should not be answered by inspecting what the schema makes convenient. Methodology-first surfaces these as named decisions before any code lands, and the schema derives from the methodology rather than dictating to it. The cost is one drafting conversation of Doug's time; the benefit is catching second-order edges that schema-first would miss.
title: Methodology-first sequencing for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream

alternatives_considered: Three planning_items (methodology, build, back-fill) — rejected for hiding intermediate state. Five planning_items (collapse PI-029 with PI-030 into one and PI-031 with PI-032 into one) — rejected because PI-029 (schema plus access plus API) and PI-030 (apply plus ingestion) have independent acceptance gates and benefit from independent resolution edges; same logic for PI-031 (UI) and PI-032 (methodology rollout). Twelve planning_items (one per implementation slice) — rejected for over-decomposition and methodology overhead. PI-033 (back-fill) as a separate workstream rather than a planning_item in this workstream — rejected because the back-fill is structurally part of the same scope and authoring it as a sibling planning_item keeps the audit chain coherent.
consequences: Seven planning items authored. Resolution order is enforced by narrative dependencies in each planning_item's description rather than by typed blocks edges, because the blocks relationship semantics in the project (direction reading of the PI-024 PI-025 PI-026 chain) was ambiguous at this conversation's authoring time and the methodology conversation (PI-027) will settle the blocks direction question in passing. The workstream's overall resolution requires all seven planning_items to reach Resolved status.
context: Option III scope is substantial: methodology, schema spec, access layer plus API, close-out payload plus apply plus ingestion, UI, methodology rollout, back-fill. Two reasonable decompositions: a small number of large planning_items (e.g., three: methodology, build, back-fill) that hide intermediate progress and make granular resolution edges impossible, versus a larger number of small planning_items that increase resolution granularity at the cost of methodology overhead per planning_item.
decision: Seven planning items in strict dependency order: PI-027 methodology document; PI-028 commit entity schema spec; PI-029 schema vocab access layer and API; PI-030 close-out payload schema apply script extension and git-log ingestion tool; PI-031 UI updates for commits panel and planning_item resolution display; PI-032 methodology rollout — close-out template and work_ticket authoring rule documented; PI-033 back-fill workstream — historical work_tickets planning_item resolutions and commits.
rationale: Seven planning_items is the smallest decomposition where each has a coherent acceptance gate and corresponds to a discrete release-shippable deliverable. Three planning_items (methodology, build, back-fill) hide which schema decisions were already made versus which are pending, and offer only three resolution edges across a multi-week workstream — defeating the granular traceability the workstream is being built to provide. Twelve planning_items (one per slice of build work) is over-decomposition; the access layer and the API share infrastructure that benefits from being authored together. The seven-planning_item shape matches the v0.7 governance entity workstream's six-slice topology in spirit (one document deliverable per coherent scope) while collapsing slices that benefit from shared authoring (PI-029 covers schema migrations, vocab edits, access layer, and API together because all four touch the same files and tests).
title: Code Change Lifecycle workstream decomposed into seven planning items in dependency order

alternatives_considered: Option A — users captured to a non-YAML side artifact like users.json alongside the YAML files, paginated GET /User over 200-record batches. Rejected because it changes the audit's product identity and there is no current operational pressure for user-roster visibility. Option B — schema bump to bring user-to-role assignment into the deployment artifact. Rejected because reversing the Section 12 design decision is a much bigger discussion than this audit feature and would conflict with the design principle that user assignment changes do not require redeploy.
consequences: The v1.2 audit captures security structure (roles, teams, scope access, system permissions, role-aware visibility per DEC-172 and DEC-173) but not user records. Operators recreate user assignments manually in each target instance after deploying audited security YAML. If a future feature needs user-roster visibility (for example, comparing source-instance user roster against target-instance user roster after a deployment), it becomes its own feature outside the audit pipeline.
context: Schema v1.3 Section 12 explicitly states that user-to-role assignment is out of YAML scope and that the deployment artifact describes the security structure (what each role can do), not who is assigned to what. The v1.1 audit feature does not capture user records today. The Decision 1 fork in the audit-v1.2 planning conversation was whether to extend the audit to also capture user records as a side artifact (Option A — non-YAML users.json), to push for a schema bump bringing user-to-role assignment into the deployment artifact (Option B), or to leave user records out of scope entirely (Option C).
decision: Option C — user records remain out of scope for the v1.2 audit feature. The audit captures only what the v1.3 schema can represent. No new GET /User method on api_client.py. No users.json artifact. No user records inserted into the client database. If user-roster visibility becomes operationally important later, it can be a separate feature (working name: CRM Instance Inspector) without disrupting the audit.
rationale: Doug's stated rationale: "for now, we are interested in recreating the structure not the user data." The audit's product identity is a YAML-round-trip tool; the output is something an operator can edit and reapply against a target instance. Adding a non-YAML side artifact for users would shift the feature from configuration-capture to configuration-plus-data-snapshot — a meaningfully different product with no current operational driver. The Section 12 design decision to exclude user assignment from YAML is recent (v1.3) and intentional, motivated by the observation that user assignment changes far more frequently than security structure and is appropriately managed in the target CRM admin UI.
title: User records are out of scope for the v1.2 audit feature

alternatives_considered: Option A — audit emits, Configure silently no-ops on unknown top-level keys. Rejected as misleading; the operator would think security was deployed when it was not. Option A-prime — audit emits, Configure treats as NOT_SUPPORTED with a clear MANUAL CONFIGURATION REQUIRED block at the end of the run, matching the existing three NOT_SUPPORTED features (saved views, duplicate checks, workflows). This was the original recommendation, but Doug elected to resolve both sides. Option C — defer security entirely (audit captures only entities/fields/layouts/relationships/filtered tabs; security is its own future workstream covering both audit and deploy together). Rejected because the operational pressure for security round-trip exists now and bundling is straightforward.
consequences: The audit-v1.2 workstream expands from approximately five to six prompts (entity selection, filtered-tab audit, schema plumbing) to eleven prompts (adding deploy-side managers, condition_expression role clause, layout/panel role scoping, and audit-side reversal of role-aware dynamic logic). At workstream end, security structure round-trips cleanly between source and target instances. Personal user-to-role assignment remains a manual step in the target CRM admin UI per DEC-171.
context: Schema v1.3 Section 12 specifies roles, teams, scope access, system permissions, and role-aware visibility in over 500 lines. The schema is paper-only: config_loader.py does not reference roles or teams, models.py has no RoleDefinition or TeamDefinition dataclass, and no role_manager.py or team_manager.py exists in espo_impl/core/. Auditing roles and teams from a source instance is technically straightforward (read endpoints exist on EspoCRM's Role and Team entities) but produces YAML that the deploy engine cannot apply. The Decision 2 fork was how to handle this asymmetry between audit-side capability and deploy-side capability.
decision: Option B — roles and teams are both audited AND deployed in the same audit-v1.2 workstream. This workstream adds the deploy-side role_manager.py and team_manager.py modules following the existing CHECK→ACT pattern, plus the audit-side _discover_roles and _discover_teams methods, plus all the schema plumbing (loader recognition, model dataclasses, validator extensions, pipeline orchestration). The end state is full round-trip on security structure within a single workstream.
rationale: Doug's stated rationale: "I want to resolve both sides of the issue, so option B." Bundling deploy-side managers into the audit workstream produces a coherent round-trip-clean feature at workstream end. The alternative of shipping audit-only and treating roles/teams as NOT_SUPPORTED (Option A-prime, matching how saved views, duplicate checks, and workflows are handled today) would leave the user with half-deployable security YAML and require re-opening the workstream later when the deploy-side managers eventually land.
title: Roles and teams are audited and deployed in the same audit-v1.2 workstream

alternatives_considered: Option B — 12.1 through 12.4 plus 12.6 in this workstream, defer 12.5. Recommended originally because 12.5 is structurally different work and zero CBM YAML uses role clauses today (no current operational driver), but rejected by Doug in favor of comprehensive closure. Option C — 12.1 plus 12.2 only (bare role/team identity, no scope_access or system_permissions or role-aware visibility). Rejected because it would capture who the roles are without capturing what they can do, eliminating most of the value of Section 12.
consequences: The audit-v1.2 workstream grows by approximately four prompts (specifically Prompts F and G) to cover 12.5. condition_expression.py gains a new RoleClause AST variant and the parser/validator/render functions thread it through. Validators check that role names referenced in clauses exist in the program batch's roles: block. The audit's _reverse_dynamic_logic method picks up role-aware clauses from EspoCRM's clientDefs/entityDefs dynamic-logic JSON shape and emits them in the YAML output. Layout-level forRoles: scoping (Section 12.5.2) is implemented as a layout-set selector at deploy time. The evaluate_condition function is not extended for role clauses because role-aware evaluation requires current-user context that the deploy engine does not have — that boundary is correct and not a regression. End state at workstream completion: full v1.3 Section 12 (excluding 12.7 per schema doc) is round-trip-deployable.
context: Schema v1.3 Section 12 has five sub-sections: 12.1 Roles, 12.2 Teams, 12.3 Scope Access, 12.4 System Permissions, 12.5 Role-Aware Visibility, plus 12.6 Deploy Ordering. Sections 12.1 through 12.4 are CHECK→ACT REST operations against EspoCRM's Role and Team entities — the same architectural pattern as every other deploy-side manager in espo_impl/core/. Section 12.5 is qualitatively different work: it extends condition_expression.py with a new role: leaf clause variant, threads role predicates through the existing dynamic-logic validators, adds role-resolution to the audit's reverse-engineering of EspoCRM's logic clauses, and reaches into field-level requiredWhen/visibleWhen, panel visibility, and layouts. The Decision 2.5 fork was which parts of Section 12 to include in the workstream.
decision: Option A — all five parts of Section 12 (12.1 Roles, 12.2 Teams, 12.3 Scope Access, 12.4 System Permissions, 12.5 Role-Aware Visibility) plus 12.6 Deploy Ordering are in scope for the audit-v1.2 workstream. Section 12.7 (field-level permissions and named permission presets) remains explicitly deferred per the schema doc.
rationale: Doug elected to resolve security comprehensively in one workstream rather than stage 12.5 to a follow-up. Bundling 12.5 now avoids re-opening the security workstream later and produces complete v1.3 Section 12 coverage in a single shot. The known disadvantage — 12.5 is structurally different work requiring parser extension — is accepted in exchange for closure.
title: All five parts of schema v1.3 Section 12 are in scope for the audit-v1.2 workstream, including role-aware visibility (Section 12.5)

alternatives_considered: Cached entity list — persist the previous audit's entity list in the client database, show it immediately on dialog open, provide a manual Refresh button. Rejected because it requires new client DB schema, cache invalidation logic, and the Refresh UX affordance for no operationally meaningful benefit.
consequences: No new client DB schema for cached entity lists. Prompt J in the audit-v1.2 series implements the picker as a synchronous get_all_scopes call when the dialog opens. The picker fails gracefully (clear error message in the dialog, no crash) when the source instance is unreachable or returns an auth error. Default state: all entities checked, matching the v1.1 behavior of auditing all categories.
context: The audit-v1.2 workstream adds entity-level scope selection (new AuditOptions.selected_entities field plus an entity-picker UI in audit_entry.py). The picker needs to be populated from somewhere. Two viable approaches: pre-flight live discovery (when the user opens the dialog, call get_all_scopes against the source instance and show the live result) or cached entity list (persist the previous audit's entity list in the client SQLite database, show it immediately on dialog open, refresh on demand).
decision: Pre-flight live discovery. When the user opens the Audit dialog, it calls client.get_all_scopes synchronously (with a brief loading state in the dialog) and populates the entity picker QListWidget with checkboxes from the live result, defaulting all checked. Adds approximately one to two seconds to dialog open time.
rationale: Decided as a routine ARCHITECTURE-mode draft — did not pass the two-part test because both options produce the same end state and the difference is a UX speed-versus-staleness tradeoff. Pre-flight wins because the alternative requires client DB schema for the cache, invalidation logic, and a manual-refresh affordance — meaningful complexity for no real benefit. A full audit also takes only minutes; one additional API round trip on dialog open is operationally invisible. Recorded as DEC-174 anyway because it is a definite architectural commitment that informs Prompt J's implementation.
title: The audit dialog entity picker uses pre-flight live discovery rather than a cached entity list

alternatives_considered: Option B — backfill the six plus YAML schema v1.1 only (WS-002 through WS-008). YAML schema v1.1 had app-yaml-schema.md as a written master plan and a structured eight-prompt arc with clear end-state, structurally the most workstream-shaped of the three questionable candidates. Rejected because admitting it asymmetrically still leaves the broader question ("what's a workstream worth backfilling versus a code-change cluster worth capturing as commit records?") unresolved, and the Code Change Lifecycle workstream is being built to answer that question more cleanly. Option C — backfill all nine (WS-002 through WS-010). Rejected for the same reason scaled up: aggressively comprehensive backfill creates thin workstream records that are better captured as something else once the commit entity ships, and risks duplicating structure that PI-027 through PI-033 will provide.
consequences: Two downstream effects. (1) PI-025 (prior-conversations backfill) inherits a workstream inventory of seven (WS-001 through WS-007) for attaching conversation-membership edges. The three excluded candidates' sessions, if any (the YAML v1.1 series and error-handling series likely had no sessions; the layout-fix triplet had none), receive no workstream membership in PI-025. (2) The Code Change Lifecycle workstream gains specific scope: when PI-027 settles the methodology for capturing code-change clusters as commit records, the three excluded candidates become its first backfill targets. The PI-033 back-fill planning item explicitly anticipates this.
context: The PI-024 kickoff named six clear workstream candidates pre-dating PI-022 Phase 1 (methodology entity schema design, UI v0.5 engagement management, UI v0.6 styling, multi-tenancy routing fix, CBM paper test, catalog ingestion) plus three questionable candidates whose qualification as workstreams depends on the inclusion test (YAML schema v1.1 eight-prompt series, error-handling-prompt series A–E in espo_impl, layout-fix triplet of commits in May 2026). The kickoff's inclusion test: "coherent purpose, multiple connected conversations or prompts, recognizable beginning/end." SES-057 had just established the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (PI-027 through PI-033) which adds a first-class commit entity intended to make planning_item-to-commit-SHA traceability queryable via a single SQL join — designed to capture exactly the kind of code-change clusters the three questionable candidates represent.
decision: Backfill exactly the six clear candidates as workstream records WS-002 through WS-007 in chronological start order: WS-002 Catalog ingestion, WS-003 Methodology entity schema design, WS-004 UI v0.5 engagement management, WS-005 UI v0.6 styling, WS-006 CBM paper test, WS-007 Multi-tenancy routing fix. The three questionable candidates — YAML schema v1.1 eight-prompt series, error-handling-prompt series A–E, layout-fix triplet — remain outside PI-024 and are queued for capture as commit clusters or work_ticket bundles by the Code Change Lifecycle workstream once PI-027 (methodology) and PI-028 (commit schema spec) ship.
rationale: Two reasons. (a) The kickoff's inclusion test is cleanly satisfied by the six clear candidates but only marginally by the three questionable ones: the six have either multi-conversation arcs (v0.4/v0.5/v0.6 each have 5–15 sessions) or a written master plan (catalog ingestion has a PRD plus implementation plan; CBM paper test has a kickoff plus findings; multi-tenancy fix has a planning kickoff plus slice plan plus investigation report) or both. The three questionable candidates have neither — the YAML schema v1.1 series ran entirely as a Claude Code prompt sequence without conversational kickoffs, the error-handling-prompt series was a hardening pass on the espo_impl code path, and the layout-fix triplet was three small commits over a single day. (b) The Code Change Lifecycle workstream is being scoped explicitly to capture commit-level audit trails. The three questionable candidates are exactly the kind of code-change cluster its first-class commit entity is designed to index.
