Backfilling them as workstreams now creates structure that PI-027 through PI-033 will provide more naturally, and may force later cleanup decisions (cancel? supersede?) if the new model surfaces a better representation.
title: Backfill the six prior workstreams listed in the PI-024 kickoff; defer the three questionable candidates to the Code Change Lifecycle workstream

alternatives_considered: Option A — git-commit range (first commit touching the workstream's material as started_at, last commit as completed_at). Most objective and reproducible but conflates planning-prep commits with the work itself; a single late commit unrelated to the workstream's substance can spuriously extend the end date. Rejected because the semantic fit is poorer than session_date range for the audit-query use case. Option C as the primary rule everywhere (document timestamps for both ends). Rejected because not every workstream has a clean kickoff document with a Last Updated timestamp (WS-003 has both a planning prompt and a master plan; WS-007 has a planning kickoff plus a slice plan plus an investigation report), so the rule would itself require per-workstream source-document picks. Using Option C only as a fallback narrows those picks to the two workstreams where the primary rule genuinely degrades.
consequences: Per-workstream lifecycle dates are now reproducible from session_date data plus, for WS-002 and WS-006, the named source document's Last Updated. The choice is queryable retrospectively: workstream_notes records the source rule applied per workstream, so any future audit can compare the stored date against the named source. PI-025 will cross-validate by attaching conversation-membership edges and confirming each conversation's session_date falls inside the parent workstream's [started_at, completed_at] window.
context: Each of the six backfilled workstreams needs workstream_started_at and workstream_completed_at timestamps. WS-001's precedent set workstream_started_at to a wall-clock time approximately matching its first session (2026-05-20T22:00:00, near SES-047's start), and workstream_completed_at was server-set when the workstream transitioned to complete. PI-024's six workstreams require both timestamps backdated since no live transition occurs. Three candidate source rules: git-commit dates of the workstream's material; the session_date range from the first to the last session belonging to the workstream; document-timestamp range from the kickoff/master-plan Last Updated to the closeout payload's session_date or the findings document's Last Updated.
decision: Adopt session-date range as the primary rule (workstream_started_at equals the session_date of the workstream's first session; workstream_completed_at equals the session_date of the last). Use document-timestamp range as a documented fallback for any workstream lacking clean session bookends, with the source document named in workstream_notes for the affected workstream. Concretely: WS-002 (single build session preceded by ~5 days of PRD authoring) uses Option C for started_at (PRD Last Updated 2026-05-09); WS-006 (CBM paper-test sessions live in the CBM engagement database, not CRMBUILDER's) uses Option C for both ends (kickoff Last Updated 2026-05-18; findings v0.2 Last Updated 2026-05-19). The other four workstreams use Option B for both ends. All timestamps stored as YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00 (UTC midnight) since wall-clock precision is not available for these backdated records.
rationale: Two reasons. (a) WS-001's precedent effectively uses session-date range — its workstream_started_at approximates SES-047's session_date, the workstream's first session. Staying consistent with the one prior record is worthwhile. (b) The semantic fit is best: workstreams are conversation arcs, and session_date is the conversation arc's calendar. The audit-query "what work arcs were in flight in May 2026?" returns the most truthful answer when workstream timestamps align with the sessions that constitute them. PI-025 will formalize the same mapping as conversation-belongs-to-workstream edges and can validate PI-024's date-setting against the actual edges; any mismatch surfaces a backfill bug worth fixing, so PI-024's decisions get a free audit downstream.
title: Reconstruct prior-workstream lifecycle dates from session_date ranges where sessions are present; fall back to document-timestamp range where clean session bookends are absent

alternatives_considered: Option A — bundle all six master-plan reference_books with judgment-call picks for split-document workstreams (most likely the PRD for catalog ingestion, the kickoff for paper test, the slice plan for multi-tenancy fix). Rejected because each pick implies a precedence between documents that wasn't established at the time the workstream ran, and the resulting RB records would be harder to undo than to defer. Option C — defer all six master-plan reference_books and all other reference_book backfill to a later phase, including the three clean cases. Rejected because the three clean cases mirror WS-001's pattern exactly with no design ambiguity, and deferring them sacrifices the parity benefit without buying any future design flexibility.
consequences: Two effects. (1) PI-024 lands with three workstreams (WS-002, WS-006, WS-007) carrying no workstream_planned_in_reference_book outbound edge. Queries that count workstreams with master plans return four (WS-001 plus the three new); queries that count workstreams without master plans return three. The asymmetry is self-documenting via the workstream_notes entries on the three bare workstreams, which name their planning documents and explicitly note the deferral. (2) A future reference-book backfill phase is now scoped to handle three additional master-plan records (for WS-002, WS-006, WS-007) plus any non-master-plan reference_book records (PRDs, implementation plans, kickoff prompts, findings documents, slice plans, investigation reports) the engagement wants captured. The design question of whether to expand the reference_book kind enum and how many reference_book records per workstream to admit becomes that phase's primary concern.
context: WS-001 has RB-001 as its master-plan reference_book (kind workstream_master_plan), the established precedent for one such record per workstream. For PI-024's six backfilled workstreams, three have a clean single workstream-plan-style document: WS-003 (methodology-schema-workstream-plan.md), WS-004 (v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md), WS-005 (styling-workstream-plan.md). The other three do not: WS-002 (catalog-ingestion-PRD-v0.1.md plus catalog-ingestion-implementation-plan.md — two documents, neither a workstream-plan-style master), WS-006 (methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md plus methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-findings.md — kickoff plus findings, no plan), WS-007 (multi-tenancy-routing-fix-planning-kickoff.md plus multi-tenancy-routing-fix-slice-plan.md plus multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md — three documents). The PI-024 kickoff framed reference_book bundling as binary (bundle one master-plan record per workstream, or defer all reference_book work to a future phase), with Option B (the asymmetric middle ground) as a recognized alternative.
decision: Adopt Option B. Bundle three master-plan reference_book records into PI-024's apply: RB-011 for WS-003 (methodology-schema-workstream-plan.md), RB-012 for WS-004 (v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md), RB-013 for WS-005 (styling-workstream-plan.md). Create three workstream_planned_in_reference_book edges (WS-003 → RB-011, WS-004 → RB-012, WS-005 → RB-013). The other three workstreams (WS-002, WS-006, WS-007) land bare; their master-plan reference_book records and any other reference_book backfill (PRDs, implementation plans, kickoff prompts, findings documents, slice plans, investigation reports) are deferred to a future reference-book backfill phase that can also decide how to model multi-document planning material.
rationale: Three reasons. (a) Follows WS-001's established pattern (one workstream_master_plan reference_book per workstream) without forcing pick-one judgments on workstreams that genuinely have multi-document planning material. Picking one document from a multi-document set implies a precedence relationship between the documents that may not exist. (b) Surfaces the multi-document-planning design question as its own future decision rather than answering it implicitly through PI-024's pick choices. The future reference-book backfill phase can settle the broader question (workstream_master_plan kind only, or expand to workstream_planning_kickoff / workstream_findings / workstream_investigation_report kinds; one master-plan reference per workstream or several reference_book records per workstream with one designated master) cleanly. (c) Keeps PI-024's prompt scope manageable — three RB records and three edges is a small additive change to the workstream-only prompt and easy to review; six RBs with three needing judgment-call source documents would noticeably complicate the apply script and the review burden.
title: Bundle master-plan reference_book records only for the three prior workstreams with a single-file master plan; defer master-plan records for the three multi-document workstreams to a future phase

alternatives_considered: Option A — validate against a configured list of known personas, warning on unknown values. Rejected: introduces a configuration surface (where does the list live? per-client? in the program YAML itself?) and a coupling between the loader and client-specific PRD content for marginal benefit. Option B — validate against a list inferred from other YAML content. Rejected as too clever; persona: is metadata, not a structural reference.
consequences: No persona-validation logic in config_loader.py. No new dependency from the loader on any client-specific document list. Prompts A, B, and D treat persona: as opaque metadata. If a future feature wants persona-aware validation (for example, a CRM Methodology Auditor that checks YAML against Master PRD content), it can build that as a separate validator outside the deploy-engine loader.
context: Schema v1.3 Section 12.1 defines a persona: field on RoleDefinition as 'documentation metadata — the loader does not cross-check the identifier against any source.' The audit-v1.2 workstream needs to decide whether to honor this schema specification verbatim or to optionally extend the loader to validate persona: values against a known list (such as Master PRD personas for the client implementation) and warn on unknown values. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md carried this as §9.1 with a provisional answer of no validation. SES-060 formalizes the resolution.
decision: No validation. The loader treats persona: as opaque documentation metadata. The audit captures whatever value the source instance carries and emits it as-is. No cross-checking against any list, no warnings on unrecognized values, no new dependency from the loader on client-specific document content.
rationale: Schema Section 12.1 is explicit that persona: is metadata. Cross-checking it would require a source-of-truth list of Master PRD personas, which doesn't live anywhere the loader currently has access to and would couple the loader to client-specific PRD content (a coupling the rest of the loader carefully avoids). The failure mode of a typo'd persona name is 'documentation reader briefly confused' — minor and self-correcting. The validation infrastructure and the loader-to-PRD coupling are not justified by that failure mode.
title: RoleDefinition.persona is documentation metadata only — no loader validation

alternatives_considered: Option A — silent capture, no log line. Rejected: operator visibility into degenerate cases is cheap and useful. Option B — warning emitted as a YAML comment in the output. Rejected: intrusive on every redeploy. Option C — elevate to warning severity that surfaces in the audit summary panel. Rejected: implies a problem where there isn't one.
consequences: Prompt H's _write_yaml_files() emits the informational log line for every captured role with empty scope_access. The validator, the loader, and the deploy side are unaffected — they handle empty scope_access roles identically to non-empty ones. The audit progress dialog's summary line at end of run mentions the warning count if any informational warnings were emitted.
context: If a source EspoCRM instance has a role with no per-entity access — for example, a placeholder role kept for documentation purposes or a role that was created and never had scope_access wired up — the v1.2 audit captures it as a role with an empty scope_access: map. The schema allows this construct (an empty mapping is valid). The audit-v1.2-planning.md v1.0 §9.2 carried the open question: should the audit surface this case to the operator, and if so, how? v1.0's provisional answer was yes-as-informational-log-warning. SES-060 formalizes the resolution.
decision: Yes, informational warning in the audit log, not in the YAML output. When the audit captures a role with empty scope_access, the audit log records a line of the form 'Role X has no scope_access; this role grants no entity access on the source instance.' The YAML output itself is unaffected — the role is emitted with whatever its source state was, including the empty scope_access mapping. The warning level is informational, not error or warning-severity.
rationale: Matches the existing audit log pattern for analogous edge cases (e.g., entity with no custom fields, layout with no rows). Gives operators visibility into 'this role grants nothing on the source and probably wants cleanup' without altering the audited content. Putting the warning in the YAML output would be intrusive (operators would have to remove a YAML comment or warning block on every redeploy), and elevating the severity to warning or error would imply a deploy-blocking condition where there isn't one — an empty scope_access role is valid YAML that deploys cleanly.
title: Captured role with empty scope_access produces an informational audit-log warning

alternatives_considered: Option A — default False per v1.0 provisional answer. Rejected on grounds above. Option B — split default (one True, one False, e.g., filtered tabs default True because lower behavioral footprint, security default False because larger). Rejected as arbitrary; both new options sit at the same conceptual level as existing options, and splitting defaults invites confusion. Option C — prompt operator on first v1.2 run to choose defaults. Rejected as over-engineered.
consequences: Prompts H, I, and J use default=True for the new AuditOptions booleans. Prompt K's documentation includes a release-notes mention of the new defaults so operators know what to expect on the first v1.2 run. First v1.2 audit run after the workstream lands produces security.yaml (under security/) and filteredTabs: blocks automatically. Operators who specifically don't want this output uncheck the boxes once and the AuditOptions persistence preserves that choice on subsequent runs (where AuditOptions persistence already exists; if not, that's a separate feature).
context: Audit-v1.2 adds two new category-wide AuditOptions booleans: include_security (gates the role and team discovery added in Prompt H) and include_filtered_tabs (gates the filtered-tab discovery added in Prompt I). The Audit dialog exposes these as checkboxes. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §9.3 carried the open question: should these new checkboxes default to checked or unchecked? v1.0's provisional answer was default False, on the rationale of avoiding surprising operators on the first v1.2 run. SES-060 revises this.
decision: Both new AuditOptions booleans default to True. The Audit dialog's Filtered tabs and Security checkboxes start checked when the dialog opens. Operators opt out by unchecking. This matches the existing pattern — every current AuditOptions category-wide boolean defaults True.
rationale: The audit's product identity is 'capture the full configuration of a source instance for round-trip deploy.' Every existing AuditOptions boolean defaults True, expressing that identity in code. A default-off new capability is a quiet inconsistency in that identity and has a bad failure mode: operators who never notice the new checkboxes get half-audits indefinitely without realizing what's missing. The 'default-off avoids surprising operators' reasoning that produced v1.0's provisional answer was overcautious for the actual operator situation — Doug is effectively the sole operator at this stage and will read the v1.2 release notes. The one-time surprise of new artifacts appearing on the first v1.2 run is preferable to silent half-audits in perpetuity.
title: AuditOptions.include_security and include_filtered_tabs default to True

alternatives_considered: Option A — overwrite without any guard. Rejected: minor UX cost, real safety benefit. Option B — alongside output (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory). Rejected: file proliferation and deploy ambiguity. Option C — merge (parse existing, diff, emit merged output). Rejected on maintenance grounds.
consequences: Prompt J implements the confirmation dialog as part of the entity-picker UI work. The dialog's trigger condition uniformly matches *.yaml at root and security/*.yaml under the subdirectory. Operators who want to preserve prior audit output have two options: (a) cancel the dialog and manually rename or move the output directory before re-running, (b) point the audit at a different output directory. Neither is automated by the audit; both are operator responsibility.
context: v1.2 audit runs against a source instance whose output directory may already contain artifacts from a prior v1.1 audit run — per-entity YAMLs without filteredTabs: blocks, no security.yaml, possibly with operator edits. v1.0 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §9.4 carried the open question: what happens to the existing files? Three options were framed: overwrite (latest snapshot replaces prior), alongside (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory, both versions coexist in the same root), merge (parse existing files and emit files merging new blocks into existing content). v1.0's provisional answer was overwrite. SES-060 formalizes the resolution and adds a UX guard.
decision: Overwrite, with a pre-run confirmation guard. When the operator clicks Run Audit and the output directory contains files matching the audit emission pattern (any file matching *.yaml at the output directory root OR security/*.yaml under the subdirectory), the Audit dialog displays an overwrite-confirmation prompt: 'Output directory contains N existing audit YAML files; running this audit will overwrite them. Proceed?' The dialog defaults focus to Cancel; the operator must explicitly click Proceed to continue. Empty output directories run without the prompt.
rationale: Audited YAML is a snapshot, not source code. An audit run is the operator's explicit request to capture the current state of the source instance. Hand-editing audit output is an anti-pattern; the right place to fork audit output is into a separate working directory under the operator's control. But the audit itself doesn't enforce or document that, and there is a real failure mode of losing operator edits on a subsequent audit run. The confirmation guard catches that failure mode for a one-line UX cost. The merge option was rejected on maintenance grounds (diffing structural YAML is fragile across schema evolution, renamed fields, reordered blocks); the alongside option was rejected because two YAML versions of the same entity coexisting in the same program root creates real deploy ambiguity.
title: Existing audit output is overwritten with a pre-run confirmation guard

alternatives_considered: Option A — root placement, security.yaml alongside per-entity YAMLs. Rejected on future-proofing grounds detailed above. Option B — per-entity security blocks embedded in the entity YAML rather than a separate file. Rejected because roles and teams are not entity-scoped; the data shape doesn't fit. Option C — named subdirectories per security category (security/roles/, security/teams/) anticipating v1.4. Rejected as premature; v1.4 may or may not produce that structure, and over-anticipating creates its own migration burden.
consequences: Prompt A extends config_loader.py's scan to include security/*.yaml under the subdirectory. Prompt H emits to <output_dir>/security/security.yaml. Prompt J's overwrite-confirmation trigger pattern matches both root *.yaml and security/*.yaml uniformly. The user-guide update in Prompt K calls out the subdirectory convention. v1.4 work picks up the same subdirectory for any additional security-related YAML it produces. Programs audited under v1.2 that need to be hand-edited or forked to a working directory should preserve the security/ subdirectory structure to avoid loader confusion.
context: v1.2 audit emits a new artifact, security.yaml, containing the roles: and teams: blocks captured from the source instance. The placement of this file in a program directory is consequential for two reasons: it determines where the loader looks for the file (Prompt A's config_loader.py scan), and it sets a precedent for where future security-related files land. Section 12.7 (field-level permissions and permission presets) is named in audit-v1.2-planning.md §7 as deferred v1.4 work and will likely produce additional security-related files. v1.1 of audit-v1.2-planning.md §10 surfaced this question separately from the §9 resolutions. SES-060 resolves it.
decision: security.yaml lives in a security/ subdirectory of the program directory, not at root alongside per-entity YAMLs. The loader's program-directory scan is extended to scan both root *.yaml and security/*.yaml. The audit emits to <output_dir>/security/security.yaml, creating the subdirectory if missing. The overwrite-confirmation trigger pattern (DEC-181) matches files at either location. The subdirectory is conventional — the parser does not require any specific filename within it; any YAML in security/ is parsed by the same loader logic as root YAML.
rationale: v1.4 deferred work (Section 12.7) will produce more security-related files (permission presets, possibly field-level permission overlays). Anchoring those files in a single subdirectory now is cheaper than migrating v1.2 outputs when v1.4 lands. The cost is a small loader convention (operators learn that security YAMLs live in security/, not at root like entity YAMLs) and one extra scan target in config_loader.py. The alternative — flat placement at root — would either (a) clutter v1.4 program directories with mixed entity and security files, or (b) require migrating all v1.2-audited outputs when v1.4 ships. Both are worse than the small loader convention being adopted now.
title: security.yaml lives in a security/ subdirectory of the program directory

alternatives_considered: Option (b) SHA-as-identifier rejected — breaks the NNN-prefix uniformity for marginal gain; SHA ordering is hash-random not chronological; identifier-comparable queries (e.g., 'CM-101 came before CM-102') would not work. Option (c) full both-canonical rejected — having two equally-canonical references to one record forces every downstream code site to pick which is canonical for its use, multiplying complexity. Three-digit CM-NNN rejected on the volume argument; even a single moderately active workstream produces dozens of commits.
consequences: Every commit record carries a four-digit CM-NNNN identifier assigned client-side at apply time. Audit-chain queries return CM-NNNN values; downstream code refers to commits by CM-NNNN in prose. The /by-sha/{sha} endpoint covers SHA-first callers (notably the git-log ingestion helper specified in DEC-185 and the future commits-panel UI). The high-volume nature of commits (versus other governance entities in the low hundreds) is acknowledged in the schema by choosing four-digit padding from inception.
context: The Code Change Lifecycle workstream introduces the commit entity as a first-class governance record. The kickoff named three identifier-strategy options: (a) CM-NNN sequence-style identifier with SHA stored as a separate field, (b) SHA-as-identifier with the full or stable-prefix SHA as the primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier per the prefixed-identifier convention plus SHA as a unique secondary key with a /commits/by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. The decision shapes how every future query, prose discussion, apply-prompt reference, and audit-chain walk refers to commits.
decision: Option (a) refined to four-digit padding plus the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint from option (c). Commit identifier is CM-NNNN (four-digit zero-padded sequence; matches REF-NNNN's high-volume four-digit pattern; will not need bumping for at least 10,000 commits). The full 40-character SHA is stored in a separate commit_sha column with UNIQUE INDEX. A GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint handles full or prefix-SHA lookups; prefix ambiguity returns HTTP 409 with the candidate list. Client-side identifier assignment per the existing prefixed-identifier convention (crmbuilder CLAUDE.md line 62).
rationale: Uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability. Every other governance entity uses the same NNN-prefixed shape; breaking the pattern for one entity type asks every reader to remember which entity gets which identifier shape. The /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally; routine audit-chain queries join through the CM-NNNN identifier and never see the SHA-translation hop. The four-digit choice (over three-digit CM-NNN) acknowledges that commits will be the highest-volume governance entity in the system and the four-digit padding aligns with REF-NNNN's already-established precedent.
title: Commit entity identifier strategy: CM-NNNN with full SHA in a separate commit_sha field plus /by-sha/{sha} convenience lookup

alternatives_considered: Per-commit only — accepts the authoring discipline cost in exchange for direct SQL; rejected on the discipline-burden argument and the resolved-status-overstating problem (10 contributing commits would suggest 10 'resolutions' when only one delivery happened). Both grains — accepts doubled edge volume for fastest SQL on both query directions; rejected as redundant since both directions are queryable under the per-conversation model with one extra join.
consequences: Apply transaction generates exactly one resolves edge per resolved planning_item and atomically flips planning_item.status from Open to Resolved in the same transaction. Manual status flips outside the resolves-edge mechanism are forbidden by methodology (per methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md section 5.4). The diagnostic 'planning_items in Resolved status without a resolves edge' becomes a data-integrity violation signal that the diagnostic script flags. Multi-conversation contribution is captured via addresses edges (DEC-188) without doubling commit-level edge volume.
context: A conversation that delivers a planning item commits possibly N times to deliver it. The resolution-declaration edge can live at commit grain (commit-to-planning_item), conversation grain (conversation-to-planning_item), or both. The choice shapes payload authoring discipline, edge volume, and the meaning of planning_item.status='Resolved'.
decision: Per-conversation only. Single resolves relationship_kind admitting the pair (conversation, planning_item). Multi-conversation work — where work on a planning_item spans multiple conversations and only the final one resolves — uses a separate addresses kind (per DEC-188) admitting (conversation, planning_item) for the non-terminal contributing conversations. The resolves edge is unique per planning_item (UNIQUE on target_type+target_id filtered by relationship_kind='resolves'); a planning_item can be resolved by at most one conversation. The 'commits that contributed to PI-NNN' query walks refs(relationship_kind IN ('resolves','addresses') AND target=PI-NNN AND source_type='conversation') then joins to commits via the commit_conversation_id FK.
rationale: Per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided in a close-out payload, matches how planning_item.status transitions (one event per planning_item, not N), and avoids an authoring discipline judgment ('did this kickoff commit really contribute to PI-NNN or just the methodology commit?') that should not be required at close-out time. Per-commit grain forces that judgment; both-grain doubles edge volume for marginal gain. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine.
title: Granularity of resolves edges: per-conversation only, with multi-conversation contribution via addresses

alternatives_considered: Option (a) raw manual enumeration — rejected on forgotten-commit silent-gap failure mode. Option (b) git hook — rejected on commit-time API-coupling and sandbox-unhookability of sandbox commits. Option (c) polling — rejected on latency, polling-state, and close-out-timing awkwardness. Option (d) apply-time git scan without payload declaration — rejected because apply non-determinism if repo state diverges between conversation close and apply time (rebase, force-push) silently changes what gets ingested.
consequences: Every close-out turn ends with a helper invocation that emits the commits payload section. The helper is implemented in PI-030's scope along with the close-out payload schema extension and apply script update. Until PI-030 ships, conversation close-outs continue to use the v0.7 payload format without the commits section. The helper specification includes per-repo bracketing, accommodating the conversation-scoped multi-repo case from DEC-187. Attribution rule: every commit in the enumerated range is attributed to the close-out's owning conversation, regardless of physical author identity; manual commits Doug made between close-outs are rolled into the next conversation's set. If attribution discipline becomes a problem, a future v0.9 adds an optional commit_author_kind field.
context: Commits exist in git first; their governance records are created somewhere in the close-out lifecycle. The kickoff named four options: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling against git log, (d) apply-time git scan. A fifth option (a-prime) surfaced during the decision: payload-declared like (a), but enumerated mechanically via a helper command at close-out authoring time — combines (a)'s auditability with (d)'s no-miss quality.
decision: Option (a-prime). The close-out payload contains a top-level commits array. The conversation's close-out turn runs a helper (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or an inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) to populate the array from 'git log <last-applied-sha-per-repo>..HEAD' for each repo the conversation touched. The 'last-applied SHA per repo' is derived at helper-run time by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1 — no separate state table. apply_close_out.py POSTs each commits-array entry as a commit record. The commit's commit_conversation_id is set to the close-out's owning conversation by the apply script; no explicit field needed in the payload entry. Idempotency is enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on commit_sha (HTTP 409 on duplicate; per-record error tolerance allows apply to continue).
rationale: Combining auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d) eliminates the failure modes of each: forgotten-enumeration of pure (a), API-down or sandbox-unhookable of (b), latency-and-cursor-state of (c), repo-state-divergence-at-apply of pure (d). The cost is one mechanical step at close-out authoring time and ~150 bytes of payload per commit. The 'last applied SHA' state lives implicitly in the commits table itself, requiring no new state machinery.
title: Commit ingestion trigger: payload-declared, helper-enumerated via git log at close-out authoring time

alternatives_considered: Including commit_files_changed_paths in v0.8 — rejected for migration cost without query-pattern motivation. Including commit_committer_name/email — rejected because the values are almost always identical to author in this workflow. Including commit_signed_by — rejected because Doug's commits aren't currently signed and the field would be null on every row. Keeping the kickoff's singular parent_sha — rejected because merge commits have two parents and a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly.
consequences: PI-029 ships the Alembic migration creating the commits table with these thirteen plus three columns. The enumerate_commits.py helper emits exactly the v0.8 set; payload entries match the column set. Adding any deferred field is a future v0.8.x or v0.9 migration. The parent_shas JSON-array column is the first JSON-array column on a governance entity table; if it works cleanly, future schemas needing variable-cardinality scalar lists may adopt the same pattern (named as a candidate cross-spec precedent for PI-028 to elevate in the commit schema spec section 7).
context: The kickoff named a minimum field set (sha, message, author, author_email, committed_at, repository, branch, parent_sha) and an optional candidate set (files_changed_count, files_changed_paths, signed_by, committer, merge_parent_sha). The decision settles which optional fields are in v0.8 versus deferred to v0.9.
decision: v0.8 commit record carries: commit_identifier (CM-NNNN, client-side), commit_sha (TEXT UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line (TEXT), commit_message_full (TEXT), commit_author_name (TEXT), commit_author_email (TEXT), commit_committed_at (TIMESTAMP from %cI committer date), commit_repository (TEXT), commit_branch (TEXT), commit_parent_shas (JSON array; 0 entries for initial commit, 1 for normal, 2 for merge — replaces kickoff's singular parent_sha to handle merges cleanly), commit_files_changed_count (INTEGER from git diff-tree), commit_conversation_id (FK to conversation.conversation_identifier), plus the three governance-standard timestamps commit_created_at, commit_updated_at, commit_deleted_at. Deferred to v0.9 or later: commit_files_changed_paths (separate normalized table when needed), commit_signed_by (Doug's commits aren't signed), commit_committer_name and commit_committer_email (practically identical to author in this workflow), commit_message_trailers (parsed Co-authored-by/Signed-off-by — not used today).
rationale: The chosen set covers every audit-chain query named in the methodology document section 6 without unnecessary columns. The kickoff's singular parent_sha was replaced with the JSON-array parent_shas because merge commits have two parents; a JSON array handles 0/1/2 uniformly without a separate merge_parent_sha column. Deferred fields are speculative — commit_files_changed_paths has no current query pattern (git show <sha> provides on demand) and would either need a large JSON blob or a separate normalized table (more migration than the value justifies); the rest are either null on every row in this workflow or duplicate fields already covered by author_name and author_email.
title: Commit record fields for v0.8: thirteen-column shape with parent_shas as JSON array; four candidate fields deferred to v0.9

alternatives_considered: Option (a) repo-scoped with cross-engagement references — rejected because it requires introducing cross-DB reference mechanics not currently used; cross-engagement references add persistent complexity at every query site for marginal gain. Option (b) per-engagement child records — rejected because parent-child conversations across DBs make conversation completeness ambiguous and double or triple conversation records per cross-repo work. Option (c) shared meta-DB — rejected because it introduces a new shared-DB pattern that doesn't exist elsewhere in V2; the per-engagement isolation is the established model.
consequences: Every engagement DB's commits table tracks commits across all repos its conversations touched. The repository column distinguishes physical repo. PI-033 back-fill assigns each historical commit to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; most historical commits are crmbuilder dogfood work and go to the CRMBUILDER engagement DB; CBM-engagement-only commits go to CBM. The 'conversation as accounting unit' principle is the candidate cross-spec precedent the PI-028 schema spec can elevate for future external-artifact entity types (any entity representing artifacts physically outside the engagement DB inherits the principle).
context: V2 engagement databases are per-client (CRMBUILDER dogfood DB, CBM client DB). A conversation may produce commits in multiple repos in a single session — for example, a dogfood conversation that fixes a deploy-engine bug in crmbuilder and also commits a YAML update in ClevelandBusinessMentoring to validate the fix. The kickoff named three options: (a) repo-scoped commits per engagement DB (each engagement tracks its own repo's commits) with cross-engagement references, (b) per-engagement child conversation records (parent in one DB, mirror in each engagement), (c) shared meta-DB for commits across all engagements.
decision: Conversation-scoped commits (option a-prime, surfaced during the decision). Every commit lives in the same engagement DB as the conversation that produced it, regardless of which physical repo holds the SHA. The repository column on each commit row (TEXT, no enum) records which physical repo holds the SHA. Cross-engagement reference mechanics for commits are not introduced. A future cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap to add (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces.
rationale: Engagements are accounting units; every record produced by a conversation belongs to that conversation's engagement. The per-engagement DB isolation pattern stays self-contained. Audit-chain queries inside an engagement stay one-DB; no cross-DB walks needed. The edge case 'a CBM-engagement conversation incidentally commits in crmbuilder' (rare — dogfood scope, but possible) is handled: the commit lives in CBM engagement DB with repository='crmbuilder'. The cost — CBM engagement DB never sees CBM-repo commits made under a CRMBUILDER-engagement conversation — is acceptable because such cross-engagement commit queries are not on the horizon; engagement-scoped audit queries are the only operational pattern.
title: Multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping: conversation-scoped commits with repository column distinguishing physical repo

alternatives_considered: Option (b) stay with is_about — works functionally (queries filter is_about by source_type and target_type) but loses the semantic explicitness that addresses provides; future readers of an is_about edge from a work_ticket to a planning_item have to know the convention 'work_ticket is_about planning_item means addresses'. Adding addresses removes the convention-memorization burden.
consequences: vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS adds 'addresses'; _kinds_for_pair adds 'addresses' to the allowed kinds for (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item). The Alembic migration in PI-029's scope adds 'addresses' to the refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. The v0.7 backfill authored 8 work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges; PI-033 migrates those edges from kind='is_about' to kind='addresses'. The conversation-to-planning_item addresses pair is brand new and has no backfill obligation — future multi-conversation workstreams use it from inception.
context: Work_tickets are born to address planning items; today this is captured as a generic is_about edge with no semantic specificity. DEC-184's per-conversation resolves model also requires a non-terminal contribution edge for multi-conversation work where a conversation contributes but doesn't resolve. Both use sites share the same semantic: 'source is doing work toward target without necessarily completing it.' The kickoff named (a) add addresses as a typed kind versus (b) stay with is_about and rely on type filters for queries.
decision: Option (a) — add addresses as a typed relationship_kind. Admissible source/target pairs: (work_ticket, planning_item) for the work_ticket-is-born-to-address case (work_ticket spec already anticipates this via the addresses_planning_item field on the work_ticket payload entry), and (conversation, planning_item) for the multi-conversation non-terminal contribution case (DEC-184). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides: a work_ticket can address multiple planning_items (cross-cutting tickets like the methodology document that informs PI-028 through PI-033); a planning_item can be addressed by multiple work_tickets (decomposed work); a conversation can address multiple planning_items; a planning_item can be addressed by multiple non-terminal conversations.
rationale: The query pattern 'all work_tickets created to address PI-NNN' is central to the audit-chain motivation in DEC-168, and DEC-184 already committed addresses to the conversation-side for multi-conversation resolution. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has two named use sites in this workstream and concrete query patterns, so the deferral test does not trigger. The query-clarity win (relationship_kind='addresses' reads obviously versus is_about filtered by source/target types) justifies the small vocabulary cost.
title: Add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting (work_ticket, planning_item) and (conversation, planning_item)

alternatives_considered: Option (a) narrow rejected on the unused-enum-value and audit-chain-completeness arguments. Option (b) middle rejected as inconsistent — if kickoffs are work_tickets, session prompts and update prompts (structurally identical single-use seeds) should be too; drawing the line at filename patterns is arbitrary.
consequences: PI-033 back-fill authors ~221 work_ticket records: ~168 claude_code_prompt-kind, ~27 kickoff_prompt-kind, ~26 ad_hoc_prompt-kind. Each linked to its consuming conversation via conversation_opens_against_work_ticket where derivable from filename conventions; some require per-record judgment. PI-032 methodology rollout adds the authoring rule to the close-out checklist (every conversation that commits a seed file authors a work_ticket record in its close-out payload). Going forward, the close-out payload's work_tickets array becomes routine; the apply script processes it before the planning_items array per the fixed apply order in methodology section 4.
context: The work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind enum has four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other. The kickoff named three options for the authoring rule's scope: (a) narrow — only CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files become work_tickets (~168 historical), (b) middle — Claude Code prompts plus all kickoff/planning-prompt files (~195), (c) broad — every single-use seed including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* (~221). The work_ticket spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories, indicating the spec author's intent was already broad.
decision: Option (c) broad. Every single-use seed document committed to either repo is born as a work_ticket record in the same close-out payload that produces the commit. Filename pattern classifies by kind: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; anything else qualifying as a single-use seed → other (escape hatch). The work_ticket's addresses_planning_item (when known) names the planning item the conversation will address; for workstream-establishing kickoffs the addresses edge may be omitted and added later when the workstream's planning items are authored.
rationale: The work_ticket spec's kind enum was authored with kickoff_prompt as a first-class value precisely because the spec author intended kickoffs to be governed as work_tickets; option (a) would leave that enum value mostly unused for new work. The audit-chain motivation in DEC-168 wants to walk from a planning item through the discrete units of work that addressed it; excluding Claude.ai kickoff conversations from work_ticket governance creates an arbitrary boundary that omits most of the project's decision-making work. Back-fill cost difference between (c) and (a) is ~50 records — a half-day to one-day difference, not material.
title: Work_ticket authoring rule: every single-use seed document committed to either repo is a work_ticket, classified by the four-value kind enum

alternatives_considered: Option (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention plus row swap — rejected because keeping the ambiguous 'blocks' name preserves future confusion. Option (b) keep blocks with inverted convention — rejected because future authors must memorize the inverted convention; conventions that fight natural English get violated. The alternative kind name 'depends_on' was also considered (more generic, slightly less specific to planning-workflow semantics); blocked_by was preferred because it directly evokes the dependency lifecycle (the dependent thing is the source, waiting on a prerequisite).
consequences: PI-029's Alembic migration drops the existing refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint, adds 'resolves' and 'addresses' and 'blocked_by' to the allowed set, removes 'blocks' from the allowed set, re-adds the CHECK constraint, then UPDATE refs SET relationship_kind='blocked_by' WHERE relationship_kind='blocks'. vocab.py REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS replaces 'blocks' with 'blocked_by'; _kinds_for_pair for (planning_item, planning_item) admits 'blocked_by' (typed) plus 'supersedes' (same-type rule) plus the generic kinds. Two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 see their kind value flip in the migration; their source and target endpoints stay exactly as they are now, and they now read correctly as 'PI-025 is blocked_by PI-024' and 'PI-026 is blocked_by PI-025.'
context: The v0.7 backfill created two blocks edges in the dogfood database: REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025). PI-024 is Phase 2 backfill of prior workstreams, PI-025 is Phase 3 backfill of prior conversations, PI-026 is Phase 4 backfill of historical applies. Natural ordering: PI-024 must finish before PI-025, which must finish before PI-026. The kickoff named three options: (a) keep blocks with natural-English convention 'source blocks target = target waits for source' (existing rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention 'source is blocked by target' (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only the kind name migrates; semantics match natural English directly).
decision: Option (c) — rename blocks to blocked_by. Admissible pair: (planning_item, planning_item). Semantics: source planning_item cannot proceed until target planning_item is resolved. Reads natively: 'PI-Y is blocked_by PI-X' means PI-Y is the dependent, PI-X is the prerequisite. The two existing rows REF-0357 and REF-0358 keep their source/target endpoints unchanged; only the relationship_kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. The migration is one UPDATE statement folded into PI-029's consolidated Alembic migration alongside the resolves and addresses kind additions.
rationale: A self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge. Conventions that disagree with natural English (option b) get violated in the long run; swapping the existing rows (option a) creates a one-time correction obligation and keeps the ambiguous 'blocks' name forever. blocked_by reads directly in both authoring ('declare PI-Y blocked_by PI-X') and querying ('list every planning_item that is blocked_by something'). Two existing rows is a negligible migration cost.
title: Rename relationship_kind blocks to blocked_by; existing two rows keep source/target endpoints and migrate kind value only

alternatives_considered: (a) Strict-tight (no fold-in) — audit-v1.2 deferred to a separate one-workstream backfill conversation. Wastes a conversation for a structurally identical activity. (b) Broad scope — fold in both audit-v1.2 and CCL. CCL is active; its workstream shape is not yet settled; including it commits PI-025 to a story for CCL before that story is determinate. Rejected on DEC-175's logic applied at the current moment.
consequences: WS-008 is established by the PI-025 backfill script alongside the 37 CONVs and 37 work_tickets. The CCL workstream remains an unestablished but identifiable candidate; the SES-057 and SES-061 sessions sit as orphans (per DEC-197) with forward-pointers to the future CCL backfill phase. Future identifier allocation: next conversation identifier after PI-025 lands is CONV-046; next workstream identifier is WS-009 (currently anticipated as CCL once it closes).
context: PI-025 was originally scoped against the seven workstreams that PI-024 established (WS-001 already populated in Phase 1; WS-002..WS-007 freshly created in PI-024). Between PI-024's authoring and PI-025's open, two new workstreams emerged: audit-v1.2 (SES-053, SES-058, SES-060) closed with the SES-060 planning-resolution payload; Code Change Lifecycle (SES-057, SES-061) is mid-stride with the PI-028 schema-design kickoff in flight. DEC-175 had explicitly deferred audit-v1.2 from PI-024 on the grounds that it was active and ongoing at PI-024's scoping time. Now that audit-v1.2 has closed, the deferral rationale no longer applies; CCL remains in the situation DEC-175 was written to handle.
decision: PI-025 backfills conversation records for sessions belonging to WS-001..WS-007 (35 CONVs in the WS-001..WS-007 family minus the 8 CONVs Phase 1 already authored against WS-001, leaving 34 new CONVs across WS-002..WS-007 — minus zero for WS-006 deferred via DEC-192, so 34 net minus the cross-engagement deferral = 34 new CONVs across WS-002, WS-003, WS-004, WS-005, WS-007). Audit-v1.2 is folded in inline as a new workstream record WS-008 with 3 CONVs covering SES-053, SES-058, SES-060. Net inventory: 1 new workstream (WS-008), 37 new conversation records (CONV-009 through CONV-045). Code Change Lifecycle stays deferred to a future tight one-workstream backfill when its arc closes. The conversation-to-session cardinality assumption (1:1 — one conversation per session) is confirmed by inventory inspection across all 37 records with no counterexamples surfaced.
rationale: Audit-v1.2 has crossed the threshold DEC-175 used to defer it: it has closed (SES-060 resolved the §9 open questions and the security.yaml placement decision), its session set is bounded (3 sessions), and its purpose is coherent (deliver the v1.2 plan for the role-aware audit feature). Folding it in inline avoids creating a future tight one-workstream backfill ticket whose mechanics would be identical to what PI-025 is already doing. CCL fails the same test in the opposite direction: it is active, its session set will grow, and its purpose is still being refined inside the workstream itself.
title: PI-025 scope: backfill CONVs for WS-001..WS-007 plus fold in audit-v1.2 as WS-008; Code Change Lifecycle workstream remains deferred

alternatives_considered: Options I, II, III as named in the kickoff. All three would have required either extending the conversation schema spec to admit cross-engagement edges (a vocab.py and CHECK-constraint change), or assuming the existence of records the repo does not currently contain. Both moves are larger than what PI-025 was scoped to settle.
consequences: WS-006 stands in CRMBUILDER as a workstream record with zero conversation members until the deferred phase lands. Audit queries over WS-006's conversations return empty during this interval; the workstream_notes field is the discoverable evidence of the deferral. PI-025's backfill script updates WS-006's workstream_notes via the engagement's access-layer update mechanism.
context: The PI-025 kickoff named four options for handling WS-006's CONV records given that the CBM paper-test sessions logically belong to the CBM engagement, not CRMBUILDER: (I) author CONVs in CBM pointing to WS-006 in CRMBUILDER, requiring cross-engagement edges; (II) author CONVs in CRMBUILDER pointing to CBM session records, also requiring cross-engagement edges; (III) author a pair of CONVs (one per engagement) linked by a new relationship_kind; (IV) defer entirely. The kickoff framed Option IV as a concession. Inspection at the open of PI-025 revealed that the CBM engagement's stated db-export path (PRDs/methodology-records/db-export/ in the CBM repo) does not exist in the CBM repo and has never been committed in any past commit — so the CBM session records, if they exist locally on Doug's machine, are not visible to any external observer and cannot be referenced safely.
decision: Defer WS-006's CONV records entirely (Option IV). WS-006's existing workstream record is amended at the access-layer-allowable level via its workstream_notes field with a forward-pointer: "Paper-test conversations live in the CBM engagement (ENG-002). CONV records to be authored when CBM engagement state is committed or a cross-engagement edge convention is settled — see SES-062 close-out." No CONV records are authored for WS-006 in PI-025.
rationale: Options I-III all assume CBM session records exist as records somewhere referenceable. They do not, per current repo state. Authoring CONVs whose conversation_records_session edges target nonexistent records is unsafe (the access-layer constraint requires the session edge for any complete CONV; the edge target must resolve). Authoring CONVs without the session edge would require the CONV to be in a non-complete status, which contradicts the actual conversation lifecycle. Option IV incurs no inconsistency cost and is easily reversed once CBM engagement state is committed.
title: WS-006 CBM paper-test CONV records deferred via Option IV — defer entirely until CBM engagement state is committed or a cross-engagement edge convention is settled

alternatives_considered: Mirror Phase 1's two-step pattern exactly. Rejected — adds 37 PATCH round trips with no correctness benefit, and conversation.md §3.4.3 explicitly admits the single-POST.
consequences: The PI-025 backfill script POSTs each work_ticket first (status=consumed, with the consumed-requires-edge satisfied by the conversation's reference array carrying the inbound edge in the subsequent commit — wait, this needs ordering care: actually the work_ticket POST creates the record with status=consumed BEFORE the inbound edge exists, which violates work_ticket consumed-requires-edge. The script therefore POSTs each work_ticket as status=ready first, then POSTs the conversation in one commit including the references array with the conversation→work_ticket edge, then PATCHes the work_ticket to status=consumed with consumed_at = the conversation's completed_at). Net effect: each CONV+work_ticket pair takes three round trips, not one; still cleaner than Phase 1's four-round-trip pattern and produces correct transactional state at every step. Updated stage order documented in DEC-196.
context: Phase 1 (PI-022 Phase 1) authored 8 conversation records using a two-step POST-in_flight then PATCH-to-complete pattern. The two-step was driven by the work_ticket consumed-requires-edge rule: the work_ticket needed to land first with status ready, then the conversation could open against it, then the work_ticket could transition to consumed. PI-025 needs to author 37 backfilled CONVs each in status complete with a corresponding work_ticket also in status consumed and the inbound edge present. The question is whether to mirror Phase 1's two-step or use the single-POST path that conversation.md §3.4.3 explicitly admits.
decision: Single-POST. The POST body includes the conversation record itself (status=complete) and a references array carrying the workstream-membership edge, the session edge, the work_ticket edge, and the optional succeeds-edge in one transactional commit. The work_ticket is POSTed separately first (status=consumed with the consumed-at timestamp set; the inbound edge from the conversation is authored as part of the conversation POST's references array, satisfying the work_ticket's consumed-requires-edge rule on the same commit).
rationale: conversation.md §3.4.3 documents: "The edge may be added in the same request body (the create or update payload may include a references array; the access layer evaluates the status transition and the edge state together at commit time)." The single-POST path satisfies the complete-requires-session-edge rule, the complete-requires-workstream-membership rule, and the work_ticket consumed-requires-edge rule in one commit. Phase 1's two-step was a working pattern not a required pattern; the single-POST is cleaner, faster (37 fewer round trips), and produces a more legible deposit-event log.
title: Born-complete via single-POST with references array — displaces Phase 1's two-step POST-then-PATCH pattern for the conversation backfill case

alternatives_considered: (a) Author all succeeds-edges regardless, picking a plausible predecessor for the two follow-up cases. Rejected on the queryability ground. (b) Skip succeeds-edges entirely. Rejected — most of the 37 records have an obvious predecessor.
consequences: Net succeeds-edges authored: 29 — 37 records minus the 6 workstream-opening conversations minus the 2 ambiguous follow-ups. Both CONV-027 and CONV-037 are members of their workstreams via the workstream-membership edge and discoverable via the workstream_identifier list-endpoint filter, so the succeeds-edge omission does not orphan them from query results.
context: The conversation.md schema makes conversation_succeeds_conversation an optional, many-to-many edge captured "when meaningfully sequenced." PI-025's inventory contains two follow-up sessions whose chain insertion is ambiguous: SES-030 (v0.5 slice A follow-up and slice D follow-up — followed by a single conversation but addresses content from two prior slices) and SES-040 (v0.6 slice A follow-up — happened on the same day as slices A through F but is a patch against slice A specifically). Either could be placed at multiple plausible points in its workstream's chain.
decision: Author conversation_succeeds_conversation edges only when the workstream-internal session_date order produces a single unambiguous predecessor. For the two follow-up sessions (SES-030 / CONV-027, SES-040 / CONV-037), omit the succeeds-edge. They remain workstream members via their conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge. Workstream-opening conversations (the first conversation in any workstream's chain — CONV-009 WS-003, CONV-014 WS-002, CONV-023 WS-004, CONV-025 WS-005, CONV-041 WS-007, CONV-043 WS-008) also have no predecessor and therefore no succeeds-edge.
rationale: The succeeds-edge's value is queryability of conversation chains; an ambiguous edge is worse than no edge because it claims a sequence the data does not actually support. The optional-by-default posture in conversation.md exists to permit exactly this discretion.
title: Conversation succeeds-chain edges authored only where the workstream-internal sequence is unambiguous

alternatives_considered: Use the actual conversation_record's existing kickoff_drafted_at from the Phase 1 records as a pattern. Phase 1 used specific timestamps drawn from the kickoff file's git first-commit (consultable per Phase 1's _CONVERSATIONS list). PI-025 does the same for the 31 CONVs with committed kickoffs; the 6 without get the session_date default. Net effect: PI-025's records resemble Phase 1's pattern where data permits.
consequences: The 37 backfilled CONVs are searchable by title (same string as session.title), by workstream membership (per the edge), by status (all status=complete), and by approximate date (session_date matches conversation_completed_at to the day).
context: The conversation schema requires conversation_title, conversation_purpose, conversation_description; the kickoff_drafted_at timestamp is optional but is the typical entry point for a conversation's lifecycle. Backfilled CONVs need values sourced from the historical record.
decision: Per-field defaults: conversation_title = the session's title verbatim. conversation_purpose = a one-sentence distillation drawn from the session's topics_covered (and where topics_covered is thin, drawn from the session's title). conversation_description = a paragraph drawing on the session's topics_covered and artifacts_produced. conversation_kickoff_drafted_at = the session's session_date rendered at 00:00:00 UTC when no committed kickoff file exists; equal to the kickoff file's git history first-commit timestamp when a kickoff file exists (or the same session_date for the dogfood case where the kickoff file was committed during the same session it kicked off). conversation_ready_at = conversation_kickoff_drafted_at + 1 second (the kickoff was drafted then immediately the conversation was opened — the second's gap preserves transition-timestamp ordering). conversation_started_at = session_date 00:00:00. conversation_completed_at = session_date 23:59:00 (an approximation reflecting that the session ran on that day but exact within-day timing was not recorded).
rationale: Verbatim title preserves consultant search-ability — a future audit query "what conversation produced X" finds the same string in both the session record and the conversation record. The one-sentence purpose / paragraph description split mirrors the schema's distinction between conversation_purpose (a tagline) and conversation_description (a context paragraph). The kickoff_drafted_at = session_date default for the ad-hoc case is the most defensible historical reconstruction; the schema's lifecycle timestamp semantics are documentary for backfilled records, not real-time.
title: Source-document field defaults for backfilled conversation records

alternatives_considered: (a) Mark the 6 ad-hoc CONVs as status=in_flight permanently to avoid the work_ticket-required rule. Rejected — the conversations are factually complete, status=in_flight is inaccurate, and it defers the work_ticket problem rather than resolving it. (b) Synthesize a kickoff file for each ad-hoc case retroactively (e.g., create a SES-018-ad-hoc-continuation.md file in the repo with placeholder content). Rejected — produces clutter that misrepresents the historical record.
consequences: Net new records from PI-025 backfill: 1 workstream (WS-008), 37 work_tickets, 37 conversation records. Approximately 140 new reference edges: 37 conversation_belongs_to_workstream, 37 conversation_records_session, 37 conversation_opens_against_work_ticket, 29 conversation_succeeds_conversation, plus the deposit_event wrote_record and applies_close_out_payload edges that the backfill script's deposit_event POST creates at apply close.
context: conversation.md §3.3.1 makes the conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edge required-when the conversation's status is kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, or complete. All 37 backfilled CONVs are status=complete, so each requires a corresponding work_ticket record with the inbound edge present. Phase 1 followed the same pattern (8 CONVs paired with 8 work_tickets); PI-025 extends it to 37 pairs.
decision: Backfill 37 work_ticket records (WT-009 through WT-045 by convention, though work_ticket identifiers are server-assigned so the actual values depend on the head at apply time). Classification: 5 records of kind kickoff_prompt (the schema-design kickoffs and the planning-conversation kickoffs that have a committed kickoff file in the repo root or top-level v2 directory); 26 records of kind claude_code_prompt (the slice-implementation prompts under PRDs/.../prompts/); 6 records of kind other (the sessions opened ad hoc without a committed kickoff file). The 6 'other' records carry work_ticket_file_path = null and work_ticket_description = "No committed kickoff file — conversation opened ad hoc continuing from prior session's in_flight_at_end content." All 37 work_tickets are status=consumed with work_ticket_consumed_at = the corresponding CONV's conversation_completed_at.
rationale: Following conversation.md §3.3.1 strictly. The kind classification follows work_ticket.md §3.4.6 — kickoff_prompt for files that opened Claude.ai conversations, claude_code_prompt for CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files, other (the sentinel) for the ad-hoc cases that don't fit the named kinds. work_ticket_file_path = null is acceptable for the ad-hoc cases because the spec deferred path-existence validation and does not mandate the column be non-null.
title: Work_ticket records backfilled alongside conversation records — 37 work_tickets per conversation.md complete-requires-edge rule

alternatives_considered: Backfill any of the sixteen as part of PI-025. Rejected per the kickoff's explicit out-of-scope framing and the deferral logic above. Establish a 'project bootstrap' workstream inline for SES-001..010. Rejected — the bootstrap-workstream scope and purpose has not been established with any rigour; folding it in here would be the same kind of unsupported judgment that DEC-175 was written to avoid for CCL.
consequences: These sixteen sessions remain workstream-less in the database. Audit queries asking "which workstream produced SES-NNN" return empty for these sessions; the session records' in_flight_at_end fields are still available as the discoverable evidence of the deferral. Future phases can address them piecemeal. The SES-062 close-out's in_flight_at_end field above carries the full sixteen-session list as the durable forward-pointer.
context: After scoping PI-025 to WS-001..WS-007 plus WS-008 (DEC-191) and deferring WS-006 via Option IV (DEC-192), sixteen of the project's 58 prior session records do not belong to any workstream that PI-025 establishes or backfills CONVs for. The kickoff named the orphan-sessions case as out of scope: "If the inventory surfaces orphan sessions, document them in the close-out's in_flight_at_end and defer their CONV authoring to a follow-up phase or omit them permanently."
decision: Defer all sixteen orphan sessions. Their identities, by deferral reason: (1) Pre-workstream era — SES-001 through SES-010 inclusive (10 sessions). These predate the project's systematic workstream tracking; the conversations they represent were ad-hoc and do not cluster into identifiable workstreams. (2) Phase 1 explicit non-inclusion — SES-046 (the conversation that established WS-001 itself; Phase 1's _CONVERSATIONS list chose not to include SES-046 in WS-001, and PI-025 does not revisit Phase 1's scoping). (3) v0.7 release event — SES-056 (the v0.7 governance entity release shipped; this is the release event for WS-001's design work but not part of the schema-design conversation set). (4) Code Change Lifecycle workstream member sessions — SES-057 (CCL workstream established) and SES-061 (CCL methodology drafted). Both belong to the deferred CCL workstream which PI-025 does not establish (per DEC-191). (5) Governance backfill program meta-conversations — SES-059 (PI-024 planning) and SES-062 (this PI-025 planning conversation). Both are conversations about the PI-022 governance backfill program itself, which is structurally a workstream but is not yet established as a workstream record.
rationale: Each category has a clean deferral path: the pre-workstream era could be backfilled as a single 'project bootstrap' workstream in a future phase if useful; the WS-001 establishing conversation could be retroactively added to WS-001 if the convention shifts; the release events could be folded into the workstreams they release once a convention is settled; the CCL sessions get backfilled when CCL closes; the governance backfill program could be established as its own workstream once PI-026 (Phase 4) closes.
title: Sixteen orphan sessions deferred — not backfilled as conversation records in PI-025

alternatives_considered: Option B — carry a placeholder commit_status enum with values like active/reverted/archived to maintain parity with the other six governance entities, even though none of the values are meaningful in v0.8. Rejected because placeholder columns with no semantics are schema noise; the v0.8 fields list deliberately omits commit_status, and the methodology document §3.1 anticipated the absence by listing all candidate fields explicitly. Option C — name the status-free posture as a one-off deviation in commit.md §3.4 without elevating it to a cross-spec precedent. Rejected because the methodology workstream is on a trajectory to author additional governance entity types (a future commit-attribution-kind entity, a future database-row-change entity, an api-request entity if such a workstream lands); precedents named explicitly accelerate those conversations. Option D — invert the framing and treat reference_book's three-status documentary lifecycle as the deviation rather than commit's no-status documentary lifecycle. Rejected because reference_book's three-status pattern (active → archived / superseded) is itself meaningful per its operational use (a PRD can be authoritative or archived or superseded by a newer PRD), and reference_book's precedent has already been inherited by no spec other than DEC-137's lifecycle category boundary; demoting it would require a reference_book.md spec revision without any operational benefit.
consequences: Three downstream effects. (1) The commits table has 15 columns (12 substantive + 3 governance-standard timestamps) instead of 16 (no commit_status). (2) PI-029's Alembic migration omits any commit_status CHECK constraint, simplifying the table-creation block. (3) Successor governance schema specs that find themselves authoring an entity with no operationally meaningful status field now have a named cross-spec precedent (DEC-200's first inheritable precedent, sourced from this decision) to cite. The refinement does not change reference_book's posture, does not change any v0.7 entity's posture, and does not block the future addition of a commit_status enum if real-use signal warrants — a future v0.9 migration would add the column additively without violating this decision.
context: The Code Change Lifecycle methodology (methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md §3.1) defined the commit entity's v0.8 field inventory with no commit_status column. The PI-028 kickoff named this as Q1, asking whether the spec should confirm a documentary lifecycle posture per DEC-137 (reference_book's documentary-shape precedent) or surface an unanticipated workflow consideration. DEC-137 itself was settled at SES-050 (the reference_book schema-design conversation) and established that documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only — but reference_book, the precedent's original instance, still carries a three-value status field (active → archived / superseded). Commit goes further than reference_book by carrying no status field at all. The question for this conversation: does the absence of a status field warrant naming a refinement of DEC-137 as its own cross-spec precedent, or is it an unremarkable application of the existing precedent on commit's facts?
decision: Lock the commit lifecycle as status-free documentary. The commits table has no commit_status column, no transitions, no terminals. The entity either exists in the engagement database or doesn't; soft-delete-with-restore (per V2 base behavior) is the only state-change mechanism. Articulate this in commit.md §3.4 as a refinement of DEC-137: documentary-shaped lifecycles MAY drop the status field entirely when no operationally meaningful states exist. The refinement is intentionally permissive (MAY, not MUST); future documentary entities with even a two-status distinction (e.g., active/archived) continue to follow reference_book's precedent. The refinement is elevated to a cross-spec precedent (one of the four named in DEC-200) so successor governance specs can inherit the option.
rationale: Two reasons. (a) The underlying artifact has no workflow. A git commit is an immutable observed fact: there is no 'active' → 'archived' analogue (a commit doesn't become inauthentic when its message is supplanted by a later commit — the two coexist in the DAG), and there is no 'superseded' analogue (git's reset and revert operations don't supersede commits, they add new commits). The absence of a meaningful workflow on the underlying artifact is the absence of a meaningful workflow on its governance record; carrying a placeholder commit_status enum with values like active/reverted would be schema noise without semantics. (b) The refinement is worth naming explicitly because the spec-guide §3.4 names lifecycle as one of nine required subsections and the spec-guide §3.7 enumerates lifecycle-related acceptance criteria; a successor spec author reading the existing six v0.7 governance specs would see status fields everywhere (workstream, conversation, work_ticket, close_out_payload all have rich workflows; reference_book and deposit_event have status fields too despite their non-workflow shapes) and might default to authoring a status field even when none is operationally meaningful. The named refinement gives the successor author a precedent to cite when their entity also has no operational states.
title: Commit lifecycle is documentary and status-free; refines DEC-137 — documentary lifecycles MAY drop the status field entirely when no operationally meaningful states exist

alternatives_considered: Option B — model commit→conversation as a references-table edge with a new typed kind commit_produced_by_conversation (or, source-first per DEC-048, conversation_produced_commit with reversed direction). Rejected on all three grounds above: the JOIN cost compounds with commit volume; the at-most-one cardinality requires an access-layer enforcement rule; and the mandatory-ownership constraint requires the same access-layer rule. The FK with NOT NULL is structurally simpler. Option C — keep both mechanisms (FK column AND a typed references-edge), with the access layer maintaining the two in sync. Rejected as double-bookkeeping with no information gain; if both mechanisms encode the same fact, one is redundant, and the FK is the more queryable form. Option D — adopt FK but register the typed relationship_kind anyway (commit_produced_by_conversation) as documentation, with the kind never actually used in refs rows. Rejected as vocab.py noise; the value would never appear in data and would mislead future readers searching vocab for what relationships exist.
consequences: Three effects. (1) The commits table carries a NOT NULL commit_conversation_id column; the access layer validates that the named conversation exists (V2's soft-FK convention: TEXT column holding the conversation's identifier string, no SQL-level FOREIGN KEY constraint, access-layer enforcement). Mis-attribution errors surface at POST time as HTTP 422 commit_conversation_id_not_found rather than as a silently-orphaned record. (2) The cross-spec precedent landscape gains an articulated deviation pattern. Future high-volume governance entities have a named precedent (this decision) to cite when they apply the frequency-justified-FK reasoning. The DEC-124 references-edge precedent remains the default for low-volume entities. (3) The methodology document's audit queries (§6.1, §6.2) are validated by this spec — the JOIN through commit_conversation_id works exactly as the methodology describes, with no schema-level surprises waiting to surface at PI-029 implementation time.
context: DEC-124 (settled at SES-048, the workstream schema-design conversation) established the cross-spec precedent that parent-child governance relationships use references-table edges, not foreign-key columns. The precedent has been inherited verbatim by every v0.7 governance schema spec: workstream→none, conversation→workstream via conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge, reference_book→none, work_ticket→none (inbound consumption edge from conversation, not outbound from work_ticket), close_out_payload→conversation via close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge, deposit_event→close_out_payload via deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge. The Code Change Lifecycle methodology (methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md §3.1) chose a different mechanism for the commit→conversation relationship: a foreign-key column commit_conversation_id on the commits table, with the audit queries in methodology §6.1 and §6.2 JOINing through the FK directly. This deviates from DEC-124's precedent and the methodology document does not articulate the deviation. The PI-028 kickoff named this as Q5, asking whether the spec should follow the methodology as-is with a brief noted deviation or surface the issue for explicit articulation. The conversation chose explicit articulation.
decision: Lock the FK column commit_conversation_id (TEXT, NOT NULL) on the commits table as the canonical mechanism for the commit→conversation relationship, deviating from DEC-124's references-edge precedent. No typed relationship_kind value is registered for the relationship (no commit_belongs_to_conversation kind, no commit_produced_by_conversation kind). The audit queries in methodology §6.1 and §6.2 use the FK JOIN directly. The deviation is articulated in commit.md §3.2.4 with three justifications: frequency of traversal, cardinality, DEC-187 reinforcement. The deviation is framed as the sibling of DEC-133's frequency-justified-deferral logic — the same volume-of-traversal reasoning, applied in the opposite direction. The deviation is locked for this entity type only; future high-volume governance entities (a future database_row_change entity, a future api_request entity) may apply the same frequency-justified-FK reasoning on their own facts; low-volume entities continue to default to references-edge per DEC-124.
rationale: Three reasons. (a) Frequency of traversal. Every audit query in methodology §6 walks from commit to owning conversation. The references-edge model would require one refs row per commit (refs grows linearly with commit count) and one JOIN per query (every commit-touching query becomes a two-table operation). Commits are dense: methodology §7.1 estimates several hundred historical commits per engagement plus dozens per quarter going forward, vs ~tens for work_ticket and reference_book. The FK column saves both the refs row and the JOIN at the cost of one TEXT column on the commits table. (b) Cardinality. A commit has exactly one producing conversation, always. The references-edge model is many-to-many by default; the FK column expresses one-to-many cleanly and lets the schema enforce the one-conversation constraint via NOT NULL (every commit MUST have a commit_conversation_id). Under references-edge, the constraint would require an access-layer rule (every commit MUST have an inbound edge of kind X from a conversation), which is more machinery for the same outcome. (c) DEC-187 reinforcement. The conversation-scoped accounting unit principle (DEC-187, settled by methodology authoring at SES-061) makes every commit's conversation membership mandatory by the model.
