FK enforces mandatory ownership at the schema level via NOT NULL; references-edge does not (a record without an edge is still a valid record absent a separate access-layer rule). The FK is the more correct semantic expression of the mandatory-ownership constraint DEC-187 establishes.
title: Model commit→conversation as a foreign-key column on the commits table, not as a references-table edge; deviation from DEC-124 on frequency-justified-denormalization grounds

alternatives_considered: Option B — elevate only the three precedents the kickoff named, on the grounds that the fourth (status-free documentary) is already captured by DEC-198 and a separate cross-spec listing would be duplicative. Rejected because cross-spec precedents are inheritance signals to successor specs, and a successor spec author reading commit.md §1 should see the full inheritable set in one place. DEC-198 records the architectural decision; DEC-200's (a) line records the inheritability. Option C — elevate only the third (conversation-scoped accounting unit principle), on the grounds that it is the most consequential and the other three are minor patterns that don't need precedent-level naming. Rejected because the other three are not minor in the aggregate — the by-sha lookup is a non-trivial URL-design choice with ambiguous-prefix semantics; the JSON-array-column choice has implications for query patterns (JSON_EACH() vs separate-table JOIN); the status-free refinement is a real choice future specs face. All three are worth naming. Option D — elevate zero new precedents, on the grounds that commit is sufficiently different from the v0.7 governance entities that inheritance is unlikely. Rejected because the Code Change Lifecycle methodology is the start of a longer arc (PI-029 through PI-033 in scope; future commit-attribution-kind, future audit-event-record, future workspace-state-snapshot entities all plausible), and inheritance becomes more useful as the family grows.
consequences: Three effects. (1) The cross-spec precedent landscape grows by four entries. Successor governance schema specs that find themselves authoring an entity with one of the four pattern shapes have a named precedent to cite. The cumulative precedent set after this spec is: DEC-124 (references-edge over FK; deviation in DEC-199), DEC-125 (terminal-states-are-terminal), DEC-126 (per-status timestamps for workflow lifecycles), DEC-133 (typed sequencing introduced when frequency justifies), DEC-137 (documentary lifecycle category; refined by DEC-198), DEC-143 (terminal-state consumption requires inbound edge), DEC-156 (born-terminal append-only), DEC-158 (multi-event-per-target-record), and now DEC-200's four. (2) PI-029's structural foundation slice (Prompt A) implements the schema-level realization of three of the four precedents: status-free table shape (no commit_status column); the JSON column type for commit_parent_shas; the by-sha endpoint stub in vocab.py is not directly relevant to Prompt A but the endpoint pattern is documented in commit.md §3.5 for subsequent slices. The conversation-scoped accounting unit principle does not need a schema-level implementation — it is satisfied by the access layer routing every record to the producing conversation's engagement database, which already happens per the v0.7 multi-tenancy fix (SES-045, DEC-167 et seq.). (3) Future build-planning conversations have a clearer inheritance map. The PI-029 build-planning conversation (not yet held; will follow Prompt A's execution and review) opens against this spec's §3.8 open questions plus DEC-200's four precedents as starting context.
context: The PI-028 kickoff named this as Q2: what (if anything) does the commit spec establish that successor governance schemas should inherit? The kickoff offered three candidate precedents and recommended elevating all three: (a) the <entity>_by-natural-key/{value} lookup endpoint pattern (introduced by GET /commits/by-sha/{sha}), (b) the JSON array column pattern for variable-cardinality scalar lists (introduced by commit_parent_shas), (c) the conversation-scoped accounting unit principle (restating DEC-187 in spec-inheritable form, since this is the first governance entity whose physical artifact lives outside the engagement database). The conversation surfaced a fourth candidate during the spec-drafting phase: the status-free documentary lifecycle refinement of DEC-137 (per DEC-198's standalone decision; the refinement is also a precedent successor specs may inherit). The question for this conversation: which (if any) of the four to elevate.
decision: Elevate all four as named cross-spec precedents in commit.md §1, with each precedent stated as MAY-not-MUST and framed for inheritance with rationale. (a) Status-free documentary lifecycle — documentary lifecycles MAY drop the status field entirely when no operationally meaningful states exist. Refines DEC-137; precedent generalizes to any future immutable-observed-fact entity. Sourced from DEC-198. (b) Natural-key lookup endpoints via GET /{plural}/by-{key}/{value} — the standard endpoint set includes identifier-based lookup at GET /{plural}/{identifier}; the natural-key variant is introduced for entities with a strong natural key distinct from the V2-assigned identifier. Ambiguous prefix returns HTTP 409 with the candidate list. Precedent generalizes to a future file_artifact entity with content-hash natural key, a future network_response entity with response-id natural key, and similar. (c) JSON-array columns for variable-cardinality scalar lists — commit_parent_shas is the first JSON array column on a governance entity table (0/1/2 elements). Precedent generalizes to any 0-to-N scalar list intrinsic to the entity's identity that is read as a unit and doesn't warrant a separate normalized table. Unbounded or queried-by-element lists still warrant separate tables (reference_book → reference_book_versions pattern remains the precedent for those). (d) Conversation-scoped accounting unit principle — restates DEC-187 (settled by methodology authoring at SES-061) in spec-inheritable form. The physical home of an artifact and the governance home of its record are independent; the governance home is always the producing conversation's engagement database; the physical home is captured by an attribute (here, commit_repository). Precedent generalizes to any future governance entity representing an external artifact (a deployed config, a database backup, a third-party service response).
rationale: Two reasons. (a) Successor governance specs benefit from named precedents to inherit. The six v0.7 governance schema specs in PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/ each open with an inheritance block that lists prior precedents; named precedents accelerate successor conversations by giving them well-articulated patterns to apply on their own facts (rather than re-deriving the pattern from scratch). (b) Each of the four named precedents is likely to recur. The by-sha lookup pattern especially: future entities with strong natural keys (content hashes, response IDs, API tokens) will reach for the same endpoint shape. The JSON-array-column pattern is a small enough idea that any future entity needing a bounded scalar list (a list of email addresses, a list of categorical tags) may apply it. The conversation-scoped accounting unit principle is the most consequential structurally — it generalizes the engagement-as-accounting-unit principle to any future entity type representing artifacts that physically live outside the engagement database, of which commit is the first instance.
title: Commit spec establishes four new cross-spec precedents for successor governance schema specs to inherit: status-free documentary lifecycle (refining DEC-137), natural-key lookup endpoints, JSON-array columns for variable-cardinality scalar lists, and conversation-scoped accounting unit principle as a cross-spec precedent (restating DEC-187)

alternatives_considered: Option B: remote-canonical — move the SQLite to a small VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, Fly.io) or migrate to a managed DB (Postgres, Turso). Always-on access from any actor. Costs $5-15/mo. Requires the desktop UI to handle network latency on every read and write, and requires an offline story that does not exist today; the v2 access layer assumes local SQLite. Apply scripts would re-target the remote API (mostly a config change). The async DB-access patterns and connection-pool management add real implementation work. Rejected as premature — the gain (always-on claude.ai access) does not yet justify the desktop-side rework. Option C: hybrid scheduled push of read-only snapshot to cloud — keep canonical DB local, push a daily or per-commit snapshot to a read-only cloud endpoint claude.ai polls. Cheapest and simplest. Rejected because it explicitly does not solve the stated problem: claude.ai would still write out-of-date prompts because the snapshot it reads is itself out of date. The asymmetry between session-time writes and stale reads is precisely the failure mode the user is trying to escape.
consequences: Claude.ai's database access is bounded by laptop online time. When the laptop is asleep or off-network, claude.ai's MCP calls return connection errors and the conversation falls back to whatever it can infer from conversation memory — current behavior, just in the gaps rather than everywhere. The shared-secret header lives in claude.ai's MCP server configuration (custom headers) and on Doug's laptop in an environment variable or config file; rotation is a manual step. Operational footprint adds one persistent service (cloudflared as a launchd job). The MCP server code needs a transport flag and an auth middleware — both small additions, scoped under PI-045. Option B remains available as a future workstream; the apply scripts, MCP tool registrations, and desktop access patterns all stay compatible because the wire format (FastAPI REST + MCP protocol) does not change between Option A and a future Option B.
context: The v2 architectural premise is that a structured database is canonical for all governance artifacts (sessions, decisions, planning items, references, charter, status, risks, topics). Today only the desktop UI and Claude Code reach this database — both run on Doug's laptop against the local SQLite file. Claude.ai cannot reach it, so claude.ai conversations infer state from conversation memory and produce prompts and references that drift from canonical truth. The architectural fork is whether to close the gap by exposing the local database to claude.ai (keeping the laptop as source of truth) or by moving the canonical database to a cloud-hosted store that all three actors reach equally.
decision: Option A — local-canonical SQLite stays on Doug's laptop; expose the MCP server publicly via a tunnel so claude.ai can read and write through it; REST API stays bound to 127.0.0.1; auth via identity-provider gating at the tunnel edge plus a shared-secret header validated by the MCP HTTP transport. The desktop UI, Claude Code, and Claude.ai all read and write the same single SQLite file via the same REST API; Claude.ai's path is just MCP-over-tunnel-to-localhost-API instead of MCP-over-stdio-to-localhost-API.
rationale: Solves the stated problem (claude.ai's data drift) with minimum architectural change. Preserves the desktop UI's local-first behavior — no network latency on CRUD, no offline-mode design needed. The migration to remote-canonical (Option B) is left as a separable later workstream if and when the laptop-online-time bound starts hurting; a few months of operating under Option A will reveal what the real claude.ai read/write patterns are and inform whether the larger migration is justified.
title: V2 remote-access deployment architecture: local-canonical SQLite on Doug's laptop with public MCP via tunnel (Option A) versus remote-canonical cloud migration (Option B)

alternatives_considered: (a) Expose both MCP and REST API — appealing because Claude Code prompts could run from any machine and apply scripts could target the remote API directly. Rejected because Doug's actual Claude Code usage is laptop-local, and the security posture cost is real. (b) Expose REST API only and have claude.ai talk REST through a thin shim — rejected because claude.ai's MCP integration is the supported protocol and the existing MCP tool definitions already provide the right abstraction layer.
consequences: Cloudflare Tunnel ingress has exactly one route (mcp.crmbuilder.com → 127.0.0.1:<mcp_port>). Cloudflare Access has exactly one policy. If a future use case for remote REST API access surfaces (cross-machine apply scripts, remote Claude Code), exposing it is a separate decision and a separate tunnel route — explicitly not bundled here. The MCP server's HTTP transport binds to 127.0.0.1 (so it is reachable from cloudflared but not from anywhere else on the network); cloudflared dials it as an internal connection.
context: DEC-201 commits to exposing the v2 stack to claude.ai through a tunnel. The follow-on question is what gets tunneled. The candidates are (a) MCP server only, with the MCP process speaking to the API over 127.0.0.1 unchanged, or (b) both the MCP server and the REST API, which would let Claude Code prompts and ad-hoc curl run remotely as well.
decision: Expose the MCP server only. The REST API keeps its 127.0.0.1 bind and is reachable only from processes on Doug's laptop. The MCP process speaks to the API over localhost unchanged.
rationale: Narrows the public surface to the MCP protocol contract (44 named tools with declared schemas) instead of the full FastAPI route table (every endpoint, every list query, every error envelope). The MCP layer also provides natural rate-limiting and tool-selection discipline through the tool-call mechanism that bare HTTP would not. Doug currently runs Claude Code only at the laptop, so the marginal value of exposing the REST API (cross-machine Claude Code runs) does not justify the doubled attack surface and doubled auth-policy maintenance.
title: Public exposure scope: MCP server only, REST API stays on localhost

alternatives_considered: Per-engagement deploys — run one tunnel + API + MCP triple per engagement (mcp-crmbuilder.crmbuilder.com, mcp-cbm.crmbuilder.com), let claude.ai pick by registering both URLs in its MCP integration and choosing which one to call. Architecturally cleaner because the engagement is in the URL path and there is no implicit state in current_engagement.json. Rejected because it roughly doubles the operational footprint (two cloudflared services, two API processes, two Access policies, two MCP URLs in claude.ai), and Doug switches engagements rarely enough that marker discipline is not burdensome.
consequences: Known footgun: if Doug switches engagements in the desktop UI mid-conversation (rare but possible — e.g., reaches a stopping point on CBM and flips back to CRMBUILDER while a claude.ai window is still open), claude.ai's reads silently switch to the new engagement. The API process needs either to re-read current_engagement.json on each request (cheap, ~µs file stat) or to detect a marker change and restart itself. Mitigation is in PI-045's scope. The current API behavior of reading the marker once at launch needs to change for the remote deployment to be safe under engagement switching. If the per-engagement model becomes more attractive later (e.g., regular concurrent multi-engagement work), the migration cost is one extra cloudflared tunnel and one extra API process — not architectural.
context: The v2 API is single-engagement per process — cli.py:run_api reads current_engagement.json (written by the desktop UI's Engagements panel) or takes --engagement on launch to pick which engagement's SQLite database it serves. CRMBUILDER (dogfood) and CBM (paper-test client) are separate databases. When claude.ai opens a conversation, it needs to read the right one. The routing question is whether to run one remote MCP that follows whichever engagement is currently active in current_engagement.json (matching how the local desktop API works) or run one remote MCP per engagement (separate tunnel, separate API process, separate claude.ai MCP URL for CRMBUILDER versus CBM).
decision: Single remote MCP, marker-driven. The deployed API + MCP read current_engagement.json the same way the local stack does. When Doug switches engagements in the desktop UI's Engagements panel, the remote MCP's next request serves the new engagement. Claude.ai's MCP integration is registered once with one URL (mcp.crmbuilder.com).
rationale: Matches the existing single-engagement-active workflow with zero new state for Doug to manage — engagement switching already happens in the desktop UI, and the remote MCP becomes another consumer of that switch. Operational footprint stays at one tunnel, one API process, one MCP process, one Access policy. Doug's pattern of working on one engagement at a time across all surfaces (desktop, Claude Code, Claude.ai) means the simpler model fits actual usage.
title: Engagement routing for the remote MCP: marker-driven single deployment versus per-engagement tunnel deploys

alternatives_considered: Tailscale Funnel — no DNS migration needed and Tailscale's ACLs are clean, but ties identity to tailnet membership rather than Google login (cuts off claude.ai web sessions that are not authenticated via tailnet); also gives a {machine}.{tailnet}.ts.net hostname rather than a project-scoped one. Rejected on the auth-model fit. ngrok — free tier has unstable hostnames that change on each restart; paid tier (~$8/mo) for a reserved subdomain; auth via reserved-domain restriction is weaker than Cloudflare Access. Rejected on cost and auth. Raw SSH reverse tunnel from a small VPS — no identity gating at all without additional layers; rejected on auth.
consequences: crmbuilder.com domain administration moves to Cloudflare's DNS console (the domain registrar is unchanged; just the nameservers point to Cloudflare). cloudflared installs as a launchd service on Doug's laptop and reconnects automatically on reboot or network change. The shared secret lives in claude.ai's MCP server custom-header configuration and in an environment variable Doug's local MCP process reads; rotation is a manual edit in both places. If Doug later wants to expose other services under crmbuilder.com (a status page, a docs site), the domain is already on Cloudflare and adding ingress is straightforward.
context: DEC-201 and DEC-202 commit to publicly exposing the MCP server. The implementation choices are tunnel product (Cloudflare Tunnel, Tailscale Funnel, ngrok, raw SSH reverse tunnel) and host (which domain and subdomain). Doug confirmed during the conversation that crmbuilder.com is the right host — it is the project-scoped domain Doug already owns, and the project-scoped framing is clearer than using dougbower.com (his personal domain). The domain is not currently on Cloudflare.
decision: Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com. Cloudflare Access policy gates by identity (Google login) before requests reach the MCP. A shared-secret header (X-CRMBuilder-Secret or similar) is validated by the MCP HTTP transport as a second-layer check in case Access is misconfigured. Prerequisite operational step: move crmbuilder.com nameservers from the current registrar to Cloudflare (free plan, ~15 minutes).
rationale: Cloudflare Tunnel gives a stable hostname free, supports Cloudflare Access identity gating free, and ties cleanly to a domain Doug already owns. The two-layer auth (Access at the edge + shared secret at the MCP) means a misconfiguration at either layer alone does not expose the write surface to the internet. The project-scoped subdomain reads correctly in claude.ai's MCP integration listing and in any future documentation.
title: Tunnel product and host: Cloudflare Tunnel on mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access identity gating

alternatives_considered: (a) Per-request reroute, silent follow. Every API request re-resolves the marker and reroutes settings if it changed. Claude.ai cannot detect the switch from its side — the world it sees just changes from the next call onward. Implementation reality is either calling route_settings_to_engagement() per request (process-global mutation, engine cache reset on every dispatch, racy under concurrency) or refactoring the access layer to take a per-request engagement code (structural change beyond PI-045's scope). Rejected for the reasons named in the rationale: the cost framing in the kickoff was misleading once route_settings_to_engagement was read in full, and the cheap alternative is out of scope. (c) Hybrid considered briefly: detect-and-reroute with a graceful drain of in-flight requests via Starlette lifespan events. Rejected as adding complexity without addressing the underlying split-correctness problem — claude.ai would still write half a decision flow to one engagement and the other half to another within a single conversation. The fail-loud path makes drift a visible event the operator must acknowledge before continuing.
consequences: Operators must remember to restart the API process when switching engagements; if they don't, claude.ai's next request 409s with a structured body naming marker_at_start and marker_now, surfacing the drift visibly rather than silently. Manual restart is `pkill -f crmbuilder-v2-api && crmbuilder-v2-api &` in v1. A desktop-UI 'Restart API' button is a natural follow-up if the manual-restart friction surfaces in actual use — not in scope for PI-045 itself; would be a small slice in a v0.8 UI workstream if surfaced as a planning item. The implementation lives in a new module api/marker_guard.py exporting _MARKER_AT_START, set_marker_at_start(value), get_marker_at_start() (for tests), EXEMPT_PATHS (frozenset of /health, /openapi.json, /docs, /redoc), and EngagementMarkerGuardMiddleware. cli.run_api() calls set_marker_at_start(active_code) after route_settings_to_engagement(active_code) and before uvicorn.run. The middleware is registered in api.main.create_app() before any other middleware so exempt paths short-circuit cleanly. Tests in tests/api/test_marker_guard_middleware.py cover match (200), mismatch (409 with correct body fields), missing marker file (409 with marker_now: null), and exempt-path bypass (200 on /health even under drift). The semantic that the captured value is active_code (which may have come from --engagement flag) rather than a re-read of the marker means a process started with --engagement override stays bound to that engagement; this is the correct behavior for the override pattern.
context: DEC-203 chose marker-driven single deployment for the remote MCP and named the footgun explicitly: if Doug switches engagements in the desktop UI's Engagements panel mid-conversation, claude.ai's reads silently switch with the desktop UI under the current API behavior. The API process today reads current_engagement.json once at launch via runtime.engagement_routing.resolve_active_engagement(), then calls route_settings_to_engagement(active_code) before uvicorn.run() — and that routing call mutates process-global state (os.environ for CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH and CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR, reset_settings_cache(), access_db.reset_engine_cache(), init_meta_db_pool()), not request-scoped state. With the remote MCP exposing this API to claude.ai over Cloudflare Tunnel, the question is what the API should do when the marker on disk diverges from the marker the process was launched against. The two viable options framed under the eight-element consequential decision template were (a) per-request reroute and (b) per-request fail-loud.
decision: Per-request fail-loud. Capture the engagement code that run_api() resolved at process start in a module-level constant _MARKER_AT_START at api/marker_guard.py, set via a helper called from cli.run_api() after route_settings_to_engagement(active_code) succeeds and before uvicorn.run. Register an EngagementMarkerGuardMiddleware in api.main.create_app() that runs before route dispatch. On every non-exempt request, the middleware calls resolve_active_engagement() to read the live marker value, compares to _MARKER_AT_START, and on mismatch returns HTTP 409 with body {"error":"engagement_marker_changed","marker_at_start":"<code>","marker_now":"<code>","action":"restart API to bind to new engagement"} and a WARNING log line naming both values. Exempt paths bypass the guard so operators retain liveness and schema introspection on a stale-marker process: /health, /openapi.json, /docs, /redoc. The captured value is active_code (the resolved engagement code that may have come from --engagement flag or from the marker file at startup), not the marker's current value at each comparison — so a process started with --engagement CRMBUILDER stays bound to CRMBUILDER even if the marker later names CBM. Restart mechanism is manual (pkill + relaunch) in v1; a desktop-UI 'Restart API' button is a natural follow-up if friction surfaces.
rationale: Silent follow is wrong by default for write correctness, and the actually-cheap version of silent follow is a structural refactor of the access layer well beyond PI-045's scope. Per-request reroute as framed in the kickoff (a stat + a JSON read per request) misstates the cost: rerouting actually means calling route_settings_to_engagement on every request, which resets the SQLAlchemy engine cache (defeating connection pooling), mutates process-global env vars, and creates a race window under any concurrent in-flight requests bracketing a marker flip. The cheap path — refactoring the access layer to thread a per-request engagement code through every DB call instead of reading from singleton Settings — is a meaningful architectural change that does not belong in a tunneling workstream. Fail-loud is ~10µs per request (one stat plus one small JSON read warm in the OS page cache after the first request), no global state mutation, no concurrency risk, and forces correctness at the cost of a manual restart on engagement switch. The cost is small for Doug's actual workflow of stretches on one engagement, and the system surfaces drift visibly via the structured 409 response rather than silently splitting writes across databases.
title: Engagement-marker-mid-process handling on the remote v2 API: per-request reroute (silent follow) versus per-request fail-loud (HTTP 409 on drift)

alternatives_considered: Match-Phase-1-precedent — skip references as wrote_record targets. Rejected because the kickoff's literal scope calls for inclusion, the lookup complexity is one-time-trivial, and the audit-graph completeness payoff is permanent. Sub-question — backfill the 8 Phase 1 historical references' wrote_record edges retroactively. Rejected because deposit_event is born-terminal append-only (POST + GET only); records_summary on DEP-001..008 is frozen at references=0 and cannot be amended via PATCH; adding wrote_record edges without amending records_summary would silently break the spec's cross-check invariant.
consequences: PI-026's backfill script gains a reference-resolution stage with forward-then-reverse-then-skip semantics (DEC-208 details). Each DEP record's records_summary.references field carries the resolvable-reference count rather than 0. Total wrote_record edges PI-026 authors increases from 105 (sessions + decisions + planning_items only) to 221 (with references included). The future apply path (apply_close_out.py running real-time) already writes wrote_record edges for references; PI-026's choice aligns the historical-era graph with current behavior for all sessions PI-026 covers. Phase 1's references-orphan pattern stands as a permanent historical asymmetry.
context: The PI-026 kickoff's scope language enumerated four target-type sets for the wrote_record back-edges: "session / decisions / planning_items / references". Phase 1's backfill_governance_phase_1.py deliberately skipped references with the inline comment "skip references — REF-NNNN lookup adds complexity for limited value during backfill" and set records_summary.references=0 on DEP-001..008 to satisfy the spec's count-equals-edge-count cross-check. The kickoff did not flag this precedent gap as a surface-and-settle question; the conversation discovered it during preflight when comparing Phase 1's actual script behavior to the kickoff's literal scope. The decision determines whether PI-026's audit-event graph is complete for references or matches Phase 1's lossy pattern.
decision: PI-026 authors deposit_event_wrote_record edges for resolvable references in each historical close-out payload, in addition to the wrote_record edges for sessions, decisions, and planning_items. The backfill script's reference resolver maps each payload reference entry to its REF-NNNN identifier via the references snapshot, attempting forward-direction match first then reverse-direction match, and authors one wrote_record edge per resolvable reference. records_summary.references on each DEP carries the count of resolvable references (matching the count of wrote_record edges authored, per the spec cross-check).
rationale: The kickoff's literal scope language is the authoritative spec for PI-026. The reference-lookup complexity Phase 1 named as the rationale for its skip is straightforward to discharge: a single pass over the references snapshot builds a lookup table keyed by (source_type, source_id, target_type, target_id, relationship_kind) → REF-NNNN, and resolution becomes O(1) per payload entry. The audit-graph completeness gain is real: after PI-026 lands, the query "which apply created REF-NNNN?" returns the responsible DEP-NNN for 125 of 139 payload references (the 14 unresolvable references are documented as data-quality exceptions in DEC-208's resolution rules and in the in_flight_at_end notes). Phase 1's 8 historical references remain orphan because deposit_event records are immutable; the precedent asymmetry is acknowledged as permanent under the current spec.
title: PI-026 includes references as deposit_event_wrote_record targets, departing from Phase 1's precedent of skipping them

alternatives_considered: Option B — match-to-SES for backfilled records. Two sub-variants considered: (i) admit a -h suffix to the identifier regex (schema change touching the ^COP-\d{3}$ / ^DEP-\d{3}$ constraints, the access-layer validation, and Alembic migrations); (ii) use an offset like COP-101..126 / DEP-101..126 to encode "historical of session NNN" implicitly. Either way required leaving Phase 1's COP-001..008 as the only sequential-numbered exception or retroactively renumbering them (blocked by deposit_event immutability for DEP-001..008). Rejected on schema-stability and minimum-viable-change grounds; the kickoff's flagged default carried.
consequences: PI-026's allocation is locked: COP-009..COP-032 to ses_012, ses_013, ses_014, ses_015, ses_025, ses_026, ses_027, ses_029, ses_030, ses_031..044, ses_046 [excluded — see DEC-210], ses_053 in inventory order; DEP-020..DEP-043 in matching positions. Audit queries that ask "which COP covered SES-NNN?" require a refs/file_path lookup rather than an identifier inspection — acceptable since both the COP and DEP UIs are read-only audit logs where lookup is the normal access pattern. Future historical-backfill work (e.g., the deferred orphan-conversation backfill that would land SES-001 and SES-046's COPs) inherits the same sequential pattern from the next available identifier.
context: The kickoff explicitly flagged identifier allocation as a surface-and-confirm question with two options. Option A continues the sequential numbering Phase 1 used: COP-001..008 are sequential by allocation order, not matched to the session each applies to. Option B would have used match-to-SES numbering for backfilled records, requiring either a schema-level identifier-format change to admit a backfill suffix (e.g., COP-012-h) or an offset numbering range (e.g., COP-101..). The kickoff default was Option A. The decision locks in the convention for any future historical-backfill work.
decision: PI-026 allocates COP-009 through COP-032 (24 records) and DEP-020 through DEP-043 (24 records) sequentially by inventory order against the historical close-out JSONs. The convention reads going forward: "Backfilled COPs and DEPs are sequential by allocation order; real-time records match the session they apply to." Phase 1's COP-001..008 follow the same sequential pattern; real-time records COP-056..064 and DEP-009..017 follow the match-to-SES pattern.
rationale: Matches Phase 1's existing pattern, preserves the schema's ^COP-\d{3}$ and ^DEP-\d{3}$ identifier regexes with no change, avoids retroactive renumbering (which is blocked by deposit_event immutability for the DEP side regardless), and avoids inventing an offset numbering convention that has no precedent. The numbering inconsistency between backfilled (sequential) and real-time (match-to-SES) is factually accurate to the project history.
title: PI-026 continues sequential identifier allocation for backfilled COP and DEP records (Option A), preserving Phase 1's pattern

alternatives_considered: Strict forward-only resolution — would have failed PI-026 by requiring abort on the 3 ses_001 reverse-direction references; rejected. Synthesize-missing-REFs strategy — create new reference records to match what the payload claims; rejected because (a) the references already exist in alternate direction or alternate session for the two affected patterns, (b) creating duplicates would corrupt the snapshot, (c) the payload's claims about ses_030 are factually inconsistent with database state. Per-file-special-case resolution — hardcode the 7 exceptions in the script; rejected as fragile to future similar patterns and as encoding data-quality issues opaquely. The three-tier policy generalizes to any future historical-payload backfill encountering similar issues.
consequences: PI-026 authors 125 reference wrote_record edges in the post-shrink scope. Decomposition: 132 forward-resolvable across all 26 historical close-outs minus 7 forward-resolvable in ses_046 (deferred by DEC-210) = 125 forward-resolvable in scope; plus 3 reverse-resolvable in ses_001 (also deferred by DEC-210) = 0 reverse-resolvable in scope; plus 4 unresolvable in ses_030 that remain in scope and are skipped per DEC-208 contributing 0 wrote_record edges. DEP-028 (for ses_030) carries records_summary.references=0 and zero outbound wrote_record edges for references; all other DEPs PI-026 authors have records_summary.references matching their resolved-reference counts. Skip events log to the script's stdout (and the script names the four skipped references in its console output); the four ses_030 skips are also explicitly named in SES-066's in_flight_at_end.
context: DEC-206's resolution requires the backfill script to map each historical close-out payload's reference entries to their corresponding REF-NNNN identifiers in the references snapshot, so the script can author deposit_event_wrote_record edges pointing at those REFs. During inventory authoring, three patterns emerged: (a) most payload references resolve directly when matched by (source_type, source_id, target_type, target_id, relationship_kind); (b) three payload references in ses_001.json store decision->session decided_in while the snapshot stores them as session->decision decided_in — same conceptual edge, inverse direction; (c) four payload references in ses_030.json don't resolve in either direction because the relevant snapshot edges target SES-036 rather than SES-030. The script needs a deterministic policy for each pattern.
decision: The backfill script's reference resolver applies three steps in order, stopping at the first success: (1) Forward match — look up (source_type, source_id, target_type, target_id, relationship_kind) in the references snapshot; if found, use the corresponding REF-NNNN as the wrote_record edge target. (2) Reverse match — swap source and target and look up again; if found, use that REF-NNNN. Reverse matches are logged with a note distinguishing them from forward matches. (3) Skip — log a data-quality exception with the payload file, the unresolvable reference entry, and the message "reference unresolvable in either direction; skipped"; do not author a wrote_record edge for this entry; do not fail the script. records_summary.references for the affected DEP is decremented to match the actual count of wrote_record edges authored.
rationale: Three-tier resolution captures the actually-observed data patterns without inventing semantics the snapshot doesn't support. Forward match handles the typical case (132 of 139 payload references). Reverse match handles the direction-inversion case (3 references in ses_001.json — the earliest references created in the database, stored by an apply path that wrote decided_in in canonical session->decision direction while the close-out payload format later adopted the decision->session direction). Skip handles cases where no edge exists in either direction (4 references in ses_030.json — the close-out's reference list describes edges that were never actually created in the database in the form the payload claims). Logging skips as data-quality exceptions preserves auditability without forcing the script to abort; the exceptions are documented in SES-066's in_flight_at_end.
title: Reference-resolution strategy for historical close-out payloads: forward-then-reverse-then-skip, with skip events logged as data-quality exceptions

alternatives_considered: Real apply_script_version — attempt to identify the actual version that was running at the historical apply moment by git-log on the close-out file's commit timestamp. Rejected as low-value; the runner field already captures the backfill provenance; spurious precision would imply a level of forensic recoverability the records don't actually support. Use script's invocation command verbatim — "python scripts/backfill_pi_026_historical_applies_deposit_events.py" as the invocation string for all 24 DEPs. Rejected because it loses per-record specificity (every DEP carries the same string); the per-JSON synthesized invocation preserves which payload was applied. Sparse log file content (empty file) — rejected because deposit_event_log_file_path's spec semantics admit an empty file but Phase 1's three-line placeholder provides enough context for a forensic reader to identify the file as a backfill placeholder without consulting external state.
consequences: PI-026 authors 24 deposit_event records with the four conventions above, and writes 24 placeholder log files (dep_018-historical.log through dep_042-historical.log) committed alongside the regenerated db-export snapshot. The conventions are documented for any future historical-backfill work that follows the same pattern (e.g., the deferred orphan-conversation backfill, when it lands).
context: The deposit_event spec section 3.2.6 requires three JSON fields populated on every record: records_summary, apply_context, and (conditionally) error_info. apply_context's required keys are apply_script_version, invocation, and runner. The deposit_event_log_file_path field is also required (non-empty, repo-relative, unique within the engagement). For real-time applies, apply_close_out.py populates these fields automatically. For backfilled applies, the historical apply's actual invocation, script version, and stdout log are not recorded. The kickoff flagged these as surface-and-settle questions with proposed defaults; the conversation confirmed the defaults and locks them in as the PI-026 convention.
decision: PI-026's backfill script populates deposit_event records with: (1) apply_context.apply_script_version = "backfill" — the version that ran the original historical apply is not recorded; the runner field carries the provenance, version doesn't add information. (2) apply_context.invocation = "backfill_pi_026: ../PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json" per close-out JSON. Synthesizes a representative string that names the script and the payload file path. (3) apply_context.runner = "backfill_script". (4) deposit_event_log_file_path follows Phase 1's pattern: "PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/deposit-event-logs/dep_NNN-historical.log" where NNN matches the DEP identifier. Each log file contains three lines: a header naming the DEP, a line noting "This apply ran pre-v0.7 (before deposit_event logging existed).", and a backfill provenance line naming the backfill script and timestamp.
rationale: Each convention follows Phase 1's precedent or the kickoff's default. apply_script_version="backfill" is semantically more precise than picking an arbitrary historical version string. The synthesized invocation string is unique per record (file path varies), human-readable, and self-identifying as backfilled rather than real-time. runner="backfill_script" matches Phase 1 verbatim. The log_file_path convention parallels Phase 1's dep_NNN-historical.log naming, preserves uniqueness (each DEP gets its own log file), and the three-line content matches Phase 1's content shape; the placeholder log file is committed alongside the deposit_event POST per the spec's file-pointer semantics (the path is repo-relative, not validated for existence at write time, but the file is provided for completeness).
title: Apply_context and log_file_path conventions for PI-026 backfilled deposit_event records

alternatives_considered: Expand PI-026 — backfill the 2 missing CONV records inline. Requires creating workstream parents for them (the pre-workstream era has no workstream record; SES-046 is in WS-001's domain but Phase 1 explicitly excluded it). Rejected on scope-creep grounds. Skip the producing-conversation edge for these two COPs — would require either (a) spec amendment to make the edge optional for historical-backfill cases (a substantive cross-spec change rejected on architectural-purity grounds) or (b) hacking the access layer (rejected outright). The deferral path preserves the spec invariants and produces a clean PI-026 deliverable.
consequences: PI-026 authors 24 COPs and 24 DEPs rather than 26. SES-001 and SES-046's close-out JSONs remain on disk but unreferenced by any COP record; both are documented as future-work candidates in SES-066's in_flight_at_end. The full DEC-197 orphan-session list (16 sessions) remains as a future tight follow-up whose planning item is not yet allocated. PI-023 (the workstream-state reconciliation utility that closes the PI-022 program) will report on these orphan close-outs alongside any other invariant violations it surfaces.
context: close_out_payload.md §3.4.5 establishes the production-edge-required-at-all-statuses rule: every close_out_payload record must have exactly one outbound close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge regardless of status (including drafted), enforced at the access layer with HTTP 422 payload_requires_producing_conversation_edge if the edge is absent on POST. The edge cannot be added via PATCH later — the record cannot exist without it. PI-026's original 26-JSON inventory included ses_001.json and ses_046.json. Neither SES-001 nor SES-046 has a corresponding conversation record: both are explicitly in DEC-197's orphan-session list (SES-001..010 "predate systematic workstream tracking"; SES-046 is the WS-001-establishing conversation Phase 1 explicitly left orphan). Backfilling their CONV records in turn requires workstream parents that do not exist for the pre-workstream era. PI-026 must either shrink its scope or expand to include CONV backfill (and workstream creation) for the pre-workstream era.
decision: PI-026's scope shrinks from 26 to 24 close-outs. SES-001's close-out (ses_001.json) and SES-046's close-out (ses_046.json) are deferred alongside DEC-197's existing orphan-session pattern. COP range adjusts to COP-009..COP-032 (24 records); DEP range adjusts to DEP-020..DEP-043 (24 records). Total wrote_record edges decrease to 221 (24 sessions + 63 decisions + 9 planning items + 125 references). The deferred close-outs and their backfill requirements are documented in SES-066's in_flight_at_end as a future tight follow-up that addresses the broader orphan-conversation backfill question for the 16 DEC-197 orphan sessions.
rationale: Aligns with DEC-197's deferral logic — those sessions are already in the orphan list for related reasons, and the close_out_payload spec's producing-conversation requirement is a hard constraint at the access layer. Expanding PI-026's scope to backfill the missing CONV records would expand the scope to include backfilling a pre-workstream-era workstream record (or some other parent shape for those CONVs), which the conversation declined to invent on this conversation's facts. Deferral preserves PI-026's tight planning-item shape (one focused phase of the PI-022 program) and consolidates the broader orphan-conversation question for a future tight follow-up.
title: PI-026 scope shrinks to 24 close-outs after the close_out_payload producing-conversation edge constraint excludes SES-001 and SES-046

alternatives_considered: Option A: ship both /conversations/{id}/commits and /workstreams/{id}/commits. Rejected — the workstream endpoint is speculative for v0.8 and adds two-hop query machinery (or a denormalization migration) for a use case that has not yet appeared operationally. Adding it now is more machinery than the evidence supports. If the workstream-scoped use case emerges, PI-032+ adds it as one additive migration plus one route handler. Option C: ship neither; rely on GET /commits?commit_conversation_id=<id> against the bare list endpoint. Rejected — the FK column was added per DEC-199 specifically because the conversation → commits walk is high-frequency; the discoverable URL is the natural API-surface complement to that storage decision, and shipping the access without the discoverable URL undersells the storage investment. The two options match the spec's §3.8.1 enumeration; no third candidate URL shape was viable.
consequences: Slice B's REST surface includes one new derived endpoint (mounted on the conversations router) plus its three tests (happy path, empty list, 404). The /commits routes themselves carry the bare list endpoint with the ?commit_conversation_id= filter as the underlying mechanism that the derived endpoint delegates to. Cross-entity asymmetry persists until or unless future build-planning conversations add parallel conversation-scoped derived endpoints for work_tickets, close_out_payloads, etc. — the asymmetry is a real signal to API consumers that commit is treated differently because of its FK column and frequency profile. PI-032+ is the natural slot for adding /workstreams/{id}/commits if operational evidence emerges; the deferred-to-build-planning entry in §3.8.1 records the pending decision.
context: commit.md §3.8.1 named two candidate derived endpoints and deferred the URL-shape decision to build-planning: (a) GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits — list every commit produced by a specific conversation, equivalent to GET /commits?commit_conversation_id=<id> against the standard list endpoint but more discoverable as a nested URL; (b) GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits — list every commit produced by any conversation belonging to the workstream, a two-hop query (workstream → conversations → commits) implementable either as a two-step query at the endpoint or via denormalization of workstream linkage onto the commits table. The kickoff's Q1 surfaced this as a real-downstream-impact AND two-viable-options stop-the-flow decision under operating-mode discipline. Methodology §6 audit queries walk commit → conversation routinely; conversation → commits is the reverse walk and equally common at audit time, justifying frequency-of-traversal arguments parallel to DEC-199's FK-column justification. The workstream → commits walk is two-hop, lower-frequency, and no operational audit query has yet been documented at that scope. Cross-entity consistency cuts both ways: the existing six v0.7 governance entities (workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) do NOT have FK-scoped derived endpoints — they expose ?<fk>= filters on their list endpoints and stop there. Shipping /conversations/{id}/commits makes commit the first governance entity with a parent-scoped derived URL, setting a precedent future workstreams will be asked to match for their own dense FK relationships.
decision: Ship Option B — only the one-hop derived endpoint GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits in PI-029 slice B. Defer the two-hop GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits endpoint pending operational evidence that the workstream-scoped query pattern is worth the two-hop machinery or the workstream-denormalization migration. The endpoint accepts standard list-endpoint query parameters (?include_deleted, ?commit_repository, ?sort, ?order, ?limit, ?offset) but locks commit_conversation_id to the path identifier (rejecting any conflicting ?commit_conversation_id= query parameter). The endpoint returns HTTP 404 with code conversation_not_found if the named conversation does not exist (existence checked against soft-deleted conversations too so that commits attributed to a restored conversation can still be listed during administrative correction); returns HTTP 200 with empty array if the conversation exists but produced zero commits. The endpoint is mounted on the conversations router rather than the commits router so the URL path nests naturally under /conversations.
rationale: Frequency-of-traversal justifies the one-hop derived endpoint by the same logic DEC-199 used to justify the FK column (dense entity type, every audit query walks the FK direction, the discoverable URL pays itself back through use). The two-hop workstream endpoint is speculative for v0.8 — no operational audit query has yet been documented at that scope, and adding it now without evidence repeats the pattern DEC-133 was written to discourage (do not introduce typed structure absent frequency justification). The honest cost of asymmetry across governance entities (work_tickets, close_out_payloads do not have parallel conversation-scoped derived endpoints) is acknowledged and accepted; future build-planning conversations may add the parallels if frequency justifies, or may accept the asymmetry permanently. The client-side fan-out cost for workstream-scoped queries (two round trips: one to list the workstream's conversations, one to filter commits by that list) is acceptable for v0.8's expected commit volume of hundreds, not tens of thousands.
title: PI-029 slice B ships only the one-hop derived endpoint GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits; the two-hop GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits variant is deferred

alternatives_considered: Narrow PATCH to the message and authorship fields only, treating parent_shas / files_changed_count / conversation_id as identity-like and requiring soft-delete-and-recreate for their correction. Rejected — those three fields capture observable git metadata or attribution metadata that can be wrong at ingestion (helper bugs, manual-commit attribution mistakes); forcing soft-delete-and-recreate for what is materially the same git object is overkill and conflicts with the spec's stated correction posture in §3.4.3. Lock PATCH to no fields, requiring all corrections to go through PUT. Rejected — the PUT path requires a full-record body, which is more friction than the rare-correction case warrants, and PUT does not change the identity-protection logic (commit_identifier and commit_sha are still rejected on PUT too).
consequences: The _PATCHABLE_FIELDS frozenset in commits.py admits ten fields. The CommitPatchIn Pydantic schema declares ten optional fields and rejects commit_identifier and commit_sha at the request-validation layer (extra='forbid' inherited from _Base). The administrative-correction test surface (DEC-212 tests in test_commit.py) covers patching parent_shas to a new value, patching files_changed_count to a new value, patching conversation_id with FK existence check, and the two rejection paths (commit_identifier and commit_sha patches return 422). Future workstreams correcting historical commit metadata use PATCH as the primary mechanism; the soft-delete-and-recreate path remains documented in the commit.md spec for the rare wrong-SHA case.
context: commit.md §3.4.3 admits PATCH for 'any correction that doesn't change the commit's identity' but does not explicitly enumerate which fields qualify as identity beyond stating that commit_identifier and commit_sha are not updatable. The kickoff's Q3 surfaced commit_parent_shas as an ambiguous case: arguably part of identity (they define the commit's position in the git DAG) but also a candidate for the rare-correction case (mis-ingestion via apply-script bug capturing wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P). The kickoff's Q4 raised the parallel question for commit_files_changed_count and other denormalized fields. The decision determines the patchable field set the access-layer's _PATCHABLE_FIELDS frozenset enforces and the corresponding PatchIn Pydantic schema's optional field list.
decision: The identity-fields-only PATCH restriction stays on commit_identifier and commit_sha. Every other field is updatable via PATCH: commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, and commit_conversation_id. commit_conversation_id updates trigger the FK existence check (the new conversation must exist, even as a soft-deleted record). Attempts to patch commit_identifier or commit_sha return HTTP 422 with field_not_updatable; the corresponding PatchIn schema does NOT include these two fields, so Pydantic also rejects them at the request-validation layer.
rationale: The rare-correction case the spec's PATCH path was designed for is exactly the mis-ingestion scenario where the helper script (PI-030 work) captures the wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P, the wrong file-change count from git diff-tree --name-only -r <sha> | wc -l, or the wrong conversation attribution per methodology §5.6's between-conversations-manual-commit concern. Narrowing PATCH to a smaller field set would force soft-delete-and-recreate for those scenarios, which §3.4.3 explicitly characterizes as 'reserved for the case where the correction changes the entity identity' — and parent SHAs, file counts, and conversation attribution are NOT identity. The DB-level constraint that does protect identity (UNIQUE on commit_sha) holds regardless. The PATCH path remains the lightweight correction mechanism the spec intended; the soft-delete-and-recreate path remains reserved for the genuinely identity-changing case.
title: Every commit field except the identity pair (commit_identifier, commit_sha) is updatable via PATCH for the administrative-correction path

alternatives_considered: (a) Bump minimum prefix length to 7 to match git's --short default. Rejected — the spec settled on 4 and the project's commit volume does not justify the tighter minimum. (b) Reject uppercase input with HTTP 422. Rejected — worse API ergonomic than normalizing. Strict-validation arguments favor rejection, but transparent normalization is the more common API convention for case-insensitive natural keys (git's own behavior, GitHub's SHA URLs, GitLab's SHA URLs). (c) Return V2 identifiers (CM-NNNN list) as candidates instead of or in addition to the SHAs. Rejected for v0.8 — the caller is asking by SHA so SHAs are the symmetric response; identifiers could be added later if a concrete UI use case wants them.
consequences: The access-layer's find_by_sha function returns three return shapes: a dict for unambiguous hits, a list[str] for ambiguous prefixes (router maps to 409), or None for misses (router maps to 404). The function lowercases its sha_or_prefix input parameter before query; the SQL startswith does NOT case-normalize, so the access-layer lowercase is the canonical normalization point. The router-level by-sha endpoint raises FastAPI HTTPException with status_code=409 and detail={code: 'ambiguous_sha_prefix', candidates: [...]} for ambiguous matches, and HTTPException with status_code=404 and detail={code: 'commit_sha_not_found', value: <input>} for misses. The test surface in test_commit.py and test_commit_api.py covers all four cases plus the prefix-too-short rejection plus the uppercase-normalization happy path plus the soft-delete exclusion behavior.
context: commit.md §3.5.1 sketched the GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} endpoint as accepting a full 40-character SHA or a prefix of any length 4+, with four-case behavior (full hit, unambiguous prefix hit, ambiguous prefix → 409, miss → 404). Three sub-questions were left to build-planning: (a) minimum prefix length — confirm 4 hex characters is enough, or bump to 7 to match git's --short default; (b) case normalization — what does the endpoint do if the caller supplies uppercase or mixed case input, given the schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1; (c) ambiguous-prefix 409 response body shape — field name and whether candidates are full SHAs or identifiers or both.
decision: (a) Minimum prefix length is 4 hex characters per the spec's '4+' language. Prefixes of length 1-3 return HTTP 422 with code prefix_too_short. (b) Uppercase or mixed-case input is lowercased before the query rather than rejected with HTTP 422. The schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1; the endpoint transparently normalizes. The same lowercasing applies whether the input is a full 40-char SHA or a 4+ char prefix. (c) The ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses the field name 'candidates' (per the spec's sketch in §3.5.1) carrying full 40-char SHAs only. The response body shape is {"detail": {"code": "ambiguous_sha_prefix", "candidates": ["<sha-1>", "<sha-2>", ...]}}; the candidates list contains every commit_sha value whose lowercase form starts with the lowercased input prefix (excluding soft-deleted unless ?include_deleted=true).
rationale: (a) Git's --short default is 7 but the spec settled on 4, and the conversation did not surface evidence to bump it. 4 is enough to disambiguate in this project's expected commit volume (hundreds, not the tens of thousands where 7-char prefixes become necessary). (b) Rejecting uppercase as HTTP 422 is a worse API ergonomic than transparently normalizing — git's own commands accept both cases, and the schema's lowercase storage is an internal canonicalization choice that should not leak into the API contract. (c) The 'candidates' field name comes directly from the spec's §3.5.1 sketch. Full 40-char SHAs match the entity's natural key (the caller is asking by SHA; returning SHAs is the symmetric response); V2 identifiers could be added to the response body in a future version if a UI use case wants them, but for v0.8 the bare SHA list is sufficient and minimizes payload size.
title: By-sha endpoint specifics: minimum prefix length 4, uppercase input lowercased before query, ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses 'candidates' field with full 40-char SHAs

alternatives_considered: Match V2's apparent default ascending sort across other entity types. Rejected — for commits, descending matches the audit-log nature of the entity and the UI master pane convention; the operational pattern for 'what just happened' queries wants most-recent first. Admit any column for sort with free-form input. Rejected — opens the door to injection of unintended columns and makes column-rename refactors more dangerous; the known-safe subset is the standard V2 posture for sort parameter validation. Lock the sort to commit_committed_at only and disallow user-supplied sort parameters. Rejected — too restrictive for legitimate audit queries that want to sort by identity or by physical home (e.g., 'show me every commit in the crmbuilder repo by identity').
consequences: The access-layer's list_commits function accepts sort and order keyword arguments with defaults commit_committed_at and desc. Sort validation runs at function entry with the _SORTABLE_COLUMNS frozenset; order validation accepts only 'asc' or 'desc'. The router exposes both as query parameters with the same defaults. The test surface in test_commit.py covers the default-descending behavior, the asc reversal, the invalid-column rejection, and the invalid-order rejection. Future workstreams adding new sortable columns extend the _SORTABLE_COLUMNS set in one additive change; the API surface remains backward-compatible because previously-valid sort values continue to validate.
context: commit.md §3.6.2 names the UI master pane's default sort as commit_committed_at descending — the operational pattern for 'what just happened' queries, parallel to deposit_event's deviation from the V2 default ascending sort per the audit-log nature of the entity. The kickoff's Q5 asked whether the list endpoint's default sort and the sort/order query parameter shape should match the V2 list-endpoint pattern. The decision determines the list endpoint's default sort, the accepted query parameter shape, and the safety posture for the sort column input.
decision: API default sort is commit_committed_at descending matching the UI master pane convention. The endpoint accepts ?sort=<column> and ?order=asc|desc per V2's list-endpoint pattern. The sortable column set is locked at the access layer to a known-safe subset: commit_identifier, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at. Invalid sort columns return HTTP 422 with code invalid_sort_column. Invalid order values (anything other than 'asc' or 'desc') return HTTP 422 with code invalid_order. The known-safe-subset approach prevents free-form column injection via the sort parameter while admitting every column an operational query would reasonably want to sort by.
rationale: Matching the UI master pane convention (commit_committed_at descending) keeps the API and UI surfaces consistent — an API consumer running a list query and pasting into the UI master pane gets identical ordering. The known-safe-subset approach is the V2 convention for sort columns; free-form column input is rejected by other list endpoints similarly. The five admitted columns cover the operational query patterns: by audit time (commit_committed_at, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at), by identity (commit_identifier), and by physical home (commit_repository). Columns NOT in the safe subset (commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, commit_conversation_id, commit_deleted_at) are excluded because they are unlikely to drive useful sort orders and because expanding the set after the fact is one additive change.
title: List endpoint default sort is commit_committed_at descending; sortable column set locked to a known-safe subset; ?sort and ?order query parameters per V2 list convention

alternatives_considered:
(1) Schema migration in-line — add "reference" to ENTITY_TYPES, extend the CHECK constraints ck_ref_source_type and ck_ref_target_type via Alembic migration, regenerate RELATIONSHIP_RULES at module load, retry the PI-026 backfill. Rejected at apply time on scope grounds: schema migrations are not part of PI-026's deliverable; the downstream cascade is non-trivial; and Phase 1's reference-orphan pattern is permanent regardless so the asymmetry described in DEC-210's consequences is unavoidable for the historical graph.

(2) Leave DEC-206 active and let PI-023's reconciliation utility surface the contradiction as a finding — equivalent to option γ in the consequential-decision framing of this follow-up close-out. Rejected per Doug's α approval: DEC-206 sitting Active without a supersession edge means audit queries between now and PI-023's run see a fake invariant violation rather than a known-historical-override.

(3) Transition DEC-206 to a hypothetical "Superseded" status. Rejected as a decide-and-announce: the decisions schema does not define such a status; the supersedes edge in references is the canonical signal; introducing a status enum value is scope creep.
consequences:
PI-026's 24 backfilled DEPs carry records_summary.references=0 and zero wrote_record edges for reference targets. Mirrors Phase 1's DEP-001..008 reference-orphan pattern symmetrically — the asymmetry described in DEC-210's consequences (Phase 1 orphan, PI-026 full coverage) no longer applies; both phases skip references uniformly.

PI-023's workstream-state reconciliation utility (to be designed next) must respect supersedes edges when computing "active decisions honored by records." The naive implementation (DEC-X is Active AND records contradict DEC-X → invariant violation) would flag the 24 PI-026 DEPs against DEC-206; the correct implementation traverses supersedes edges first and reports against the latest-in-chain decision (DEC-215 in this case, which the records honor).

PI-046 (the schema-vs-spec contradiction) enters the planning queue. Whichever option PI-046 chooses (add "reference" to ENTITY_TYPES vs remove the speculative admission from _kinds_for_pair) determines whether wrote_record-to-reference edges become POST-able in the future. If PI-046 chooses to admit them, future close-out applies from the real-time path will carry reference-target wrote_record edges; PI-026's historical records remain orphan (immutable). If PI-046 chooses to remove the admission, the spec's wrote_record description in close_out_payload.md and deposit_event.md needs amendment to drop "references" from the target-type list — that's PI-046's call.
context:
DEC-206 prescribed that PI-026 author deposit_event_wrote_record edges with target_type="reference" for the 125 resolvable references across the 24 historical close-outs. The decision's rationale rested on a spec read — close_out_payload.md and deposit_event.md both describe wrote_record as covering "sessions, decisions, planning items, and references." vocab.py's _kinds_for_pair at lines 354-360 admits reference as a valid target_type for source_type="deposit_event" with kind "deposit_event_wrote_record". The decision was authored and applied (in SES-066) without anyone running the backfill script against the API as a smoke test.

At apply time (Step 2 of the orchestrator at commit fb184d5), the first DEP POST that tried to attach a wrote_record edge to a reference target was rejected by the API's validator with a 422 error citing an invalid target_type. The vocab.py contradiction surfaced: ENTITY_TYPES (lines 250-289) does NOT include "reference"; the RELATIONSHIP_RULES dict comprehension at line 379 iterates over ENTITY_TYPES × ENTITY_TYPES, so the ("deposit_event", "reference") key is never generated; the validator's lookup against RELATIONSHIP_RULES fails. The _kinds_for_pair admission is speculative — it admits the kind for the future case where "reference" enters ENTITY_TYPES, but the validator's dict lookup is the binding gate.

A parallel sandbox session investigated, refactored the backfill script to Option I (skip references entirely, mirror Phase 1's pattern, leave the schema-vs-spec contradiction as future work), and ran the backfill (commits 384c224 and 944495e). All 24 PI-026 COPs and 24 DEPs landed; the DEPs carry records_summary.references=0 and zero reference-target wrote_record edges. Total wrote_record edges authored: 96 (24 session + 63 decision + 9 planning_item), not the 221 that DEC-206 prescribed.
decision: Option I is the binding implementation: PI-026's 24 backfilled DEPs do not carry deposit_event_wrote_record edges with target_type="reference". records_summary.references on each PI-026 DEP is 0. The pattern symmetrically mirrors Phase 1's 8 reference-orphan DEPs (DEP-001..008). DEC-215 supersedes DEC-206 via a supersedes edge in references (DEC-215 → DEC-206, kind="supersedes"). DEC-206 itself stays at status="Active" in the decisions table — the supersedes edge is the canonical override signal, not a status field transition. Downstream consumers (audit queries, PI-023's reconciliation utility, future readers) must respect supersedes edges when computing "active decisions honored by records."
rationale: Implementation reality trumps spec aspiration when the schema doesn't admit the spec's prescribed behavior. The cheap path forward — fix the schema-vs-spec contradiction in vocab.py and retry — was rejected at apply time because (a) the fix requires a schema migration plus downstream cascades (CHECK constraint extension, dialog rule updates, possibly status enum additions), all well beyond PI-026's scope; and (b) Phase 1's identical references-orphan pattern is permanent under deposit_event's born-terminal append-only lifecycle anyway, so fixing the schema doesn't unblock retroactive amendment of Phase 1's records. Option I keeps PI-026 in scope, lands its 24 DEPs cleanly, and preserves the Phase 1 reference-orphan pattern as a consistent historical-backfill convention. The schema-vs-spec contradiction is a real bug that deserves a planning item (PI-046, surfaced in this same close-out) but not an in-line fix during PI-026's apply window.
title: Option I (skip references as deposit_event_wrote_record targets, mirror Phase 1's pattern) supersedes DEC-206 after apply-time discovery of a vocab.py schema-vs-spec contradiction

alternatives_considered: (ii) Cite DEC-215 as canonical for both Phase 1 and Option I — rejected because it conflates the supersession trigger with the original-pattern documentation. (iii) Script-and-line-range as canonical — rejected because it breaks the database-record invariant and is fragile to refactors. (iv) Defer the acknowledgement to a separate post-PI-023 conversation — rejected because PI-023's allowlist needs the canonical record to exist at the first reconciliation invocation; deferring would mean either delaying PI-023's first-run acceptance test or shipping reconcile.py with a known-stale allowlist entry.
consequences: DEC-216 establishes the precedent that historical patterns documented only in script comments may be elevated to governance decisions retroactively when a downstream consumer (such as PI-023's allowlist) requires a canonical record. The cost is one DEC per such elevation; the benefit is consistent database-record-as-canonical-acknowledgement across the governance graph. Future governance-data-quality work may surface additional similar patterns (e.g., other historical scripts with inline-comment acknowledgements); each such pattern can follow DEC-216's template. PI-023's reconciliation-allowlist.yaml file carries the `phase-1-references-orphan` entry referencing DEC-216; the entry's drift_pattern matches DEP-001..DEP-008 records_summary.references=0 against 8 of 8 records in range. No retroactive modification to backfill_governance_phase_1.py is required — the script's inline comment remains valid context. The 8 reference records that Phase 1 did not link to their producing DEPs continue to exist in the references table; they are not orphans in the references-table sense, only in the wrote_record-edge sense.
context: Phase 1 of the PI-022 governance-backfill program authored eight deposit_event records (DEP-001..008) representing the historical applies that landed before the deposit_event entity wiring was complete. Each DEP carries records_summary.references=0 by deliberate design — Phase 1 explicitly chose not to author deposit_event_wrote_record edges with target_type="reference" because the reference-lookup work would have been substantial and the deliverable was scoped to records-level wrote_record edges (sessions, decisions, planning_items). The decision was documented inline in backfill_governance_phase_1.py's docstring and never elevated to a governance decision record. PI-023's reconciliation utility (DEC-217 invariant scope Classes 1+2+3) detects this pattern as Class 3 drift unless the allowlist (DEC-218 mechanism) has an entry covering it. DEC-218's allowlist entries are required to carry either a decided_in DEC reference or a planning_item PI reference as the canonical record of acknowledgement. The Phase 1 references-orphan has neither — only the script comment. The conversation surfaced three resolution paths during Q4 settlement: (i) author a new DEC formally acknowledging the Phase 1 pattern, then have the allowlist entry cite it; (ii) cite DEC-215 (which acknowledges the Phase 1 pattern in its rationale text but whose nominal scope is the Option I PI-026 case); (iii) allow the allowlist entry to cite a script-and-line-range as the canonical reference. DEC-216 is the embodiment of resolution (i).
decision: The Phase 1 references-orphan is formally acknowledged as a known data-quality artifact of the v2 governance graph: DEP-001..DEP-008 carry records_summary.references=0 by deliberate design of Phase 1 (backfill_governance_phase_1.py); deposit_event_wrote_record edges with target_type="reference" were not authored for these 8 deposit_event records; the corresponding reference records exist in the references table but are not linked to their producing deposit_event via wrote_record edges. This acknowledgement is binding: the pattern is NOT to be retroactively amended (deposit_event has born-terminal lifecycle; the records are immutable per their access-layer contract). The Phase 1 references-orphan is symmetrically mirrored by the Option I references-orphan (DEP-020..043) per DEC-215. PI-023's reconciliation allowlist carries a `phase-1-references-orphan` entry citing DEC-216 as decided_in; future allowlist entries acknowledging similar patterns may cite DEC-216 as a precedent.
rationale: Resolution path (i) — author a new DEC — is cleanest going forward because every allowlist entry then carries a real database record as its canonical acknowledgement, matching the DEC-218 invariant that the canonical reasoning lives in the database. Path (ii) — cite DEC-215 — works only if DEC-215's rationale is read as scoping both the Option I case (its nominal target) and the Phase 1 precedent (referenced in passing). The allowlist would then carry two entries pointing at the same DEC, which loses the asymmetry that DEC-215 is the supersession trigger for DEC-206 (specifically about Option I) and that the Phase 1 pattern predates the DEC-206/215 sequence entirely. Path (iii) — script-and-line-range as canonical reference — breaks the database-record-as-canonical-acknowledgement invariant and is fragile to script refactors. The cost of path (i) is one extra DEC at PI-023's close; the benefit is a clean database record for every allowlist entry's reasoning, no future ambiguity about scope, and a precedent for surfacing similar historical-pattern acknowledgements as governance records (a pattern likely to recur as additional governance-data-quality artifacts surface across the program).
title: Phase 1 references-orphan formally acknowledged — backfill_governance_phase_1.py's deliberate skip of reference wrote_record edges for DEP-001..008 elevated from inline script comment to governance decision so the reconciliation allowlist can cite a canonical database record

alternatives_considered: Classes 1+2+3+4 — rejected for v1 because the workstream-chain-coherence checks expand the semantic meaning of "drift" beyond what the immediate program experience justifies; orphan-CONV detection is an analytics question, not a drift-detection question. Classes 1+2+3+4+5 — rejected for the additional reason that Class 5's identifier-head continuity largely duplicates schema CHECK constraints and apply-prompt capture; the duplication produces noise without proportionate detection value.
consequences: reconcile.py v1 covers approximately seven findings against the current snapshot, all allowlisted via six allowlist entries (DEC-216 phase-1 orphan; DEC-215 option-i orphan; DEC-210 orphan-session files-without-cop covering ses_001/046; DEC-210 ses-001 unresolved references; PI-047 ses_030 unresolved references; PI-048 ses_056 stale blocks vocabulary). Future drift patterns within Classes 1+2+3 scope are detected immediately at pre-flight. Drift patterns in Classes 4 or 5 are not caught by reconcile.py; the cost of any such miss is bounded by either the conversation's own pre-flight reasoning catching it or by the next subsequent reconciliation invocation surfacing it (once the scope expands).
context: The PI-023 kickoff named five candidate drift classes for the reconciliation utility to check: Class 1 (file vs record presence both directions), Class 2 (record-claims-vs-record-presence), Class 3 (decision-vs-records consistency), Class 4 (workstream chain coherence), Class 5 (identifier-head continuity). The kickoff's proposed default was Classes 1+2+3. The planning conversation surfaced this as Q2 with the eight-element consequential decision template; concrete examples cited the PI-046 vocab contradiction case (covered by Class 3 with supersedes-edge traversal) and the PI-047 ses_030 duplicate-session case (covered by Class 2). Classes 4 and 5 cover analytics-adjacent and structurally-redundant checks respectively.
decision: PI-023's reconciliation utility implements Classes 1, 2, and 3 in v1. Class 1 checks both directions of file ↔ close_out_payload-record presence (every .json file in close-out-payloads/ has a COP record by file_path match; every COP record's file_path resolves to an extant file). Class 2 checks that every record a payload JSON claims it created (session, decisions, planning_items, references) exists in the snapshots with the claimed identifier; reference claims match the 5-tuple (source_type, source_id, target_type, target_id, relationship) against the references snapshot's relationship_kind column. Class 3 is allowlist-driven: each allowlist entry whose drift_pattern matches a record set is reported as EXPECTED; the script traverses supersedes edges so that allowlist entries citing decisions which are themselves the target of a supersedes edge are flagged as stale. Classes 4 (workstream chain coherence) and 5 (identifier-head continuity) are deferred to a future planning item if drift in those classes ever surfaces as a real cost.
rationale: Classes 1+2+3 cover every drift pattern that has actually caused rebases or apply-time discoveries in the PI-022 program's history. The SES-066→SES-068 rebase chain was triggered by a Class 3 case (DEC-206 prescribed reference wrote_record edges that the access layer rejected, with DEC-215 ultimately superseding DEC-206). The PI-047 ses_030/ses_036 duplicate-session case is a Class 2 finding. The PI-046 vocab contradiction surfaces in Class 3 once the allowlist entry citing PI-046 is added. Class 4's workstream-chain-coherence concerns are analytics-adjacent and the cost of underscoping them (a future apply leaves a CONV orphaned and reconcile.py misses it) is bounded by the next conversation's pre-flight catching it some other way. Class 5's identifier-head continuity is mostly already enforced by the schema's CHECK constraints and the apply prompts' pre-and-post-apply identifier-head capture; marginal additional value from reconcile.py implementing it is small. The deferral of Classes 4 and 5 keeps v1 focused; if either becomes a real cost, a separate PI can extend reconcile.py's scope.
title: PI-023 reconciliation utility invariant scope for v1 — Classes 1+2+3 only (file vs record presence; record-claims-vs-record-presence; decision-vs-records consistency with supersedes-edge traversal); Classes 4 and 5 deferred
