alternatives_considered: Option A (hard-coded constants) — rejected because allowlist mutations would conflate with reconcile.py source changes; the allowlist would not be greppable as a standalone artifact; reviewers would need to read Python source to audit it. Option C (database-driven via new relationship kind) — rejected because it requires schema additions disproportionate to v1's needs (new kind in vocab.py, CHECK constraint extension, Alembic migration, dialog rule updates); the database-record-as-canonical-acknowledgement invariant is already preserved in Option B by the required decided_in/planning_item field.
consequences: Adding an allowlist entry is a YAML edit committed to the repo, optionally accompanied by a new DEC or PI if no existing canonical record fits. The allowlist file ships at v1 with six entries covering the seven first-run findings (one entry covers two findings via payload_files list). reconcile.py refuses to run if any allowlist entry references a DEC or PI not in the snapshots (exit code 2), so allowlist edits referencing yet-unapplied governance records fail loudly until the corresponding governance close-out is applied. Future drift_pattern.type additions extend reconcile.py's drift-pattern matching surface; backward compatibility for existing YAML entries is preserved by the type-tagged-dispatch design.
context: The reconciliation utility (DEC-217) detects drift findings and needs a mechanism to distinguish 'drift we have formally accepted and are NOT investigating' from 'drift we have not yet seen.' The PI-023 kickoff named three candidate mechanisms: (A) hard-coded Python constants in reconcile.py, (B) YAML config file in the repo, (C) database-driven via a new relationship kind tagging DECs/PIs. The conversation surfaced this as Q4 with the eight-element consequential decision template. Four artifacts at first run need allowlisting: Phase 1 references-orphan (DEP-001..008 records_summary.references=0); Option I references-orphan (DEP-020..043 records_summary.references=0 per DEC-215); vocab schema-vs-spec contradiction (PI-046); ses_030 duplicate-session 4 unresolvable references (PI-047). The embedded follow-on to Q4 was the Phase 1 acknowledgement DEC-216 — Phase 1's only existing acknowledgement is an inline script comment, not a governance record, and DEC-218's entry-shape requires every entry to cite a real DEC or PI.
decision: The reconciliation allowlist lives at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/reconciliation-allowlist.yaml as a YAML file containing a list of entries. Each entry is a mapping with keys: `name` (string, short slug, unique across the file); `description` (string, one-line human summary); `decided_in` (DEC-NNN string, required if `planning_item` absent); `planning_item` (PI-NNN string, required if `decided_in` absent); `drift_pattern` (mapping with `type` and type-specific keys). The drift_pattern.type field admits three values in v1: `records_summary_field` (matches a deposit_event records_summary subfield against an expected_value across an identifier_range); `unresolved_payload_references` (matches a close-out payload file whose references claims do not all resolve in the references snapshot, with an expected_unresolved_count); `payload_files_without_cop` (matches close-out payload files that have no corresponding close_out_payload record). reconcile.py validates the allowlist at startup: every entry's `decided_in` and `planning_item` identifiers must resolve in the snapshots; every drift_pattern.type must be one of the three known types; configuration errors exit code 2 before any drift checking begins.
rationale: Option B (YAML config file) wins on auditability (a single greppable file at a known location vs constants buried in source vs DB-tagged records the reviewer queries the API for) and entry cost (YAML edit + commit vs code edit + commit vs governance close-out + schema migration). The required `decided_in` or `planning_item` field keeps the canonical reasoning in the database — YAML carries the pattern match, the database carries the why. Option A is cheaper to implement initially but loses the auditability benefit and conflates allowlist mutations with reconcile.py source changes. Option C is most architecturally consistent (everything in the database) but requires schema additions (new relationship kind in vocab.py, CHECK constraint extension, Alembic migration, dialog rule updates) — well beyond PI-023's scope and disproportionate to v1's needs. The four-then-six allowlist entries that first-run requires are easily expressed in the YAML schema; if the allowlist ever grows past 50 entries or develops complex pattern-composition needs, Option C can be revisited.
title: PI-023 reconciliation utility allowlist mechanism — YAML config file at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/reconciliation-allowlist.yaml; each entry carries decided_in (DEC-NNN) or planning_item (PI-NNN) as required canonical-record reference

alternatives_considered: Option A (REST API only, kickoff default) — rejected because it blocks the Claude.ai sandbox pre-flight use case and offers no countervailing benefit under the current auto-refresh architecture. Option C (both, snapshots primary with API cross-check) — rejected as over-engineered for v1; if refresh-hook bugs ever become a real concern, adding the API cross-check as a future iteration is a small follow-on. The decision intentionally chooses snapshots as the read mechanism rather than allowing reconcile.py to fall back between mechanisms; the simpler architecture pays out in audit clarity (one mechanism, one set of failure modes) and makes the snapshots' role as canonical git-tracked state explicit.
consequences: reconcile.py runs in any environment that has the cloned repo on disk: Doug's terminal, the Claude.ai sandbox, a future CI runner. The V2 API can be down, undeployed, or differently-versioned without affecting reconcile.py's output. The snapshot auto-refresh hook in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/engagement.py becomes a load-bearing component for reconcile.py's correctness; a hook regression would surface as silent reconcile.py inaccuracy. Future PI-023.x iterations may add Option C's snapshot-vs-API cross-check as a separate drift class if that risk materializes. The decision does not preclude future read-mechanism additions; it sets snapshots as the v1 primary.
context: The PI-023 kickoff proposed Option A (REST API at http://127.0.0.1:8765) as the default read mechanism for the reconciliation utility, with Option B (db-export JSON snapshots) and Option C (both with cross-check) as alternatives. The kickoff's stated rationale for A was 'the freshness guarantee matters more than the speed cost.' The planning conversation surfaced this as Q3 with the eight-element consequential decision template and recommended overriding the kickoff's default to Option B. The override rationale rested on two grounds: (1) the Claude.ai sandbox where this very planning conversation runs cannot reach the local 127.0.0.1:8765 API — the sandbox is a separate container with an allowed-domains network policy that does not include localhost; Option A would block the Claude.ai pre-flight use case entirely; (2) the snapshots are auto-refreshed transactionally on every API write per the _refresh_snapshot hook in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/engagement.py, so the kickoff's 'stale snapshot' freshness concern does not materialize under the current architecture.
decision: reconcile.py reads database state exclusively from the db-export JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. Six snapshot files are required for Class 1+2+3 scope: sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json, references.json, close_out_payloads.json, deposit_events.json. The path resolution defaults to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ relative to the repository root (auto-detected by walking up from the script's location until a .git directory is found); --db-export PATH overrides the default. A missing snapshot file is itself a configuration error (exit 2). The script makes no HTTP requests; the V2 API may be down without affecting reconcile.py's behavior. The snapshots are treated as the canonical git-tracked record of database state.
rationale: The binding constraint is the use case: PI-023's invocation context includes the Claude.ai sandbox pre-flight, and the sandbox cannot reach the local 127.0.0.1:8765 API. Option A would render reconcile.py unusable for half of its intended invocations. The deeper architectural fit is also stronger for B: reconcile.py's purpose is to detect git-vs-database state drift. The git-tracked artifacts are the close-out payload JSON files plus the db-export snapshots. Reading database state via the API instead of via the snapshots would mean reconcile.py never checks whether the snapshots themselves agree with the database — but the snapshots are the artifact operators reason about in PRs and reviews; an undetected snapshot-vs-database drift would propagate silently to every downstream reader. The auto-refresh hook eliminates the kickoff's 'stale snapshot' concern under normal operation. The single remaining cost is that if the API ever gains a derived/computed field that does not make it into the snapshot, reconcile.py will not reconcile against it — but the snapshot generator is intended to be exhaustive over governance state; a snapshot-missing-an-endpoint-field condition is itself a reportable drift class in a future iteration.
title: PI-023 reconciliation utility database read mechanism — db-export JSON snapshots only, no V2 REST API dependency; overrides the PI-023 kickoff's proposed REST-API default

alternatives_considered: Option B (JSON only) — rejected because terminal skimmability is the dominant case and JSON requires jq or similar to read at terminal; exit code already provides the machine signal. Option C (Markdown only) — rejected because plain text reads as well at terminal and Markdown rendering buys nothing for case 1/2 readers. Option D (--format flag) — deferred but not rejected; adding it post-v1 is a small follow-on once a concrete downstream consumer surfaces.
consequences: reconcile.py's output is human-readable at terminal without post-processing. Apply prompt pre-flight stanzas that wish to programmatically signal 'proceed' or 'halt' do so via the exit code (0 vs 1 vs 2), not by parsing the text body. Future format additions (JSON, Markdown) can be implemented as additional render functions dispatched on a --format flag without disturbing the v1 default. The format decision binds reconcile.py v1's stable interface for downstream consumers; format changes within plain-text (whitespace adjustments, additional summary fields) should be considered a soft compatibility break that operators may notice in apply-prompt logs.
context: The PI-023 kickoff named four candidate output formats: (A) structured plain text, (B) JSON, (C) Markdown, (D) plain text default with --format flag for JSON/Markdown. The kickoff's proposed default was A. The planning conversation surfaced this as Q1 with the eight-element consequential decision template. The use cases identified were: Doug scanning output at his terminal (case 1); Claude.ai pre-flight reading the output and deciding whether to proceed or surface to Doug (case 2); filing as a governance record evidence (case 3, rare). Cases 1 and 2 favor plain text skimmability; case 3 favors Markdown.
decision: reconcile.py emits structured plain text on stdout. The output structure is: (1) header lines reporting which inputs were loaded successfully (snapshots, payloads, allowlist); (2) one block per drift class in order 1, 2, 3, with a class-header line ('Class N (description):    K findings') followed by zero or more finding lines indented by two spaces; (3) a summary line ('Summary: N findings (X allowlisted, Y unallowlisted drift).'); (4) a closing status line ('No unallowlisted drift.' OR 'Unallowlisted drift present; investigate before proceeding.'). Each finding line is severity-prefixed by EXPECTED or DRIFT (padded to 8 columns for column alignment). EXPECTED findings carry the matching allowlist entry name and its canonical record (DEC-NNN or PI-NNN) before the per-finding summary text. DRIFT findings carry only the per-finding summary text. Exit code: 0 if zero unallowlisted drift findings; 1 if any unallowlisted drift; 2 if configuration errors prevented the drift checks from running.
rationale: Plain text serves the dominant use case (pre-flight skimmability) with minimum machinery. Exit code 0/1/2 carries the binary machine signal that downstream tooling and apply-prompt pre-flight stanzas need — JSON output would duplicate that signal without adding value at v1. Markdown's filable-as-governance-evidence advantage is real but rare; if a future PI-046 or PI-047 close-out wants to attach a reconcile.py run as evidence, a small follow-on can either render the same data to Markdown or note that the plain-text output is verbatim-quotable as code-block evidence. Option D's --format flag is the right shape if multiple downstream consumers materialize; deferring it until that demand is concrete avoids paying the cost of two more rendering paths now.
title: PI-023 reconciliation utility output format — structured plain text on stdout, severity-prefixed (EXPECTED/DRIFT), grouped by drift class, with summary line and exit code 0 (no drift) / 1 (unallowlisted drift) / 2 (configuration error)

alternatives_considered: A2: narrow scope — ship only the commits section in PI-030; defer work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items to PI-049, PI-050, PI-051. Rejected because (i) methodology already named all four for PI-030, (ii) the audit chain the methodology motivates depends on all four sections shipping, (iii) three separate architecture conversations to answer the same coupled questions wastes effort, (iv) the kickoff's narrowing didn't articulate a reason. A3: even broader — fold PI-031 (Commits UI) and PI-033 (back-fill) into PI-030. Rejected because the UI and back-fill are genuinely separable downstream concerns and the existing planning item structure (PI-031, PI-033) already captures them.
consequences: PI-030's three slice prompts (A, B, C) carry more total content than the kickoff anticipated — roughly 2x the test count and a fifth section in the payload. The methodology document gets a §4.0 amendment (per DEC-223). The vocab.py change to admit conversation/work_ticket/commit as wrote_record target_types is part of slice B. The first close-out authored after all three slices land can ingest the full machinery including historical-data-aware commit ranges. The PI-024/PI-025 backfill of historical conversation records for SES-001..SES-069 remains a separate concern not addressed by PI-030.
context: The PI-030 kickoff prompt narrowed scope to 'the commits close-out payload section' and named only commits in its goal statement, scope language, and out-of-scope list. Methodology §8, however, assigns PI-030 the full scope: 'Close-out payload schema extensions for the four new sections; apply_close_out.py updates; enumerate_commits.py helper.' The four sections per methodology §4 are commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, and addresses_planning_items. The kickoff's narrowing is silent on whether the other three sections defer to follow-on planning items (PI-049, PI-050, PI-051) or are folded back into PI-030 implicitly. The audit chain methodology §6 names depends on resolves edges landing transactionally with status flips and addresses edges populating governance — neither of which works without those sections shipping. The decision is whether to honor the kickoff's narrowing (defer three sections) or restore the methodology's stated full scope.
decision: PI-030 ships all four methodology-named close-out payload sections (commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items) plus the conversation block surfaced by DEC-223 — five new sections total. The build slicing is three Claude Code prompts: slice A (methodology amendment + resolves status-flip server behavior), slice B (apply_close_out.py extensions for all five new sections), slice C (enumerate_commits.py helper). Slice A precedes slice B; slice C is independent.
rationale: Methodology §8 already committed to this scope, and PI-030's kickoff cites methodology as its spec authority. The narrowing reads as editorial drift rather than a deliberate slicing decision — the kickoff doesn't articulate why three sections would defer or what the follow-on plan would be. Settling the full set in one architecture conversation lets us answer apply-ordering once (methodology §4 specifies session → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items — and amended by DEC-223 to insert conversation between session and work_tickets), whereas three smaller architecture conversations would answer the same question three times. The four sections are tightly coupled: work_tickets reference planning_items, commits depend on conversations that record sessions, resolves and addresses edges flow from conversations to planning_items. Splitting them creates the same architecture surface in pieces with no real benefit.
title: PI-030 scope is the full four-section close-out payload extension per methodology §8, not commits-only — kickoff's narrowing was editorial drift

alternatives_considered: Option B (apply-time discovery, SHA-only manifest): rejected because it introduces a subprocess-to-git dependency in apply_close_out.py with no compensating benefit. The apply script becomes harder to test in isolation, and the payload becomes less audit-friendly. Option C (hybrid SHA-plus-attribution in payload, apply backfills metadata): rejected as functionally equivalent to B for the same reasons — the only meaningful difference from B is one extra field per payload entry (commit_repository), which doesn't justify the architectural cost. A variant of A where the helper is folded into apply_close_out.py with auto-detection of payload format: rejected because folding tools into the apply script bloats it and obscures the separation-of-concerns (the helper is sandbox-side; the apply is local-side).
consequences: The sandbox must have a valid git clone of every repo the conversation produces commits in. For most PI-030-style conversations that's just crmbuilder; cross-repo conversations (methodology rollout, multi-domain CBM work) require the sandbox to clone the other repo too. The helper accepts a --repo-root-override REPO=PATH flag to handle this. Risk: the sandbox sees stale state if Doug pushes a manual commit between sandbox init and close-out emit — mitigated by the helper's mandatory git pull --ff-only before enumeration. Edge case: manual commits Doug never pushes are invisible to either approach. The convention captured in slice C and surfaced to repo CLAUDE.md (during slice A): 'push any manual commits before opening a planning conversation that will close out.'
context: The PI-030 kickoff framed three options for where the helper that turns 'this conversation's commits' into governance records actually runs: (A) emit-time in the sandbox, payload carries full records; (B) apply-time in Doug's local clone, payload carries a SHA-only manifest, apply does git log lookup; (C) hybrid where the sandbox emits SHAs plus the producing conversation attribution and the apply backfills metadata. Methodology §4.1's example payload already shows full commit records, not a SHA-only manifest, partially constraining the choice. The trade-off: emit-time discovery (A) keeps the payload self-describing and the apply simple but requires the sandbox to have a clone of every repo the conversation touched; apply-time discovery (B/C) gets canonical git metadata at the cost of subprocess-to-git dependency in the apply script and a less self-describing payload.
decision: The helper runs emit-time, sandbox-side, at a standalone script crmbuilder-v2/scripts/enumerate_commits.py invoked at close-out authoring time. The script reads db-export/commits.json for per-repo last-ingested SHA, runs git pull --ff-only per repo, enumerates commits via git log with ASCII control-character separators (US/RS) for safe multi-line message parsing, counts files-changed via git diff-tree, and emits a JSON array suitable to paste into the payload's commits section. The payload carries full commit records (SHA + message_first_line + message_full + author_name + author_email + committed_at + repository + branch + parent_shas + files_changed_count) per methodology §4.1's example. apply_close_out.py POSTs each record as-is and adds no git subprocess dependency.
rationale: Three reasons in priority order: (i) methodology §4.1 already specifies the full-record payload shape and this conversation has no mandate to deviate from it; (ii) the apply stays simple and matches the existing pattern (decisions and references are authored at emit time, not looked up at apply time) — the 'separation of concerns' argument the kickoff floated for option C is misapplied because decisions are also 'expensive content the emitter authors' and we don't split decisions; (iii) the payload is self-describing for audit, which matters because anyone reading the payload sees the commit content rather than just an opaque SHA list. The sandbox-visibility concern (the sandbox can't see commits Doug made locally but never pushed) applies equally to options B and C — an unpushed commit is invisible to the API anyway, so the helper missing it at emit time doesn't lose data that would have been findable later.
title: Commit-metadata helper runs emit-time, sandbox-side, with full records in the payload — apply stays a dumb POSTer with no git dependency

alternatives_considered: H2: apply lazy-creates a conversation when commits are present. Rejected because (i) it adds apply-internal magic the payload doesn't describe, breaking the payload-as-spec invariant; (ii) the conversation's title/purpose would have to be synthesized from session fields, producing low-quality metadata that the explicit-author path avoids; (iii) it doesn't help backfill (PI-024/PI-025 still needs to add historical conversations). H3: modify the /sessions POST endpoint to atomically create a paired CONV record + conversation_records_session edge. Rejected because (i) it touches an endpoint with no commits relationship, expanding PI-030 scope; (ii) it doesn't backfill old sessions; (iii) the same machinery has to be added later via PI-024/PI-025 anyway. Defer-and-pre-create: have Doug manually create CONV records before opening planning conversations. Rejected because it puts operational friction at the wrong layer — the close-out is the natural authoring moment for the conversation record, not a separate manual step.
consequences: Methodology document gets one new sub-section §4.0 plus the apply-ordering update and the change-log row — slice A applies this. Every close-out from PI-030 forward includes a conversation block when commits are present. The apply script's section ordering becomes nine entries (session → conversation → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items). The conversation_identifier propagates through the apply run to populate every commit's commit_conversation_id (slice B handles this via a per-section shape function). Historical conversations for SES-001..SES-069 remain absent until PI-024/PI-025 backfill runs; conversation records produced by this PI-030 conversation and later are reliably present.
context: Commits' commit_conversation_id is a hard foreign-key enforced by the access layer's _require_conversation_exists check (a session.get(Conversation, conversation_id) lookup; missing CONV → HTTP 422). The current db-export snapshot has 45 conversation records for 68 sessions, with no conversation records for SES-061 onward — the recent planning-conversation cohort PI-030 belongs to. Methodology §4.1 says commits' conversation_id is 'the close-out's owning conversation; no explicit field needed in the payload entry' but never specifies the mechanism for finding or creating that conversation. Recent close-out payloads (ses_067, ses_068, ses_069) carry no conversation block and no conversation_records_session edge. Without resolution, PI-030's commit ingestion would fail at apply time with conversation_id_not_found.
decision: The close-out payload format gains a top-level conversation block parallel to the existing session block. The conversation POST is one apply step (between session and work_tickets), and it carries embedded references including the conversation_records_session edge to the session in the same atomic POST. Methodology §4 is amended to add a new §4.0 'conversation block' paragraph with JSON example, update the apply-ordering bullet at the top of §4 to insert 'conversation' between 'session' and 'work_tickets', and add a row to the change log dated 05-24-26 documenting the amendment. The conversation block is required for any close-out that includes a commits section; optional when commits are absent. The conversation_identifier is computed client-side via GET /conversations/next-identifier. Backfill of conversation records for SES-001..SES-069 remains PI-024/PI-025 scope.
rationale: Three reasons: (i) the payload-as-spec discipline already established for every other governance entity — decisions are explicit in the payload, references are explicit, planning items are explicit; conversations should be too. The alternative of apply-internal magic (lazy-creating a conversation when commits are seen) introduces implicit machinery the payload doesn't describe, which is harder to audit. (ii) The H1 path doesn't expand PI-030 scope into the session endpoint (H3's drawback) — modifying /sessions to auto-create a paired conversation would touch a stable endpoint with no commits relationship, dragging unrelated concerns into PI-030. (iii) The methodology amendment is small (one new paragraph + one updated line) and the cost per close-out is modest (author writes five extra fields at close-out time).
title: Close-out payload format gains a conversation block; methodology §4 amended to add §4.0 — closes the FK gap between commits and the conversation entity

alternatives_considered: Dedicated endpoint POST /conversations/{conv_id}/resolves/{pi_id}: rejected because (i) the apply script gains a new endpoint to remember without a clear discoverability benefit, (ii) the dedicated endpoint duplicates the same edge-creation logic for one kind, (iii) the dedicated endpoint name conveys 'conversation does the resolving' framing but the same is true of the references endpoint with relationship=resolves. Magic-extension of POST /planning-items/{pi_id} to accept a 'resolved_by_conversation' field that triggers the flip: rejected because it shifts the operation to the wrong entity (the conversation is the actor; the planning item is the object — putting the action on the planning_item endpoint inverts that). Keep the operation as two separate non-atomic POSTs (apply does POST /references then PATCH /planning-items/{pi}): rejected because it violates the methodology's atomic-transaction requirement and leaves a race window.
consequences: Slice A implements the extension as a four-line addition to references.create() (an if-branch invoking planning_items.update after the existing edge insert + flush). The behavior is gated on relationship == 'resolves', so all other reference kinds are unaffected. Tests cover the flip happy path (Open → Resolved), idempotency on already-Resolved targets, duplicate-edge 409 behavior, and the non-resolves no-op. Future extensions following the same pattern (e.g., a hypothetical 'closes' kind that flips a different status) inherit the precedent: extend POST /references for the new magic kind rather than adding dedicated endpoints. The decision is reviewable if more such kinds emerge — at some threshold (probably 3+ magic kinds), a refactor to a generic 'side-effect dispatcher' may be warranted.
context: Methodology §4.3 specifies that resolves_planning_items entries 'generate one conversation → resolves → planning_item edge AND flip the planning item's status from Open to Resolved in the same atomic transaction.' The apply script can't make the operation atomic from outside (two separate POSTs leave a window where the edge exists but the status hasn't flipped); the transaction must be server-side. Two implementation paths: (i) extend the existing POST /references endpoint so that when relationship_kind == 'resolves' and source_type == 'conversation' and target_type == 'planning_item', the access layer also performs planning_items.update(target_id, status='Resolved') in the same DB transaction; (ii) add a dedicated endpoint, e.g., POST /conversations/{conv_id}/resolves/{pi_id}, that does both operations explicitly. The choice affects API surface, discoverability, and the apply script's logic.
decision: POST /references is extended to handle the atomic edge+flip for resolves kind. references.create() detects relationship == 'resolves' after creating the reference row and, in the same transaction, calls planning_items.update(target_id, status='Resolved'). Idempotent on already-Resolved targets (no-op update; reference still created). Source/target validation is delegated to the existing _kinds_for_pair (vocab.py admits resolves only for (conversation, planning_item) pairs, rejecting any other shape with HTTP 422 before this code runs). No new endpoint is added.
rationale: The apply script's logic stays simple: POST /references with the appropriate relationship; the server decides whether the edge alone or edge-plus-flip applies. Adding a dedicated endpoint would have required the apply to know two different endpoints (one for ordinary refs, one for resolves) and to dispatch correctly per section, which is more apply-side complexity for the same outcome. The 'magic' is localized to the resolves kind, which is new to vocab.py and already special — the same vocab block that enables resolves also gates source/target compatibility. The deposit_event POST already follows the same pattern (POST /deposit-events does row + parent edge + wrote_record back-refs + state transition in one transaction); the resolves extension matches that precedent rather than inventing a new one.
title: POST /references extended to atomically flip target planning_item status on resolves kind — single endpoint handles both write paths, no dedicated /resolves endpoint

alternatives_considered: For the programmatic-client path: (a) two separate Access applications under different hostnames (e.g. claude.mcp.crmbuilder.ai for Service Token, admin.mcp.crmbuilder.ai for Google login) — more moving pieces (two DNS records, two ingress rules, two Access apps, two policies) with no functional benefit when there is only one programmatic consumer; (b) drop Cloudflare Access entirely for the hostname and rely solely on the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware — contradicts DEC-204's identity-gating half and reduces the security model from dual-layer to single-layer. For the browser-admin path: (a) bring-your-own Google OAuth credentials — adds a Google Cloud project to Doug's surface, ~15 min initial setup, and ongoing OAuth-app maintenance (rotation, consent-screen updates, possible OAuth verification process) for a low-frequency admin path; (b) any other federated IdP (Microsoft, GitHub, Okta, OneLogin) — same third-party-setup overhead as Google, no compensating benefit.
consequences: claude.ai's MCP integration must be configured with three custom headers (CF-Access-Client-Id, CF-Access-Client-Secret, X-CRMBuilder-Secret) — Step 5 of PI-045 captures this. Rotation surface grows to five touchpoints (the two credential files at ~/.config/crmbuilder/, the password-manager canonical entries, claude.ai's MCP integration's three header values, and the Service Token refresh action in Cloudflare's UI plus the corresponding shared-secret rotation in the launchd/systemd unit's environment for X-CRMBuilder-Secret). The Service Token's Client Secret was exposed in this conversation's transcript during a doubled-header diagnostic — captured in this session's in_flight_at_end as a pre-step-5 hygiene rotation. Cloudflare Access's policy evaluation rule (Service Auth and Bypass policies evaluated first, then Allow policies in order from top to bottom) means policy ordering in the application's policies list is informational only for this application's two-policy configuration. The team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com is visible to anyone who hits the OAuth flow at the no-auth path (path 1 verification observed it); this is normal Cloudflare Access behavior and is not a leak. The CRM Builder MCP application's session duration is set to Cloudflare's default 24 hours; this controls how long an admin browser session remains authenticated after PIN entry, not anything about the Service Token (which has no session in the conventional sense — each request is independently validated against the CF-Access-Client-Id + CF-Access-Client-Secret presented).
context: DEC-204 settled the v2 remote-access architecture's outer security layer as 'Cloudflare Tunnel on a single subdomain under Doug's zone, gated by Cloudflare Access identity gating' — a dual-layer model alongside the inner X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware (from PI-045 slice B). SES-065's decide-and-announce items elaborated the IdP choice as 'Google login (matches Doug's primary identity), single Access policy on the hostname requiring Google auth for Doug's owner email,' built on the implicit assumption that Cloudflare's built-in Google OAuth shortcut was available (no Google Cloud project setup required) and that a single Allow policy with Email+ServiceToken Includes would suffice for both authentication paths. Operational implementation of PI-045 step 4 surfaced two gaps that the planning conversation did not grapple with. First gap: claude.ai's MCP integration is a backend HTTP client with no browser, no cookie store, and no OAuth-redirect-following capability. A Google-login-only Access policy would 302-redirect every claude.ai request to https://crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/login/... which the MCP integration cannot follow; claude.ai would be dead at the Access edge before reaching the tunnel, the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware, or the MCP server. Cloudflare ships a purpose-built primitive for exactly this scenario — Service Tokens, a Client ID + Client Secret pair sent via CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret request headers — which Access validates without redirects. Second gap: Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut. The Google IdP setup form now requires bring-your-own Google Cloud OAuth credentials (Google Cloud project, OAuth consent screen, OAuth Client ID + Secret); the 'leave Client ID and Client Secret blank for built-in' path described in older Cloudflare documentation no longer applies. This materially changes the cost-benefit of Google vs One-time PIN for the browser-admin path: where SES-065 framed Google as 'zero setup' it is now '~15 min initial setup plus ongoing Google Cloud project maintenance' for a single-user low-frequency admin path.
decision: Cloudflare Access protecting mcp.crmbuilder.ai uses TWO policies attached to the same Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP'. Policy 1 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' has Action=Service Auth and a single Include rule Selector=Service Token Value=claude-ai-mcp; this is the programmatic-client path that claude.ai exercises. Policy 2 'Allow admin' has Action=Allow and a single Include rule Selector=Emails Value=admin@crmbuilder.ai over the One-time PIN identity provider (Cloudflare emails a 6-digit PIN to admin@crmbuilder.ai which the browser admin pastes back); this is the manual browser-admin path Doug exercises for ad-hoc inspection. Cloudflare Access evaluates Service Auth policies first and Allow policies afterward per Cloudflare's documented semantics, so policy order is informational only. claude.ai's MCP integration sends three custom headers per request: CF-Access-Client-Id (the Service Token's Client ID), CF-Access-Client-Secret (the Service Token's Client Secret), and X-CRMBuilder-Secret (the inner shared-secret from SES-065 slice B). Service Token credentials are stored at ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id and ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-secret (mode 0600 each, no trailing newline, no header-name prefix); canonical copies live in Doug's password manager out-of-band; rotation is a manual five-place edit (the two credential files, the canonical password-manager entries, claude.ai's MCP integration's custom header configuration for both CF-Access-* headers, and the Service Token refresh action in Cloudflare's UI).
rationale: Preserves DEC-204's dual-layer model verbatim — Cloudflare Access remains the outer authentication gate; the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware remains the inner application gate; together they constitute the dual layer DEC-204 named. Service Tokens are Cloudflare's purpose-built primitive for programmatic clients behind Access, removing the need for any workaround. One-time PIN preserves the spirit of SES-065's 'authenticate Doug for browser admin' goal while eliminating Google Cloud project setup and ongoing maintenance for a low-frequency single-user path; the per-login PIN-check friction (email arrives, copy PIN, paste) is acceptable for the actual usage pattern (occasional ad-hoc browser admin, not daily logins).
title: Cloudflare Access auth model revisions for PI-045: Service Token added for programmatic clients; One-time PIN replaces Google login for browser admin (Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut)

alternatives_considered: Path B (use Cloudflare Access as OAuth IdP — claude.ai's OAuth fields point at Cloudflare's OAuth endpoints; we serve only Protected Resource Metadata pointing at Cloudflare) is held open as a timeboxed feasibility check before committing to Path A; less code if it works, unknown whether Cloudflare's OAuth implementation satisfies the MCP spec exactly. Path C (defer claude.ai integration entirely, keep header-based auth, ship Claude Code + desktop UI only) was rejected — defeats the cut/paste-elimination purpose of PI-045. Path D (add an external OAuth provider like Auth0, Stytch, WorkOS, Logto) was considered but adds an external dependency and likely a recurring cost for a single-user system.
consequences: PI-045 step 5 is blocked until PI-049 lands (captured as a PI-045 -[blocked_by]-> PI-049 reference). SES-072's slice B (X-CRMBuilder-Secret SharedSecretMiddleware) and the mcp-secret persistence (file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret) become obsolete on PI-049 Phase 3 cleanup. The Service Token rotation completed in this conversation was wasted work for the claude.ai path but not damaging — rotation is good hygiene regardless, and the Service Token stays in place for curl tests and defense-in-depth. The Cloudflare Tunnel + Access infrastructure (SES-071's deployment) remains useful; the Service Token specifically becomes vestigial once claude.ai goes through OAuth, but stays for admin paths. The slice C marker-drift guard from SES-072 is engagement-agnostic and orthogonal to auth — stays in place unchanged. Lesson — verify the claude.ai connector UI's auth model BEFORE designing backend auth that has to integrate with it. This belongs in PI-049's close-out as a methodology feedback entry, and in Claude Code's user memory as a feedback memory for future architecture-before-integration work.
context: PI-045 step 5 reached the claude.ai connector UI on 2026-05-25 and discovered that claude.ai's 'Add custom connector' form supports only OAuth 2.1 (Client ID + Client Secret in Advanced Settings) — no custom HTTP header configuration. The existing auth architecture (DEC-204 dual-layer + DEC-225 Service Token + X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware from SES-072 slice B) cannot be configured into claude.ai's UI. Per Anthropic's connector documentation and the MCP 2.1 spec, claude.ai's connector model is OAuth 2.1 authorization_code with PKCE; the Advanced Settings OAuth Client ID/Secret fields are for the 'Anthropic-held credentials' registration mode. DEC-225's premise that claude.ai's MCP integration cannot follow OAuth redirects was either time-bound (true at SES-065 authoring, now superseded by Anthropic's connector evolution) or scope-bound (true for some MCP transport variant but not the connector path). GitHub issue #112 (anthropics/claude-ai-mcp, 'Cannot configure Authorization: Bearer for custom remote MCP') confirms custom HTTP header auth is not supported and is an open feature request without a committed timeline. End-to-end auth verified working in this conversation prior to discovery — the Service Token rotation completed cleanly, the missing mcp-secret file was generated and persisted (SES-072's setup had set the value as an ephemeral env var only), and a curl test through the full stack returned HTTP 404 from the MCP transport (Cloudflare Access accepted the new Service Token, tunnel routed, SharedSecretMiddleware accepted the header, MCP transport answered with 404 for GET / — all four layers cooperated). The dead end is at the registration step, not anywhere in the auth chain we built.
decision: Replace the X-CRMBuilder-Secret SharedSecretMiddleware (added in SES-072 slice B) with an OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation on the v2 MCP HTTP server. Concretely: (a) serve Protected Resource Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource (RFC 9728) declaring the authorization server URI, supported scopes, and bearer token methods; (b) implement the authorization server endpoints (/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server, /authorize, /token, optionally /register for Dynamic Client Registration) — OR delegate to an external authorization server if Path B (Cloudflare Access as OIDC IdP) is feasible; (c) bearer-token validation middleware on the MCP routes; (d) token issuance via authorization_code grant with PKCE verification. The Cloudflare Access edge (Service Token policy + One-time PIN admin policy from DEC-225) remains in place for non-claude.ai access and for defense-in-depth. The decision between Path A (implement OAuth ourselves) and Path B (delegate to Cloudflare Access) is timeboxed at the start of PI-049's conversation: up to 60 minutes investigating Path B feasibility (does Cloudflare Access serve MCP-spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata? does claude.ai accept its issuer/audience values?) before committing to Path A. Path C (defer claude.ai integration entirely, keep header-based auth, use Claude Code only) was rejected as impractical — the cut/paste friction between Claude Code and claude.ai is what PI-045 was created to eliminate.
rationale: claude.ai's connector UI is the gating constraint, and it speaks OAuth 2.1 only. No amount of server-side header-based auth makes the connector usable. OAuth is also more MCP-spec-compliant — Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) and OAuth 2.1 with PKCE are the MCP spec's prescribed model for remote authorization. The shared-secret + Service Token architecture from DEC-204/225 was a useful intermediate state that proved out Cloudflare Tunnel + Cloudflare Access for the remote-access deliverable, and the Cloudflare Access edge stays in place. The middleware swap (X-CRMBuilder-Secret → bearer-token) is a discrete code change, not an architectural reset.
title: v2 MCP server auth model: replace shared-secret + Cloudflare Service Token with OAuth 2.1 + PKCE to enable claude.ai connector registration

alternatives_considered: B — Planner conversation defers its own close until builders finish. C — Each Claude Code session emits its own minimal build-receipt payload. D — First subsequent planning conversation absorbs the prior build. All rejected. Details in topics_covered (1) and in the Q1 discussion of the kickoff prompt.
consequences: Every multi-slice planning item executed via Claude Code gets a dedicated build-closure conversation. Total conversation count for such planning items: 1 planner + N executors + 1 closer = N+2. Build closure's commits section enumerates the executor SHAs; commit_conversation_id propagates to the build-closure CONV. Executor sessions themselves don't get CONV records (no governance hooks at Doug's terminal). PI-033's Phase 3 backfill ratifies the same convention for historical commits. The build closure's conversation_purpose opens with 'Build closure for SES-NNN — ' per DEC-236 to make the pattern grep-discoverable.
context: PI-030's three slices were planned at SES-070 (a Claude.ai architecture conversation) and executed across three Claude Code sessions at Doug's terminal (one per slice). The executor sessions produced code commits but no governance records — Claude Code doesn't author payloads. SES-070 is closed and immutable. The methodology §3 schema requires every commit to carry a commit_conversation_id FK to a CONV record. No CONV record exists for any of the three executor sessions. Without a clean answer to which CONV owns the three slice SHAs, the methodology has a structural gap in its first real use — and the gap reappears for every planning item that ships across multiple Claude Code sessions, which is most of them going forward.
decision: Build closure conversations are a recognized conversation pattern. A build closure is a Claude.ai conversation that opens after the Claude Code executors are done; its job is to author the close-out payload that ingests the executor commits via the commits section (commit_conversation_id set to the build closure's CONV), flip the originating planning item to Resolved via resolves_planning_items, and record any methodology drift the executors surfaced. The build closure does not author the code; it authors the governance record of the code. Methodology gains a new §10 establishing the pattern, with §5.3 (attribution) and §5.7 (multi-conversation work) cross-referencing §10.
rationale: The alternative patterns each fail on at least one structural axis: deferring the planner's close until executors finish (option B) forces planning conversations to sit open through unbounded execution windows and was non-starter retroactively for SES-070 anyway; per-Claude-Code-session build receipts (option C) triples the payload count per multi-slice planning item, requires Claude Code conventions to change, and produces decision-less payloads that exercise none of the close-out machinery; absorbing the prior build into the next planning conversation (option D) conflates planning and closure and creates false attribution chains (PI-031's commits panel UI work would have ended up owning PI-030's commits). The build-closure pattern is a small naming addition that codifies what THIS conversation is doing in practice and sets the convention going forward.
title: Build closure conversation type recognized as the governance owner of code-change work that crosses the Claude.ai → Claude Code planner-executor boundary

alternatives_considered: F — Seed commits.json with a hand-authored baseline row, then run the helper (violates snapshot-is-generated invariant; still hits the parallel-workstream problem). G — Extend helper with --since-sha flag, the kickoff's default (doesn't help: parallel commits sit within the range). G' — Extend helper with --commits SHA1,SHA2 explicit-list flag in this conversation (real cost; scope creep on a structurally complete workstream). H — Defer this closure until PI-033 backfill runs (blocks PI-030 on PI-033, same anti-pattern Q3-H rejected). All rejected.
consequences: First-time commit ingestion produces three commit records (CM-0001, CM-0002, CM-0003 — server-assigned client-side at apply time per slice B's _record_target_id implementation) attributed to CONV-046. Helper evolution falls to PI-050 (status Open) which any subsequent conversation can claim. PI-033's backfill scope quietly inherits the same constraint: historical SHAs get attributed to their authoring conversations rather than rolled into closer-in-time closures.
context: PI-030's three slice SHAs need ingestion as the first-ever commit records in the CRMBUILDER engagement (commits.json snapshot absent at this conversation's open — confirmed during pre-work). The slice C helper enumerate_commits.py's bootstrap behavior is to set log_range = HEAD and emit every commit in repo history (~900). The kickoff's default fix proposed adding a --since-sha flag and invoking with the parent of slice A. Pre-work discovered this doesn't work for THIS closure: between slice-A's parent and current HEAD, the main-branch log contains 8 commits NOT owned by PI-030 (audit-v1.2 Prompts G/H/I, PI-045 code-changes apply, PI-045 step-5 operational kickoff). The helper's since..HEAD range model assumes a single active workstream per repo; parallel workstreams break it.
decision: For this closure: commits section carries exactly the three slice SHAs (70d88e6, 2b5557d, c6ff67a) hand-curated from git log in chronological order. The slice C helper enumerate_commits.py is not invoked. For the helper's evolution: a new planning item PI-050 (status Open) is surfaced — 'Extend enumerate_commits.py with explicit-list mode for parallel-workstream closures.' Methodology §5.5 gains a clarification that closures whose commits interleave with parallel workstreams enumerate their owned SHAs explicitly rather than relying on the range-based helper.
rationale: Three SHAs hand-curated is trivial (about five minutes of git-log queries). Slice-D-equivalent work to extend the helper now would add scope to a workstream that's structurally complete after slices A/B/C and offers no value to this particular closure (the parallel-workstream finding means even the extended helper wouldn't have produced the right output without further filtering). Surfacing helper evolution as PI-050 defers the actual extension until the next conversation that needs it — most likely PI-033's backfill, which confronts the same parallel-workstream problem at much larger scale and may subsume the PI.
title: Commit-array scoping for build closures uses explicit per-SHA curation when commit ranges interleave with parallel workstreams; enumerate_commits.py extension surfaced as PI-050

alternatives_considered: J — Resolve all four (PI-027/028/029/030) in this closure and amend §9 to declare retroactive-deferral obsolete. Rejected on attribution-correctness grounds: the amendment as proposed ('any closure CAN resolve any PI it can attribute work to') slides past the question of which sense of 'attribute' governs resolves. K — Resolve PI-030 plus PI-029 only (the most tightly coupled pair). Rejected on the same grounds: slice B invoked PI-029's access-layer surface but did not author it; the proper resolving conversation is the implementation conversation at SES-067 area which still has no CONV record.
consequences: PI-030 status flips Open → Resolved at apply time via slice A's atomic server-side behavior on the resolves_planning_items section. PI-027, PI-028, PI-029, PI-032 remain Open until PI-033 resolves them. Doug's UI shows 'Open' status on those four tiles for the interim, which is a small psychic cost the rule accepts in exchange for attribution integrity. PI-033's scope unchanged from its prior planning-item description; its backfill quietly inherits the resolves-edge work for these four.
context: PI-027 (methodology document drafting), PI-028 (commit schema spec), PI-029 (commits access layer + REST) are all still Open at this closure's open. The kickoff's default J proposed resolving all four (PI-027, PI-028, PI-029, PI-030) here on the rationale that methodology §9's retroactive-resolution discipline was a workaround for the mechanism's absence — now that the mechanism exists, deferring is principle without substance. Closer reading of §9 and §5.7 reveals the deferral is doing more work than the kickoff credited.
decision: Resolve PI-030 only in this closure. PI-027, PI-028, PI-029, PI-032 stay Open. PI-033's Phase 3 backfill resolves them when it authors retroactive CONV records for the historical conversations that did the work, emitting resolves edges from those proper CONVs to the planning items they completed. Methodology §9 stays intact; no amendment.
rationale: Methodology §5.7's 'the final conversation declares resolves_planning_items' implies the resolves edge sources at the conversation that DID the work. This closure (CONV-046) did not author the methodology document, the commit schema spec, or the commits access layer — those were prior conversations whose CONV records don't yet exist. Pointing a resolves edge from CONV-046 to PI-027 misattributes the methodology drafting work to THIS conversation, breaking the audit queries in §6.1 and §6.2 (both walk the commit_conversation_id FK from the resolves source). PI-033 is the structurally correct resolver because it has to author the historical CONV records anyway (per §7.1's Historical commits item); emitting their resolves edges falls out for free. The §9 deferral is an attribution-discipline rule, not a mechanism-availability stopgap. Diagnostic from §9's tail ('PI-022 is the only Resolved planning item, and it has no resolves edge') remains usable through PI-033.
title: Build closure resolves only the planning item that motivated its build; upstream Open PIs from before the resolves mechanism stay deferred to PI-033 per methodology §9

alternatives_considered: L — Surface as a new PI status Resolved-on-arrival with resolves edge from CONV-046. Rejected (false attribution; methodology creep precedent). M — Surface as a DEC only, no PI. Rejected (decisions describe choices, and this was an executor correction more naturally framed as a PI than a decision — but the PI framing fails on attribution, so neither M nor L survives). N (selected).
consequences: Future operators reading the access layer who wonder why conversations.py runs identifier-conflict-before-title-conflict use git blame to land on 2b5557d. Methodology silence on access-layer ordering remains. Precedent established: executor improvisations are governed by commit log plus test coverage, not by retroactive governance records, unless they touch methodology surface directly.
context: Slice B's Claude Code executor session added an unplanned three-line surgical fix to conversations.py — raising ConflictError on explicit-identifier collision BEFORE the duplicate-title check fires, so that re-applying a payload (which carries the same conversation_title for the same conversation_identifier) returns the correct 409 rather than a misleading 422. The fix was undocumented at the methodology layer when the kickoff was authored. The kickoff's default L proposed surfacing it as a new PI status Resolved-on-arrival, with resolves edge from CONV-046.
decision: No governance record. The slice B commit message (2b5557d) documents the reorder rationale verbatim. The test suite — TestPI030NewSections::test_409_skip_idempotent_on_re_run_all_sections and adjacent — exercises the behavior. Anyone investigating the ordering quirk runs git blame on the reordered lines, lands on 2b5557d, reads the explanation. Methodology stays clean of executor-improvisation-noise records.
rationale: Same attribution discipline as DEC-234: this closure did not perform the fix; the slice B executor session did. A PI authored here with resolves edge from CONV-046 would carry the same false attribution pattern Q3 just rejected. More broadly: PIs represent intent that drives work, not correction that follows from it; executor sessions make many small mid-stream corrections (typo fixes, import reorders, test assertion tweaks) and surfacing each as a PI is methodology creep that fills the planning-item table with after-the-fact records that didn't motivate any work. The argument for a higher-than-commit-log trace doesn't hold: the methodology has no position on access-layer check ordering, so there's no methodology-level question to anchor a PI to.
title: Executor-improvisation fixes (e.g., slice B's conversations.py identifier-vs-title check reorder) get no governance record; commit log plus test coverage are the trace

alternatives_considered: P — No convention; conversation_purpose stays fully free-form. Rejected — Q1's pattern needs to be self-identifying somewhere, and the purpose field is the natural home. Schema-level alternatives (typed conversation_kind enum) rejected as overkill for a one-bit distinction.
consequences: Every build closure's conversation_purpose follows the convention going forward. Grep on the conversations snapshot for 'Build closure for ' returns all build-closure CONV records. This CONV-046 is the first instance and the methodology §10 worked example.
context: DEC-232 established the build-closure conversation type. The conversation_purpose field on every CONV record is free-form one-sentence prose. The build-closure pattern needs a way to identify itself in the conversations corpus without requiring a separate conversation_kind taxonomy field (none exists in the schema).
decision: Build closure CONV records open conversation_purpose with the literal string 'Build closure for SES-NNN — ' where SES-NNN is the session identifier of the upstream planner. Methodology §10 names the convention; it's discoverable via grep on the conversations snapshot or via the V2 desktop UI's free-text search.
rationale: Convention is one line in methodology §10 and zero code. Grep-discoverable. Establishes the planner→closer chain in the natural field for one-sentence statements of conversational intent. Avoids the alternative of adding a typed enum (conversation_kind) to the schema, which would be a real Alembic migration plus a schema spec amendment for a one-bit distinction. Free-form prose with a leading convention is the right cost for the value.
title: Build closure conversation_purpose opens with 'Build closure for SES-NNN — ' naming the upstream planner; methodology §10 captures the convention

alternatives_considered: β — Defer closure until PI-033 creates WS-009 first. Rejected (blocks PI-030 on PI-033, same anti-pattern Q3-H rejected). γ — Extend close-out payload schema with a workstreams section. Rejected (slice-D-equivalent work; scope creep on a structurally complete workstream; the apply-prompt pre-step achieves the same with a curl call). δ — Attach CONV-046 to a closest-fit existing workstream (e.g., WS-001 'Governance entity schema-design'). Rejected (WS-001's scope was the six v0.7 schema specs — workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event — not commit; also status complete; misattribution).
consequences: WS-009 lands at apply time, status in_flight. CONV-046 belongs to WS-009. PI-033's Phase 3 backfill scope quietly inherits attaching historical conversations (the as-yet-unauthored CONV records for SES-057, SES-061, SES-063, SES-067, SES-068, SES-070) to WS-009 via conversation_belongs_to_workstream edges. Methodology §4.0 worked example now shows the two-edge pattern correctly. Methodology document Last Updated bumps and revision row added for the §4.0 fix and §5.5 clarification alongside the §10 new section.
context: Pre-work discovered that the conversations.py access layer (crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/repositories/conversations.py _validate_edges) requires every live conversation to carry exactly one outbound conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge — missing edge or multiple edges return HTTP 422. None of the eight existing workstreams (WS-001 through WS-008, all status complete) covers PI-030's lineage. SES-057's title explicitly says 'Code Change Lifecycle workstream established' but no WS-NNN record exists for it because workstream-as-governance-entity only arrived in v0.7 after SES-057. Slice A's methodology §4.0 worked example showed only the conversation_records_session edge, omitting the workstream-membership edge that the access layer requires — a latent gap that would surface on the first real apply if not corrected. The close-out payload schema has no workstreams section, so workstream creation cannot happen inside the payload apply itself.
decision: Two-part decision. (a) The apply prompt for this closure POSTs /workstreams as a pre-step to create WS-009 'Code Change Lifecycle workstream' status in_flight before invoking apply_close_out.py. WS-009's purpose: 'Bring code-change lifecycle into V2 governance: methodology document, commit entity, payload-section extensions, UI surface, methodology rollout, and historical backfill.' Status transitions to complete after PI-031, PI-032, PI-033 resolve. CONV-046's conversation block carries both the conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge to WS-009 and the conversation_records_session edge to SES-074. title: Code Change Lifecycle workstream promoted to a governance object (WS-009) via apply-prompt pre-step; methodology §4.0 worked-example fixed to include the workstream-membership edge slice A omitted
